cyclades.metaDesc: Routes from Athens, Lavrio, Mykonos, Paros and Santorini across the Cyclades — honest about the meltemi, precise about the miles.
cyclades.title: White villages, blue water, real wind
cyclades.description: The Cyclades are the postcard — Mykonos windmills, Santorini's caldera, chapels on bare hills — but they're also the Aegean's most honest sailing. In July and August the meltemi blows a hard 20–30 knots from the north for days at a time, which turns passages into proper sailing and rewards crews who plan around it. Go south with it, not against it; leave early; treat rest days as part of the route. June and September soften everything. Paros and Naxos make the best mid-week bases, with real towns behind the quays, and Lavrio spares you an hour of Athens traffic on day one. This is the area for crews who want the sailing itself to be the story.

dodecanese.metaDesc: Routes from Kos and Rhodes: Symi's amphitheatre harbour, Nisyros' volcano and quiet Kalymnos — with real notes on wind and moorings.
dodecanese.title: Frontier islands with two flags in view
dodecanese.description: Sail the Dodecanese and you thread a chain of islands that kept crusader castles, Italian art-deco harbours and Greek fishing villages all within sight of the Turkish coast. Symi is the show-stopper — a neoclassical amphitheatre around a harbour you'll fight for at six pm and forgive by seven. Nisyros lets you walk inside a live volcano; Kalymnos still dives for sponges; Rhodes old town is a medieval city with a marina attached. The meltemi funnels between the islands, so afternoon legs can be lively — plan them early, and keep an anchorage in reserve. Charters from Kos or Rhodes both work; Kos keeps the legs shorter, Rhodes adds the grand finale.

ionian.metaDesc: Routes from Lefkas, Corfu and Preveza: Meganisi, Ithaca, Kefalonia, Paxos — flat water and gentle afternoon wind, planned day by day.
ionian.title: Greece with the difficulty turned down
ionian.description: The Ionian runs on a schedule you can set your watch by: glassy mornings, a friendly northwest breeze from lunch until sunset, then calm again for dinner on the quay. No meltemi, short legs, deep sheltered bays — this is where Greek flotillas learn to walk, and where experienced crews come to actually relax. From Lefkas you can reach Meganisi's fjords in an afternoon; Ithaca and Kefalonia fill the middle of the week with green mountains and one-taverna harbours; Fiskardo and Kioni are as pretty as Greek villages get. Further north, Corfu adds Venetian streets and Paxos its famous west-coast cliffs. Bring a long line for tying stern-to a taverna quay — you'll use it every night.

sporades.metaDesc: Routes from Volos and Skiathos through Skopelos and Alonissos — pine forests, film-set bays and Europe's oldest marine park.
sporades.title: Green islands the charts undersell
sporades.description: The Sporades never make the posters, which suits everyone who sails there. These are forested islands — pine down to the tide line — scattered across a sea that hosts Europe's oldest marine park, where monk seals still haul out and dolphins work the channels. Skiathos brings the airport and the nightlife; Skopelos brings the Mamma Mia chapel and a harbour town that smells of plums and resin; Alonissos brings the silence. Legs are short, anchorages are sandy, and the meltemi arrives here softer than in the Cyclades. Starting from Volos adds a mainland day across the Pagasitic gulf, calm as a lake. A week here feels longer than it is, in the best way.

athens-key-syros-hydra.metaDesc: Sail seven days from Athens through the Cyclades and Saronic: Cape Sounion, Kea, Syros, Serifos, Hydra and Poros, the route map's most varied winds.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 15-mile warm-up east along the Attica coast to anchor beneath the Temple of Poseidon, floodlit after dark.
1.description: The first leg is deliberately easy, fifteen miles of open water to shake out the boat and let the crew find their sea legs. We usually motor-sail it if the northerly is light. Sounion has no marina, so this is an anchor-only night: drop on sand at five to eight metres, roughly half a mile off the headland, tucked in from the north. Below eighteen knots it holds fine; above that, run the extra miles into Lavrion instead. The reward is the temple itself. Its columns catch the last of the sun, then the floodlights come up and the whole ruin glows above the water. Row ashore for grilled fish before the light goes. Snorkelling along the rock shelves east of the point fills any spare hour.
thingsToDo: Sunset beneath the Temple of Poseidon columns
Snorkel the rock shelves east of the point
Grilled sea bass at a Sounion taverna
Photograph the floodlit temple after dark
1.mooringTip: Anchor free on sand at 5-8 m about half a mile off the temple; good holding from the north under 18 knots. If it pipes up, retreat to Lavrion Olympic Marina at 60-90 euro a night.

2.shortDescription: Twenty downwind miles across to Kea, then a walk up to the archaic Stone Lion carved above Ioulida.
2.description: With the Meltemi behind you, the twenty-mile crossing to Kea is the kind of run that makes people fall for Greek sailing: sails goosewinged, the log ticking over, Attica sinking astern. Vourkari, on the northwest corner, is the sensible overnight. Stern-to with lazy lines runs twenty-five to thirty-five euros, and the bay keeps the swell out better than the town quay at Korissia. Aim to be tied up by four; the afternoon breeze funnels through and berthing gets fiddly after that. Ashore, the payoff is the Stone Lion, an archaic beast cut from the rock a half-hour above Ioulida. Nobody quite agrees what it was for. The climb through the whitewashed alleys is worth the sweat, best done as the heat drops toward evening.
thingsToDo: Hike to the archaic Stone Lion of Kea
Snorkel the Patris steamship wreck off Koundouros
Octopus carpaccio at a Vourkari ouzeri
Wander Ioulida's marble alleys at dusk
2.mooringTip: Berth stern-to in Vourkari on lazy lines for 25-35 euro; get in before 16:00 when the breeze funnels. Korissia's town quay is free but exposed once the northerly climbs past 18 knots.

3.shortDescription: A quick beam reach to Syros, where Ermoupoli's forgiving quay sits below a neoclassical capital in pastel tiers.
3.description: Thirty-two miles on a beam reach, and with the Meltemi in its usual mood the boat eats them quickly. Syros is the administrative heart of the Cyclades, and it shows the moment Ermoupoli comes into view: marble squares, a domed town hall, houses stacked up two hills in faded ochre and rose. The town quay is about as easy as stern-to gets in these islands, decent holding and shelter whatever the wind does. If you would rather swim than stroll, Finikas on the southwest coast drops you on sand under a fringe of tamarisk. My vote is the town. Climb the lanes to Ano Syros, the old Catholic quarter, for the view back down over the roofs, and stop for a box of loukoumi on the way.
thingsToDo: Climb to Ano Syros' Catholic quarter
Taste loukoumi at a 19th-century confectionery
Cross Miaouli's marble square after dark
Swim on sand at Finikas Bay
3.mooringTip: Ermoupoli's town quay is free on your own anchor and shelters from every direction, one of the calmest berths in the Cyclades. For a swim night instead, anchor at Finikas on sand in 4-7 m, protected from the north.

4.shortDescription: A long downwind leg to Serifos, where Livadi funnels the Meltemi and the Chora crowns a steep donkey path.
4.description: Thirty-two miles southwest, dead downwind for most of it, which is glorious sailing until you have to anchor at the far end. Livadi is a natural funnel; the Meltemi accelerates straight into the bay and this is where charter crews most often drag. Treat the anchor seriously. Lay plenty of scope on the sand, dive on it to check it has bitten, and sit in the cockpit for the first hour before you trust it. Above twenty-two knots I would take the quay instead, twenty to thirty euros a night. Then look up. The Chora climbs the ridge in a white cascade, a half-hour walk on the old donkey path, and the windmills at the top are the sunset spot. A bowl of revithada in a village kafeneio, and the day is complete.
thingsToDo: Climb the donkey path to Serifos Chora
Revithada chickpea stew in a kafeneio
Swim at Psili Ammos on the south coast
Sunset at the windmills above Chora
4.mooringTip: Anchor in Livadi on sand in 5-8 m with generous scope and dive to confirm it is set; drag is common here above 22 knots. The town quay runs 20-30 euro if you would rather not watch the swing.

5.shortDescription: Thirty-eight miles west out of the Cyclades into the calmer Saronic, to car-free Hydra where donkeys still haul the luggage.
5.description: This is the transition day. Thirty-eight miles on a long beam reach carries you out of the Cyclades and into the Saronic, and somewhere west of Kythnos the wind eases by five to eight knots and the sea lies down. Hydra's harbour is tiny and everyone wants the same slots, so push to arrive by three. If the wall is full, drop the hook in Mandraki Bay a mile east and run the tender in; the holding on sand at six to eight metres is good and sheltered from the north. The town is the real event. No cars have ever been allowed, and mules still carry cases up the cliff lanes to the stone houses. Swim off the flat rocks at Spilia, walk fifteen minutes west to Vlychos, or climb to the Profitis Ilias monastery for the long view.
thingsToDo: Wander the car-free cliff lanes
Swim off the rocks at Spilia
Walk 15 minutes west to Vlychos beach
Hike up to Profitis Ilias monastery
5.mooringTip: Hydra's quay charges 30-50 euro and its slots fill by 15:00, so plan your leg to arrive early. If you miss out, anchor in Mandraki Bay a mile east on sand in 6-8 m, sheltered from the north, and tender ashore.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen easy miles to Poros through a pine-lined channel, with a swim in Russian Bay before the town quay.
6.description: Short hop today, only fifteen miles, so there is no rush to leave. The approach to Poros is the treat: the channel narrows between the island and the Peloponnese, pine slopes rising on both sides, the water flat and green. Break the passage at Russian Bay on the north coast, an old naval anchorage where you can drop on sand in five to seven metres and swim off the boat through the middle of the day. When you have had enough, motor round to the town quay for the night; it shelters from any direction and runs twenty-five to forty euros. Poros is where most crews take their lay-day, and the front fills with tavernas. Order the saganaki flamed at the table, and if the legs are willing, walk up to the old Sanctuary of Poseidon.
thingsToDo: Swim at anchor in Russian Bay
Dip at pine-fringed Love Bay
Walk to the Sanctuary of Poseidon ruins
Flamed saganaki on the waterfront
6.mooringTip: Poros town quay is well sheltered from every direction and costs 25-40 euro stern-to. Use Russian Bay on the north coast, sand in 5-7 m, as a lunchtime swim anchorage before you commit to the town for the night.

7.shortDescription: Thirty miles north back to Alimos, with a last swim off Aegina and a fuel stop before the handover berth.
7.description: Thirty miles due north closes the loop. Slip the lines mid-morning and you will be back at Alimos comfortably by two, which leaves room for one more swim if the wind cooperates. Aegina sits about ten miles into the leg and makes an easy lunch stop, water and light off the beaches on its west side. After that it is a straight run up to the marina. One habit worth keeping: top up the fuel at the entrance berth before you tie up, so the boat is ready for handover first thing rather than in a scramble. Alimos is stern-to on lazy lines at seventy to a hundred euros. Once she is secure, there is time to clean up and take the crew ashore for a farewell dinner at a Glyfada taverna.
thingsToDo: Last swim off the beaches of Aegina
Refuel and rinse down at Alimos
Farewell dinner at a Glyfada taverna
Stroll the Faliro seafront promenade
7.mooringTip: Alimos Marina is stern-to on lazy lines at 70-100 euro a night. Take on fuel at the entrance berth before mooring, and confirm your handover slot with the base 24 hours ahead.

athens-mykonos-paros.metaTitle: 7-Day Mykonos & Paros Sail from Athens | Boat4You
athens-mykonos-paros.metaDesc: Sail seven days round-trip from Athens through the Cyclades: Kea, Syros, Mykonos, Paros, Serifos and Kythnos, with anchorages, tavernas and mooring tips.

1.shortDescription: A 28-mile shakedown south from Alimos past Cape Sounion's temple to Kea's sheltered Vourkari bay, your first Aegean night at anchor.
1.description: Handover at Alimos eats up the morning, so we usually slip the lines by eleven and point south. The first hours run down the Attic coast with Cape Sounion ahead, the columns of Poseidon's temple standing pale on the headland. Once past the cape the swell opens up and Kea fills the horizon. Vourkari sits on the northwest coast, a working bay where fishing boats and charter yachts share the water. Ashore, the waterfront tavernas grill the morning's catch, and octopus hangs drying on the lines. If the crew still has legs, the road climbs to Ioulida, the capital tucked into the hills, and a short walk beyond it reaches the archaic Lion of Kea, carved straight from the bedrock. A gentle first day. The real distances start tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Sail past Cape Sounion's Temple of Poseidon
Climb to Ioulida, the hilltop capital
Find the rock-cut Lion of Kea
Grilled octopus on the Vourkari waterfront
1.mooringTip: Vourkari is stern-to on the quay or anchor on sand in 5-7 m off the bay; holding is good, though gusts spill down off the hills when the meltemi is up.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles southeast to Ermoupoli, the grand neoclassical capital of the Cyclades, where marble streets and the Catholic hill town wait ashore.
2.description: Syros surprises most first-timers. It is the administrative heart of the Cyclades, and Ermoupoli looks the part, with mansions, a marble main square and an opera house modelled on La Scala. We tie up on the town quay, right in the middle of things. The old shipyards still work along the harbour, so expect some clatter in the morning. Above the port two hills carry the churches: Orthodox Anastasi on one, Catholic San Giorgio on the other, crowning the medieval maze of Ano Syros. Walk up in the late afternoon when the light goes gold on the pastel facades. Rebetiko was born in these lanes, and a few tavernas still play it live. Before you leave, buy a box of Syros loukoumi from a sweet shop near the waterfront.
thingsToDo: Stroll Ermoupoli's marble Miaouli Square
Climb Ano Syros to San Giorgio cathedral
Catch live rebetiko in a backstreet taverna
Buy Syros loukoumi near the waterfront
2.mooringTip: At Ermoupoli you back stern-to onto the town quay under your own hook, and in summer berths are first-come. Meltemi swell finds its way into the basin, so veer plenty of chain and, wherever a bollard allows, run a long line ashore.

3.shortDescription: A short 14-mile hop east to Mykonos, with a UNESCO stop at Delos, whitewashed Chora, and the windmills above the old port.
3.description: The leg to Mykonos is short, which is just as well, because the meltemi funnels hard between the islands and the last few miles into the harbour can be lumpy. This is the island of the winds, and it earns the name. Berth at Tourlos, the new port north of town, then walk or bus into Chora. The place is busy and expensive and knows it, but the old core still works its magic: cobalt shutters, the row of windmills at Kato Mili, and the waterfront at Little Venice where the sea slaps the house walls. Time your evening drink there for sunset. If you would rather trade the crowds for antiquity, the small island of Delos sits a short sail west, its ancient city and terrace of lions among the finest ruins in Greece.
thingsToDo: Day-sail to the ruins of Delos
Photograph the Kato Mili windmill row
Sunset drink in Little Venice
Wander Chora's cobalt-shuttered lanes
3.mooringTip: Book a berth at Tourlos (new port) well ahead in high season; the old harbour quay is short-stay only and the entrance gets a nasty chop when the meltemi blows straight in.

4.shortDescription: Downwind 18 miles south to Paros, splitting the day between Parikia's Byzantine church and the pastel fishing harbour of Naoussa.
4.description: With the meltemi behind you, the run down to Paros is one of the easier sails of the week, often a fast reach under a poled-out headsail. Parikia is the capital and the ferry hub, home to the Ekatontapiliani, the church of a hundred doors, one of the oldest still standing in Greece. It is worth an hour inside before the heat builds. Most crews prefer to overnight up at Naoussa, the old fishing port on the north coast, where a ruined Venetian fort guards the entrance and the tavernas spread onto the quay after dark. A short dinghy ride or taxi gets you to Kolymbithres, where wind and sea have sculpted the granite into smooth grey shapes you can swim between. Rent a scooter if you have a spare afternoon and climb to Lefkes.
thingsToDo: Step inside the Ekatontapiliani church
Swim the granite pools at Kolymbithres
Dine on the Naoussa harbour quay
Drive up to the marble village of Lefkes
4.mooringTip: Parikia's town quay is chaotic and exposed in summer; Naoussa is the calmer overnight, stern-to inside the breakwater, though space is tight, so aim to be in by mid-afternoon.

5.shortDescription: A longer 30-mile crossing west to wild, arid Serifos, where a cliff-top Chora looms 200 metres above the harbour at Livadi.
5.description: This is the week's long leg, thirty miles of open water heading back west across the Cyclades, so we like to be off the quay by nine. With the meltemi on the beam it can be a spirited sail, and the swell has room to build between the islands. Serifos rewards the effort. The island is bare and rocky, a proper contrast to polished Paros, and its Chora climbs the hillside above Livadi in a stack of white cubes crowned by an old kastro. The walk up the stepped path takes forty minutes and the view at the top runs to Sifnos and beyond. Down at sea level, Livadi curves around a sheltered bay, with a couple of good swimming beaches, Psili Ammos among them, a short hop away. Quiet nights, honest food, few crowds.
thingsToDo: Hike the stepped path up to Chora
Explore the old kastro ruins
Swim at Psili Ammos beach
Sunset from the Chora windmills
5.mooringTip: Livadi is stern-to on the harbour quay or anchor on sand in 5-7 m in the bay; it is well sheltered from the northerlies, which makes it a reliable overnight even when the meltemi is honking.

6.shortDescription: Eighteen miles up to Kythnos, an island that deserves more attention, with the twin-sided sandbar at Kolona and hot springs bubbling up at Loutra.
6.description: Kythnos gets skipped by boats racing back to base, which is exactly why we like it. The sail up from Serifos is straightforward, mostly a beam reach in settling wind. The island's showpiece is Kolona, a slender spit of sand with sea on both sides that links the shore to the islet of Agios Loukas; you can anchor off it and swim across the sandbar. Merichas is the working port, small and friendly, with tavernas serving grilled sardines a few steps from the water. Further up the east coast, Loutra has thermal springs that run warm into the sea, the only naturally heated water in the Cyclades. Inland, the village of Dryopida hides the Katafyki cave, an old iron mine you can walk into. A calm, unshowy day before the run home.
thingsToDo: Anchor off the Kolona sandbar and swim
Soak in the Loutra thermal springs
Grilled sardines on the Merichas quay
Walk into the Katafyki cave at Dryopida
6.mooringTip: Merichas is stern-to on the quay, but for a quieter night anchor off Kolona or in Apokrousi bay on good sand in 5-7 m; both open to the west, so watch the afternoon sea breeze.

7.shortDescription: A final 22-mile run north into the Saronic Gulf and back to Alimos, with a last chance to round Cape Sounion.
7.description: The last morning is unhurried. Breakfast slowly, take a final swim off the boat, then point the bow northwest for Attica. The crossing is around twenty-two miles and the meltemi, if it blows, will usually be behind you for a comfortable downwind finish. Many crews route past Cape Sounion one more time to see Poseidon's temple from seaward, a fitting bookend to the week. From there it is a straight run up the coast into the Saronic Gulf. Aim to reach Alimos with time in hand, because fuelling and pump-out slots book up fast on a Friday afternoon, and a tidy handover the evening before saves a scramble. Salt on your skin, a week of anchorages behind you, and Athens waiting to send you home.
thingsToDo: Round Cape Sounion for a last temple view
Final swim in a Cyclades channel
Photograph the Temple of Poseidon
Prep the boat for handover
7.mooringTip: Alimos is the standard handover marina and the busiest charter base in Greece; confirm your fuel and pump-out slot 24 hours ahead, and dock the night before if your contract allows.

athens-mykonos-santorini-paros.metaDesc: Sail a 14-day Cyclades loop from Athens through Mykonos, Paros and Santorini, back via Milos and Sifnos. Anchorages, mooring and wind, day by day.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 20-mile shake-down out of Alimos, rounding Cape Sounion before the easy hop across to Kea's sheltered Vourkari bay.
1.description: Provisioning always runs long on the first morning, so we rarely clear Alimos before eleven. Once the trolleys are unloaded and the fridge is stocked, point the bow east-southeast and settle in. Within the hour the columns of Poseidon's temple stand up on the headland at Sounion, off to starboard, and they make a tidy mark for the channel out into open water. The rest is a twenty-mile reach across to Kea, riding whatever northerly the day has built. Vourkari sits on the island's northwest shoulder and does the job of a first night well: good cover from the prevailing Meltemi, a generous stern-to quay, and a run of little seafood ouzeris where the grilled octopus comes straight off the coals. Korissia, the ferry town next door, is the cheaper fallback when the wind stays soft.
thingsToDo: Climb to Ioulida's marble backstreets at dusk
Find the 6th-century BC Lion of Kea
Snorkel the Patris wreck off Koundouros
Grilled octopus at a Vourkari ouzeri
1.mooringTip: Stern-to in Vourkari on lazy lines, roughly €25-35 a night for a 45-footer, with solid cover from the northerly. When the quay fills, drop the hook two miles east in Otzias, sand at 6-8 m.

2.shortDescription: Thirty-two miles southeast to Syros: light going early, a brisk Meltemi beam reach later, then the easiest quay in the Cyclades.
2.description: Leave Vourkari not long after coffee. The morning air is usually still under twelve knots, and the Meltemi tends to hold off until the early afternoon, which gives you a soft start on the thirty-two-mile run down to Syros. When the wind does fill, the back half of the leg turns into a fast beam reach with a building sea, so tuck a reef in before you need it rather than after. Ermoupoli waits at the head of a deep, well-shaped harbour, its neoclassical old town stacked up the hillside in ochre and white. The town quay here is about as forgiving as stern-to mooring gets in these islands: sheltered whatever the wind is doing, plenty of room, and the capital's cafés a two-minute walk off the passerelle.
thingsToDo: Wander Ano Syros and its Catholic quarter
Taste loukoumi at a 19th-century sweet shop
Evening stroll across Miaouli marble square
Swim the rock ledges at Asteria
2.mooringTip: Stern-to on Ermoupoli's town quay is free, but bring your own anchor as lazy lines are hit-and-miss. Shelter is excellent from any direction. For a serviced berth, Manna marina charges around €60-80 a night.

3.shortDescription: A quick fourteen-mile beam reach to Tinos through a channel that reliably adds five knots to whatever the forecast promised.
3.description: The gap between Syros and Tinos is one of the windiest stretches in the group. The geography funnels the Meltemi, so a benign twenty-knot forecast can show twenty-five on the water by mid-afternoon, and late-July gusts run higher still. Take it on the beam and enjoy the pace, but have the boat tied up well before four, before the swell starts stacking at the harbour mouth. Tinos town has stern-to berths in the newer basin, though the holding is only fair; the smaller inner harbour, kept for charter and local boats, is the calmer bet when it blows hard. Ashore, the island is the marble-carving heart of the Aegean and the great Orthodox pilgrimage site, the church of Panagia Evangelistria drawing the faithful up its long marble-paved approach.
thingsToDo: Watch marble carvers at work in Pyrgos
Walk the dovecote trail above Falatados
Artichoke à la polita in a courtyard taverna
Sunset among the boulders at Volax
3.mooringTip: Stern-to in the new harbour runs about €25-40; the bottom is moderate sand, so lay a long scope and set hard. If more than 25 knots of Meltemi is forecast, take the calmer inner small-craft harbour instead.

4.shortDescription: Just eight miles east to Mykonos, but the open channel bites — book Tourlos ahead and swim at Ornos on the way.
4.description: A short leg, this one, barely eight miles across to Mykonos. Do not let the distance fool you: the straight-line course cuts through open channel where the wind sits a good five knots above the regional number, and the afternoon sea gets short and awkward. In July and August you really want a slot booked at Tourlos, the new marina north of town, twenty-four hours ahead online. The old town quay is free but wide open to swell and only worth it when the Meltemi is under eighteen knots. There is no rush to arrive, so break the passage with a swim at Ornos or Psarou before you moor. Mykonos earns its reputation after dark, but the quieter daytime pleasure is the boat across to Delos and its ruined marble avenues.
thingsToDo: Boat across to the ruins of Delos
Beach lunch at road-less Agios Sostis
Little Venice waterfront at sunset
Hike the Armenistis lighthouse loop
4.mooringTip: Book a stern-to slot at Tourlos New Marina online for peak season; expect €70-110 a night. The free town quay is only safe below about eighteen knots of northerly, as swell builds quickly across the entrance.

5.shortDescription: Twenty-two downwind miles southwest to Naoussa, quarter-reaching the whole way — arguably the finest sailing day of the trip.
5.description: This is the leg crews remember. Twenty-two miles southwest to Paros, the Meltemi settled on the quarter, and the boat surfing along on a broad reach for four or five hours of proper open-water sailing. Keep an eye out as you pass the Naxos channel, where gusts come down off the high ground harder than you expect. Naoussa waits on the north coast, an old Cycladic fishing port that has grown into a smart little harbour village without losing the boats that gave it its name; the octopus still hangs drying on lines by the water. If the northerly is pushing into the inner basin, there is easy anchoring in the bay just outside. Save the evening for a glass of ouzo at the fishermen's quay as the light goes.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the sculpted rocks at Kolymbithres
Wine tasting at a Marpissa vineyard
Ouzo on the Naoussa fishing quay
Walk Lefkes' marble lanes at dusk
5.mooringTip: Naoussa's harbour is small and stern-to, €30-50 a night, and often full by five in the afternoon, so arrive early. If it's taken, anchor half a mile east in Plastira Bay, sand at 5-7 m and sheltered from the north.

6.shortDescription: The windiest day of the loop: twenty-eight hard miles south to Ios on full Meltemi, so reef early and be inside Ormos by four.
6.description: The Naxos-Sikinos channel is where the Meltemi shows its teeth. Unlike the downwind runs behind you, this twenty-eight-mile leg puts the wind forward of the beam, and through the channel itself you can expect a steady twenty-two to thirty knots on a hard port-tack reach. Reef down before you leave the lee of Paros and aim to have the anchor down inside Ormos well before four. The harbour there is sheltered and the marina is being enlarged; the old quay still works, though slots are few. A mile south, Mylopotas is the swimming bay of choice for the afternoon, a long sweep of sand that empties out once the day boats leave. Ios has a reputation as a party island, but the walk up to the windmills above Chora at sunset is a calmer pleasure.
thingsToDo: Hike to the reputed tomb of Homer
Swim the sands at Manganari Bay
Sunset drinks by the Chora windmills
Late rembetika in a Chora square
6.mooringTip: Stern-to in Ormos harbour, €25-40 a night, with pre-bookable slots in the new marina when open. Mylopotas is a daytime anchorage only, sand at 5-8 m; it becomes untenable overnight once the Meltemi sets in.

7.shortDescription: Eighteen miles south to Santorini — leave at first light for Vlychada, because the caldera itself is far too deep to anchor.
7.description: The mileage is nothing, but this leg wants a calm window and good daylight, because arriving at Santorini is no place to be rushed. The honest advice is to forget anchoring in the caldera. The water inside drops past two hundred metres a short way off the cliffs, so swinging on your own hook is simply not on; the only holding is off Thirasia at the cruise-ship buoys, and that is unreliable at best. Instead, aim for Vlychada on the south coast, a proper sheltered marina with road access for a crew change. Leave Ios at first light, be tied up by lunchtime, and put the afternoon into a taxi up to Oia. The sunset there is a cliché for a reason, but go early and claim a spot before the coach crowds arrive.
thingsToDo: Taxi up to Oia for the sunset
Wine tasting at Santo Wines or Sigalas
Swim below the cliffs at Red Beach
Fava with capers in Megalochori
7.mooringTip: Vlychada Marina is stern-to at roughly €60-100 a night and needs booking ahead in peak season. Treat the Thirasia cruise buoys as a last resort only; the caldera's depth rules out free anchoring almost everywhere.

8.shortDescription: The return begins: twenty-two miles northwest into the Meltemi to little Folegandros, its cliff-top Chora high above the port.
8.description: From here the trip turns for home, and home means working back up against the Meltemi in shorter, more manageable hops. Folegandros lies twenty-two miles northwest and is the smallest of the big-name Cyclades stops, all the better for it. Karavostasi is the only real port, a modest place open to the south but well tucked in when the wind holds its usual northerly slot. You'll either take a stern-to berth or anchor off, depending on what's free when you arrive. The Chora sits high on a clifftop above the harbour, reached by taxi or a stiff half-hour climb, and it is worth every step of it: a lantern-lit maze of squares that feels a world away from Santorini's crowds. Dinner up there, with the drop to the sea below, is the evening's reward.
thingsToDo: Climb to the cliff-edge Panagia church
Swim the boat-only cove at Katergo
Matsata pasta with goat ragù
Walk the kalderimi to Ano Meria
8.mooringTip: Karavostasi is stern-to at €25-40 with only a handful of slots; when full, anchor just east of the port in sand at 4-7 m. Stay clear if any southerly is forecast, as the harbour is fully open that way.

9.shortDescription: Twenty upwind miles northwest to Milos, where Adamas at the head of its long bay is the calmest overnight in the west.
9.description: Another twenty miles of beating northwest, the Meltemi square on the bow, so plan on a couple of long tacks and a slower average than the downwind days gave you. The payoff is Adamas, tucked right at the head of Milos's deep natural inlet. Once you're inside the bay the wind falls away and the shelter is about as complete as you'll find anywhere in the western Cyclades. Break the passage with a stop at Sarakiniko if the day is quiet; the white volcanic rock there looks like something off another planet, though the anchorage is exposed and only sensible in light air. Milos rewards a slower look than one night allows, but Adamas puts you within easy reach of Plaka's castle hill and the painted boathouses at Klima for a first taste.
thingsToDo: Swim beneath the lunar cliffs of Sarakiniko
Snorkel the Papafragas sea caves
Up to Plaka Castle for the view
Photo walk the Klima boathouses
9.mooringTip: Stern-to on Adamas town quay, €30-50 a night, with reliable shelter from every direction. Pollonia in the northeast is cheaper but lies open to the northerly Meltemi, so save it for settled spells.

10.shortDescription: Eighteen miles north to Sifnos, the Cyclades' kitchen — anchor in Kamares, or Platys Gialos if the wind swings west.
10.description: An easy eighteen miles north brings you to Sifnos, and Kamares on the west coast is the natural place to stop. The bay shelters you nicely from the north and northeast, but it does open to the west, so in late-summer spells when the Meltemi carries a westerly slant a swell can work its way in and make the night rolly. That's your cue to shift round to Platys Gialos on the south coast. Sifnos is, by common agreement, the eating island of the Cyclades: chickpea revithada baked slow overnight in a wood oven, lamb mastelo sealed in clay pots, a whole cooking tradition traced back to a local nineteenth-century chef. Come hungry. The clifftop monastery at Chrysopigi, out past Faros, is the walk to earn your dinner.
thingsToDo: Walk to Chrysopigi monastery past Faros
Lamb mastelo in clay pots at Artemonas
Visit a working pottery in Vathy
Swim the calm water at Vathi Bay
10.mooringTip: Anchor on sand at 5-8 m in Kamares, or take one of the few stern-to slots on the small quay for €20-30. When the wind clocks into the west, move round to Platys Gialos on the south side.

11.shortDescription: A fourteen-mile hop north to Serifos, where Livadi Bay funnels the wind — set a long scope and dive the anchor.
11.description: A gentle fourteen miles separates Sifnos from Serifos, but the anchorage asks for more care than the passage. Livadi Bay faces straight into the Meltemi and works like a funnel, and while the sand holds well, the wind can stretch out even a well-set anchor. Dragging is the classic Serifos mishap. Lay plenty of scope, dive the anchor to see it dug in properly, and don't wander off ashore in the first hour until you're sure it's holding. Above the port, the Chora ranks among the prettiest hill towns in the islands, a white cascade of houses climbing to a ruined castle. Get up there on the old donkey path or by road taxi and time it for sunset, when the windmills on the ridge catch the last of the light.
thingsToDo: Walk the donkey path up to Chora
Chickpea revithada in a village kafeneio
Swim at Psili Ammos beach
Sunset by the ridge-top windmills
11.mooringTip: Two choices at Livadi: let the hook go on sand in 5-8 m with plenty of scope, or take the town quay stern-to for €20-30. Once it's blowing past 22 knots, dragging becomes a genuine worry, so free-dive the anchor before you leave the boat.

12.shortDescription: Fifteen quiet miles north to Kythnos and the Kolona sandbar — a double-sided beach reachable only by boat, and the trip's best swim.
12.description: Kythnos flies under the radar next to its famous neighbours, which is exactly its charm. Fifteen miles north of Serifos, it has none of the crowds and one genuinely special spot: Kolona, a couple of miles up the coast from the little ferry port at Merichas. A narrow ribbon of sand, maybe two hundred metres of it, links the islet of Agios Loukas to the main island, so you get a beach with sea on both sides and shallow, clear swimming whichever way you look. This is a place to drop the hook and stay put. Anchor off, swim until the light softens, and let dinner happen aboard as the day boats thin out. If the legs want stretching, the ridge road up to Dryopida makes a good cycle, and Loutra has thermal springs to soak tired shoulders.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at the Kolona sandbar
Soak in the Loutra hot springs
Cycle the ridge road to Dryopida
Sfougato cheese pie in a Dryopida taverna
12.mooringTip: Anchoring at Kolona is free, on sand at 4-6 m, and the islet screens you from the northerly Meltemi. Merichas town quay is the paid stern-to fallback at €20-30, and Loutra has both thermal springs and a few marina berths.

13.shortDescription: The long leg home: thirty-five miles west-northwest to Alimos, downwind on a good day, motor-sailing on a light one.
13.description: The last real sailing day is the biggest single hop of the fortnight, thirty-five miles west-northwest back across the open Saronic. Get away early. With the Meltemi behind the beam it can be a glorious downwind run, but the wind is fickler out here than in the islands, and on a soft day you'll be motor-sailing to make a sensible arrival by late afternoon. Cape Sounion lifts over the bow about halfway across, the temple columns marking the turn in toward Athens, and after a fortnight in the Cyclades it feels like the gate closing behind you. Duck in at the fuel berth to top up the tanks before you take your berth at Alimos, so the boat is fuelled and ready for the base come morning. Save the last night for a long crew dinner in Glyfada.
thingsToDo: A last swim below the Sounion temple
Refuel and rinse down at Alimos
Crew dinner in a Glyfada taverna
Souvenir stop on the Faliro promenade
13.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth on lazy lines at Alimos Marina, €70-100 a night, and refuel at the entrance fuel berth before you moor. Confirm your handover slot with the base 24 hours ahead.

14.shortDescription: Handover before nine at Alimos: a walk-round with the base, deposit back within a week, then the short run to the airport.
14.description: The last morning is a gentle one. Have the boat cleared and ready for handover before nine, when someone from the base will walk round it with you, checking her over while you're there to answer for anything. Assuming nothing is flagged, the security deposit comes back to you within a week or so. The airport is only twenty-five minutes up the road from Alimos, though on a Friday it pays to allow a full hour for the traffic. There's no need to rush the goodbyes. Grab a last frappé and a plate of something on the Glyfada seafront while the bags are loaded, and settle up with the dock hands if they looked after you well. Two weeks and a good stretch of the Cyclades behind you, it's a fine place to finish.
thingsToDo: Final walk-round with the base manager
Frappé on the Glyfada promenade
Transfer to the airport, 25 minutes
Tip the dock crew for good service
14.mooringTip: Hand the boat over at Alimos before nine. The deposit is normally released within seven days of the inspection; photograph and agree any damage notes before you sign anything off.

athens-saronic-gulf-14-day.metaDesc: Sail 14 days from Athens through the Saronic Gulf to medieval Monemvasia and back via Hydra, with quiet Peloponnese anchorages and famous fortress towns.

1.shortDescription: A 28-mile opening leg runs southwest to Poros, finishing stern-to on the town quay beneath the clock tower.
1.description: The first leg is longer than most, so we like to slip out of Alimos by mid-morning while the water is flat. The run down to Poros stays inside sheltered Saronic water the whole way, which makes it a forgiving day to find your rhythm with a new boat and crew. Poros sits in a narrow channel across from the lemon groves of Galatas, and the town quay runs the length of the waterfront under the clock tower. Before tying up, drop the hook for an hour at Love Bay on the south side, a pine-ringed cove that shelves gently into sand. Ashore, the ruins of the Sanctuary of Poseidon reward a short climb, and the waterfront kitchens flame saganaki at the table. Water and fuel are easy here.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in Love Bay
Climb to the Sanctuary of Poseidon ruins
Flamed saganaki on the Poros waterfront
Cycle the Lemonodasos lemon groves at Galatas
1.mooringTip: Stern-to along the Poros town quay runs €25–40 a night and stays sheltered from every direction. Arrive by late afternoon in peak season, as the central berths fill first.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles southwest to car-free Spetses, mooring in Dapia below the old cannons after a swim stop at sandy Zogeria.
2.description: Spetses keeps cars out of the old town, so the loudest thing you will hear on the waterfront is hooves. Horse carriages and bicycles do the work here, and the harbour of Dapia still has its row of old cannons pointing out to sea. We usually break the passage with a swim at Zogeria on the northwest coast, where the bottom is clean sand in five to seven metres and the pines come right down to the shore. Ashore, walk east to the Old Harbour, past the shipyards where wooden hulls are still repaired and the nineteenth-century mansions of the shipowning families face the water. The perimeter road makes a fine flat cycle if the crew has energy. Come evening, the bars around the Baltiza inlet fill up slowly.
thingsToDo: Swim the sandy bay at Zogeria
Walk the Old Harbour shipyards
Cycle the flat perimeter road
Sunset drinks around the Baltiza inlet
2.mooringTip: Tie stern-to in Dapia harbour for €30–50 a night; it holds well and is protected from the north. The smaller Old Harbour is a quieter alternative if you want the mansion frontage to yourself.

3.shortDescription: A committed 32-mile run south to Kiparissi, a Parnon-backed fishing hamlet that the road barely reaches and most charter fleets never do.
3.description: This is the leg that leaves the busy Saronic behind. Thirty-two miles of open water bring you to Kiparissi, a scatter of white houses at the foot of Mount Parnon on the Laconian coast. Almost no charter boats push this far south, and the payoff is the quietest night of the fortnight. The anchorage sits in the bay below the village on good sand in five to seven metres, sheltered from the north. There is not much ashore beyond a handful of tavernas and a pebble beach, which is rather the point. Provision properly before you leave Spetses, because the village shop is small and the mountains cut it off from easy resupply. If the crew wants a walk, the old stone hamlet of Vrisi climbs the slope behind the harbour.
thingsToDo: Hike up to Vrisi stone hamlet
Snorkel the rocky shore north of the bay
Grilled fish at a quayside taverna
Watch sunset behind the Parnon ridge
3.mooringTip: Anchor free in Kiparissi bay on sand at five to seven metres, well sheltered from the north. Shore facilities are minimal, so fill water, fuel and food before leaving Spetses.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles on to Monemvasia, the medieval town welded to a tidal rock, anchoring off Gefyra and crossing the causeway on foot.
4.description: Monemvasia hides its whole town on the far side of a great rock, invisible from the sea until you round it. Locals call it the Gibraltar of the East, and the comparison holds. The anchorage lies off Gefyra, the modern town on the mainland, on sand in five to eight metres with shelter from the north. From there a single causeway carries you across to the medieval Lower Town, where perhaps a hundred people still live among the stone lanes, chapels and vaulted passages. Give yourself the stiff climb to the ruined Upper Town for the view back over the sea. This coast gave its name to Malvasia wine, the sweet Malmsey that medieval Venice shipped across Europe, and the local cellars still pour it. Book a marina slot inside Gefyra if the wind is building.
thingsToDo: Walk the car-free medieval Lower Town
Climb to the Upper Town ruins
Taste sweet Malvasia wine at a cellar
Photograph the rock from the causeway at dusk
4.mooringTip: Anchor off Gefyra on sand at five to eight metres, sheltered from the north. When the breeze picks up, a slot inside Gefyra's small marina runs €30–40 and is worth booking ahead.

5.shortDescription: A short 12-mile hop south to Gerakas, a deep natural cut into Cape Maleas that shelters you from every wind on the compass.
5.description: Gerakas is the sort of place you remember long after the charter. A narrow fjord bites into the Cape Maleas peninsula, and once you are inside, the swell and wind simply stop. This is the southernmost natural harbour on the Greek mainland, and its all-round shelter and firm sand make it one of the safest overnights on the route. The community is tiny: a few fishing boats, a couple of tavernas serving whatever came up that morning, and a single road climbing to the ancient ruins of Zarax on the cliff above. Swim in the still water of the inlet, then follow the olive-grove path along the ridge for the long view down the coast. There is little to do here in the organised sense, and that is exactly why we linger.
thingsToDo: Hike to the ancient Zarax ruins
Swim the calm inner inlet
Grilled octopus at a fishing taverna
Walk the olive-grove ridge path
5.mooringTip: Anchor free on sand at five to seven metres with all-round shelter, or take the small village quay for €15–25 when a berth is open. Holding is excellent.

6.shortDescription: Thirty-two miles back north to Leonidio, where a 600-metre red cliff towers over citrus orchards and the little port of Plaka.
6.description: Turn north now and start working back up the coast. Leonidio sits a few miles inland at the foot of a vast wall of red conglomerate rock that glows at the end of the day. The harbour is at Plaka, a small port among citrus orchards where you can lie stern-to on the quay or anchor in the bay outside. The town belongs to the Tsakonian region, which keeps its own ancient dialect and grows the prized Tsakoniki aubergine, deep purple and mild, that turns up in half the local dishes. Climbers know these cliffs well; in the cooler months Europeans come for the sport routes on the red rock. If you want a walk, the road up the gorge leads to Elona Monastery, clinging to the cliff face high above the valley.
thingsToDo: Drive up to Elona Monastery in the gorge
Taste the local Tsakoniki aubergine
Slow-cooked goat at a Plaka courtyard
Watch the red cliff glow at sunset
6.mooringTip: Lie stern-to in Plaka harbour for €15–25 a night, sheltered from the north. If the quay is full, anchor in the bay outside on sand at five to seven metres.

7.shortDescription: A lazy 15-mile leg to Sampatiki, a roadless pebble cove under a Venetian watchtower and the best place to steal a rest day.
7.description: Sampatiki asks nothing of you, which is why it earns a lay day. A short sail north of Leonidio brings you to a quiet inlet with a Venetian watchtower standing guard on the headland and no proper road linking it to the main Peloponnese routes. The pebble cove holds well on sand in four to six metres with shelter from the north, and two small tavernas cover everything you need for lunch and dinner. Spend the morning snorkelling the rocks below the tower, then climb up to the ruins for the view over the bay. The afternoon is for the cockpit and the cicadas. Because there are no services here, do your provisioning back in Leonidio or before, and arrive with full water tanks.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the rocks under the watchtower
Climb to the Venetian tower ruins
Chickpea pitaroudia fritters at a beach taverna
Read on deck through the afternoon
7.mooringTip: Anchor free on sand at four to six metres, sheltered from the north. There are no marina services, so arrive with full tanks and provisions already aboard.

8.shortDescription: Twenty miles north to Nafplio, the elegant first capital of Greece, mooring below the Palamidi fortress and its long stone staircase.
8.description: Nafplio is the prettiest town on the route and knows it. This was the first capital of independent Greece, and the Venetian past shows in the fortress walls, the marble Old Town squares and the little Bourtzi castle sitting on its islet in the middle of the harbour. Boats moor stern-to in the small marina beneath the Palamidi, the great hilltop fortress reached, so the story goes, by nine hundred and ninety-nine steps. Climb them in the late afternoon when the heat has eased and the light turns gold over the gulf. Down in the Old Town, the pedestrian streets are made for a slow evening: worry-bead shops, marble stairways, and cafés serving the almond amygdalota that the town is known for. It is a good place to reprovision properly.
thingsToDo: Climb the 999 steps to Palamidi
Wander the marble Old Town squares
Buy komboloi worry beads
Almond amygdalota at a back-street café
8.mooringTip: Stern-to in Nafplio marina costs €30–50 a night with shelter from the north. The town quay is not open to charter boats, so plan on the marina and reprovision while you are here.

9.shortDescription: A 32-mile crossing east to uninhabited Dhokos, anchoring in Skindos Bay above the oldest known shipwreck on the seabed.
9.description: Dhokos is a bare island with a big secret on its seabed. Lying between the Argolic peninsula and Hydra, it has no village, no road and no shop, just goats, dry-stone walls and a single chapel. Divers know it as the resting place of the Dokos wreck, a cargo of Bronze Age pottery that went down around 2200 BC and counts as the oldest shipwreck ever found. The pottery sits at about fifteen metres, so only certified divers reach it, though snorkellers can float over the shallower ground nearby. We anchor in Skindos Bay on the north coast, on sand in five to seven metres, tucked in from the south. Bring everything with you, because there is nothing here but the island itself. Sunsets over an empty horizon are the whole evening's entertainment.
thingsToDo: Snorkel above the Bronze Age wreck site
Walk the island's dry-stone walls
Beachcomb the empty north shore
Deck picnic of feta and figs
9.mooringTip: Anchor free in Skindos Bay on sand at five to seven metres, sheltered from the south. There are no services on Dhokos, so arrive fully provisioned with water and fuel.

10.shortDescription: A quick 8-mile skip to Hydra, the car-free island of stone captains' houses where donkeys still carry the luggage uphill.
10.description: Hydra runs on donkey power. No cars, no scooters, no bicycles even; mules and porters haul everything up the stepped lanes to the grey-stone mansions the sea captains built with their fortunes. The harbour is a natural amphitheatre and one of the loveliest in Greece, which is why the stern-to slots go fast. Aim to be tied up by mid-afternoon, and if the quay is full, anchor off Mandraki Bay a mile east and tender in. Once ashore, the island is all walking. Swim off the flat rocks at Spilia below the old cannons, or stroll fifteen minutes west to the shingle at Vlychos. The energetic can climb to Profitis Ilias monastery for the view. Come sunset, the cliff-cut bar at Spilia is the spot everyone drifts toward.
thingsToDo: Walk the car-free stone lanes uphill
Swim off the rocks at Spilia
Stroll west to Vlychos shingle beach
Climb to Profitis Ilias monastery
10.mooringTip: Hydra harbour is stern-to at €30–50 a night, and slots fill by mid-afternoon; arrive early or anchor in Mandraki Bay a mile east on sand at six to eight metres.

11.shortDescription: Twenty miles north to Palaia Epidavros, where you can snorkel a sunken Roman town and taxi up to the great ancient theatre.
11.description: Palaia Epidavros is a low-key mainland port with two archaeological draws, one wet and one dry. Just off the beach lies the Sunken City, the ruins of a Roman villa complex resting under two or three metres of clear water, close enough to the surface that a mask and snorkel show you the walls and jars quite clearly. We usually anchor there for the swim before moving to the town quay for the night. The bigger prize is inland: the fourth-century theatre of Epidaurus, whose acoustics are so precise that a coin dropped on the stage carries to the top row. It is a short taxi ride up, and worth the trip. Back at the harbour, look out for ladenia, the local tomato-and-onion flatbread, on the taverna menus.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the underwater Sunken City
Taxi to the ancient theatre of Epidaurus
Ladenia tomato flatbread at the quay
Paddleboard off the town beach at sunset
11.mooringTip: Stern-to on Palaia Epidavros town quay runs €15–25 a night, sheltered from the north. Day-anchor over the Sunken City on sand at three to five metres before mooring.

12.shortDescription: Twenty miles east to Aegina, mooring in the quiet fishing village of Perdika within reach of the Doric Temple of Aphaia.
12.description: Aegina is pistachio country, and the roadside stalls near the harbour sell the local Fistiki Aeginis by the bagful, sweeter and greener than most. Rather than the busy main town, we drop down to Perdika on the south coast for the night, a small fishing harbour where family tavernas line the quay and caïques run across to the islet of Moni. The headline sight is the Temple of Aphaia, a Doric temple from the fifth century BC that stands almost complete on a pine-covered hill in the northeast, older than the Parthenon and one of the best-preserved in the country. It is a short taxi ride from Perdika. Round off the evening with a slice of pistachio baklava and a walk along the water as the fishing boats come in.
thingsToDo: Taxi to the Doric Temple of Aphaia
Eat at the Perdika quay tavernas
Buy Aegina pistachios from a stall
Pistachio baklava at a kafeneio
12.mooringTip: Stern-to in Perdika harbour costs €20–30 a night in a calmer setting than the main town. Aegina town quay is the alternative if you want the bigger waterfront and its cafés.

13.shortDescription: A short 15-mile run back to the Alimos base, with a last swim off Agistri before refuelling and settling the crew's final dinner.
13.description: The last full day is deliberately easy. Fifteen miles northeast takes you back toward Alimos, and there is time to break it with a final swim; the sandy shallows off Skala on Agistri, just west of Aegina, make a good last dip before the marina. Aim for a mid-afternoon arrival so the crew is not rushing the tidy-up. Refuel at the fuel berth by the entrance before you take your berth, and give the boat a proper clean while things are still relaxed. Confirm your handover slot with the base a day ahead so the morning runs smoothly. In the evening, the tavernas up in Glyfada are an easy walk or a short taxi from the marina, and the Faliro promenade is the place for a last souvenir stroll along the water.
thingsToDo: Last swim off Skala beach on Agistri
Refuel and clean the boat at Alimos
Crew dinner at a Glyfada taverna
Souvenir stroll on the Faliro promenade
13.mooringTip: Alimos Marina is stern-to with lazy lines at €70–100 a night. Refuel at the entrance berth before mooring and confirm your handover slot with the base 24 hours ahead.

14.shortDescription: Handover at Alimos before nine, a walk-through inspection with the base manager, and a 25-minute road transfer to Athens airport.
14.description: Checkout mornings move quickly, so keep the last night's packing tidy. The base wants the boat handed back before nine, and the inspection is done with the skipper aboard so anything found gets agreed on the spot. Photograph any mark or scrape the manager notes before you sign the paperwork, and the deposit is normally released within a week once everything checks out. If the service was good, the dock crew appreciate a tip. Athens airport is only about twenty-five minutes away by road, though Friday traffic can stretch that, so allow a full hour if you are flying out at the end of the week. With time to spare, the Glyfada promenade is a short walk for a last Greek coffee before the transfer.
thingsToDo: Walk-through inspection with the base manager
Photograph any noted damage before signing
Last Greek coffee on the Glyfada promenade
Tip the dock crew if deserved
14.mooringTip: Hand back at Alimos before 09:00 for the inspection; the deposit is usually released within seven days. Take photos of any damage the base notes before signing off.

athens-saronic-gulf-poros.metaDesc: Round-trip yacht charter from Athens through the Saronic: Aegina, Poros, Ermioni, Methana, Palaia Epidavros and Agistri, temples and calm sailing.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 15-mile shakedown southwest to Aegina, where a hilltop temple, pistachio stalls and a working fishing quay set the tone for the week.
1.description: An easy first leg. Fifteen miles down from Alimos gives the crew time to find where everything lives on the boat before the real cruising starts, and the Saronic seldom throws more than 15 to 18 knots at you in July. Aegina's town quay sits on the west side, so you tie stern-to with the afternoon sun on your back; reckon on 25 to 40 euros. The Temple of Aphaia is the reason most people come. It stands on a pine ridge in the northeast, older than the Parthenon, and a short taxi gets you there before the light goes flat. Back at the harbour, buy a bag of the local pistachios from the stalls and try the revithokeftedes, fried chickpea patties the island does well. If the quay feels busy, Perdika down south is calmer.
thingsToDo: Taxi up to the Temple of Aphaia
Buy Aegina PDO pistachios at the quay stalls
Swim off Marathonas Beach south of town
Order revithokeftedes at a harbour taverna
Dinner among the fish tavernas at Perdika
1.mooringTip: Stern-to on Aegina's town quay runs 25 to 40 euros and shelters well from the north, though the wash from ferries and hydrofoils rocks the outer berths until late evening. For a quiet night, drop the hook off Perdika instead.

2.shortDescription: Fourteen miles to Poros, a pine island split from the mainland by a channel you could almost swim, with a long quay and quiet anchorages.
2.description: Another short passage, which leaves the morning free. We usually anchor at Love Bay on the south coast first, drop the hook onto sand in five to seven metres, and swim before the day-boats arrive from town. Poros itself sits behind a narrow strait, so the water along the quay stays flat whatever the wind does outside. Tie stern-to below the white clock tower and walk the front in the evening when the tavernas fire up their saganaki. Two things are worth the effort ashore: the scattered ruins of the Temple of Poseidon on the ridge above town, and the lemon groves at Galatas across the channel, a green wall of trees you can loop through on foot or by bike. Berthing runs 25 to 40 euros.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in Love Bay
Climb to the Temple of Poseidon ruins
Loop the Lemonodasos lemon groves at Galatas
Watch flaming saganaki on the waterfront
Evening walk up to the clock tower
2.mooringTip: The town quay wraps the whole waterfront and holds well in any wind thanks to the strait, but grab a spot by early afternoon in high season because it fills fast. Love Bay is day-anchoring only, sand at five to seven metres.

3.shortDescription: Ermioni sits ten miles south, a low-key fishing town on the Peloponnese with two harbours, a pine-tipped headland and the week's easiest pace.
3.description: Ermioni is where the trip slows down. It is a real fishing town rather than a resort, built along a spit of land with water on both sides, so you can pick your harbour by the wind. The north bay is smaller and better for anchoring on sand in four to six metres; the town quay on the south side is longer and costs next to nothing, around 15 to 25 euros. Break the passage at Bisti Beach, three miles short of the port, where a pine wood runs down almost to the shingle. Above the village, a half-hour walk brings you to the old Temple of Demeter and a long view back over the gulf. Down on the quay in the evening you will see octopus hung out to dry and stifado on most menus.
thingsToDo: Walk up to the Temple of Demeter
Anchor and swim at Bisti Beach
Stroll the pine-covered Bisti headland
Try stifado at a courtyard taverna
Watch octopus drying on the fish quay
3.mooringTip: Take the south-side town quay stern-to for 15 to 25 euros; it is well protected from the north. If you prefer swinging room, anchor in the north bay on sand at four to six metres and dinghy across to the tavernas.

4.shortDescription: Turn north for fifteen miles to Methana, a volcanic thumb of land where sulphur springs bubble into the harbour and a crater walk waits above the port.
4.description: Methana is the oddest stop on the loop, in the best way. The whole peninsula is an old volcanic field, and warm sulphur water seeps straight into the port, so pick a berth on the far side of the basin and the smell stays behind you. Stern-to costs around 20 to 30 euros. Late afternoon is the time to walk up to Kameni Chora and out onto the black lava flow, an hour each way over ground that looks nothing like the rest of Greece. Come back down for a soak in the thermal spring by the water, then eat grilled fish on the quay with a cold local rose. If you want a swim first, Vathy Bay just north of the port has clean water and room to anchor.
thingsToDo: Soak in the harbour-side thermal springs
Hike to Kameni Chora and the lava flow
Swim and anchor at Vathy Bay
Grilled fish and local rose on the quay
4.mooringTip: Moor stern-to for 20 to 30 euros on the north-sheltered quay, and choose a berth away from the spring outflow at the head of the harbour, where the sulphur is strongest. Nothing here needs booking.

5.shortDescription: A short eight-mile hop to Palaia Epidavros, where you snorkel a sunken Roman town by day and taxi up to the great theatre by afternoon.
5.description: The shortest leg of the week, eight miles up the coast, so there is no rush to leave. On the way in, anchor over the Sunken City off the north cove, where a Roman harbour lies under two or three metres of water and snorkels up clearly on a calm morning. The town quay is small and cheap, 15 to 25 euros, and the village keeps a slow, unpolished feel. The headline is inland: the great theatre of Epidavros, one of the finest antiquities in Greece, sits about four kilometres up the valley and takes a short taxi. If you would rather stay by the boat, the little ancient theatre right in the village sometimes hosts summer concerts. Ladenia, a Greek tomato flatbread, makes a good lunch on the front.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Sunken City Roman ruins
Taxi to the great theatre of Epidavros
Catch a concert at the little ancient theatre
Lunch on ladenia tomato flatbread
Paddleboard off the quay at sunset
5.mooringTip: Stern-to on the town quay is cheap at 15 to 25 euros and sheltered from the north. Anchor over the Sunken City only as a day stop, on sand at three to five metres, and move into the harbour before the afternoon breeze fills in.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen miles back across the gulf to Agistri, the quietest island of the loop, ringed with pine, pebble coves and easy family tavernas.
6.description: Agistri is tiny, green and largely overlooked, which is exactly why it is worth a night. The crossing back east runs about fifteen miles, with Aegina passing to port near the end. Aim for Skala on the east coast, where the harbour is simple and the tavernas are run by the same families year after year; the local move is lobster spaghetti. Berthing sits around 20 to 30 euros. Before you tie up, stop at Halikiada on the south coast, a pebble cove under a cliff reached only from the water, and anchor on sand in four to six metres for a swim. With time to spare, kayak round to the Dragonera sea caves or borrow bikes and ride the shaded loop through the island's cypress and pine.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at Halikiada cove
Kayak to the Dragonera sea caves
Cycle the shaded cypress-and-pine loop
Lobster spaghetti at a Skala family taverna
Beach afternoon under the pines at Dragonera
6.mooringTip: Skala harbour takes you stern-to for 20 to 30 euros with good shelter from the north. Halikiada has no road access, so treat it as a day anchorage on sand at four to six metres and leave before the evening wind.

7.shortDescription: Twenty miles northeast back to Alimos, with a last swim off uninhabited Moni before you refuel, tidy the boat and hand her back.
7.description: The run home is the longest of the week at twenty miles, but the Saronic usually stays kind and you will have the wind behind you on a northeasterly heading. Break it with one more swim off Moni, the little uninhabited islet just south of Aegina, where the water drops away clear and there is rarely anyone about. Most charter bases want the boat back around two in the afternoon, so leave Agistri by mid-morning and top up the tanks at the Alimos fuel berth before you slot into the marina. Once she is tied up and rinsed, the evening is yours: the tavernas up in Glyfada are a short hop from the base, and the Faliro promenade is the place for a last coffee and a souvenir before flights.
thingsToDo: Last swim off uninhabited Moni islet
Refuel at the Alimos fuel berth
Crew dinner at a Glyfada taverna
Coffee and souvenirs on Faliro promenade
7.mooringTip: Alimos is a lazy-line marina at 70 to 100 euros, so come in bows-out to your allocated berth and pick up the lazy line rather than dropping an anchor. Refuel at the entrance berth first, and confirm your handover slot 24 hours ahead so a place is kept for you.

athens-saronic-gulf.metaDesc: Round-trip yacht charter from Athens through the Saronic Gulf: Aegina, Poros, Spetses, Hydra and Agistri. Short hops, sheltered water, easy mooring.

1.shortDescription: An easy fifteen-mile run southwest from Alimos gets the crew settled, with Aegina's pistachio stalls and the Aphaia temple waiting at the far end.
1.description: First day out is really about bedding in — stowing the provisions, finding where the winch handles live, getting a feel for the boat before anything demanding. Alimos to Aegina is a gentle fifteen miles, and the Saronic hands you sheltered water from the start; the surrounding islands and the Peloponnese keep the fetch down, so even a brisk afternoon breeze rarely builds a real sea. Aegina town sits on the west coast, and you moor stern-to along the quay right in front of the cafes. Grab pistachios from the harbour stalls; the island grows the best in Greece. If you have an afternoon spare, a short taxi ride climbs to the Temple of Aphaia, older than the Parthenon and worth the detour. We usually eat late here, gourounopoulo off the spit.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Temple of Aphaia
Buy Aegina pistachios at the quay stalls
Swim at Marathonas beach south of town
Try gourounopoulo at a waterfront taverna
See the lone column at Kolona
1.mooringTip: The town quay is stern-to and holds well, but it fills with day-trippers' boats and the ferry wash rolls through; if you want calm, Perdika on the south coast is quieter for the night. Reckon on 25-40 euros for the town berth.

2.shortDescription: Fourteen miles down to Poros, where a slim channel divides the island from the mainland and Love Bay's pines shade a lunchtime swim.
2.description: Poros is a short leg, so there's no rush; we tend to leave Aegina mid-morning and take our time. The approach is one of the prettier ones in the gulf, threading the channel between the island and Galatas on the Peloponnese, close enough to wave at people on the quay. Before you tie up, drop anchor at Love Bay on the south side. Pines run right down to the water, the bottom is sand at five to seven metres, and it holds fine in a northerly. The town quay stretches along the whole waterfront under the clock tower, so finding a slot is rarely a problem. Evening plan: a plate of flaming saganaki and a walk up the stepped lanes for the view over the strait.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at Love Bay
Walk up to the Poros clock tower
Order flaming saganaki on the waterfront
Dinghy across to the Galatas lemon groves
Find the Temple of Poseidon ruins inland
2.mooringTip: Stern-to anywhere along the town quay; it's well sheltered whatever the wind does, so slot pressure is low. At Love Bay drop the hook in five to seven metres of sand and you'll sit comfortably in a northerly.

3.shortDescription: Twenty miles southwest to Spetses, the car-free island where horse carriages clatter past old shipyards and Dapia harbour buzzes after dark.
3.description: Longest hop so far, and the one where the breeze usually earns its keep; the afternoon sea wind fills in from the south, so an earlier start pays off if you want to sail rather than motor. Spetses keeps cars out of the old town, and the result is a place that runs on horse carriages and bicycles. Dapia is the main harbour and the social heart, lined with cafes and the grand old Poseidonion hotel. Walk round to the Old Harbour at Baltiza, where craftsmen still build wooden caiques the traditional way and the pirate-styled bars come alive at sunset. If the sun's high when you arrive, anchor first at Zogeria on the north coast, all pine and clear sand, and swim before you commit to a berth.
thingsToDo: Watch caique builders at the Old Harbour
Anchor and swim at Zogeria bay
Cycle the coast road to Agioi Anargyroi
Sunset drinks at the Baltiza bars
See Bouboulina's mansion near Dapia
3.mooringTip: Dapia can get chaotic in high season and the berths angle awkwardly, so arrive by mid-afternoon and expect a lively night; the smaller Old Harbour is calmer and far more photogenic if you don't mind the dinghy walk into town. Around 30-50 euros a night.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles north to Hydra, all cliffside stone and no cars; the harbour fills early, so plan to arrive before mid-afternoon.
4.description: Hydra is the showpiece of the Saronic and it knows it, so the harbour is the one genuine scramble of the week. There are no cars anywhere on the island; donkeys and mules haul everything up the stepped lanes to the grey-stone captains' houses stacked above the water. The catch is berths. Slots along the quay go fast, and if you roll in after three you may find nothing left. We aim to be tied up by two, or else drop anchor in Mandraki a mile east and run the crew in by tender. Once you're settled, the town rewards a wander with no plan at all. Climb past the cannons at the harbour mouth, find the Spilia rocks for a swim and a cold drink, and let the evening happen.
thingsToDo: Swim off the Spilia rocks below town
Wander the stone lanes above the harbour
Walk west to Kamini and Vlychos beach
Hike up to Profitis Ilias monastery
Drink at Hydronetta on the cliff rocks
4.mooringTip: The main quay is stern-to and coveted; get in by early afternoon in July and August or you'll be hunting. If it's full, Mandraki Bay a mile to the east has good holding in six to eight metres of sand and a short tender hop into town. Roughly 30-50 euros for a berth.

5.shortDescription: The long leg back north to Aegina, twenty-five miles; skip the busy town this time and tuck into Perdika's quiet fishing quay.
5.description: Twenty-five miles makes this the biggest passage of the trip, so we get the anchor up early and use the flat morning water before the breeze wakes. It's a straight enough run north, and by early afternoon you're back off Aegina. Rather than fight for the town quay a second time, aim for Perdika on the southern tip. It's a working fishing village with a row of tavernas along the water and none of the ferry commotion. On the way in, Marathonas beach is an easy anchor for a last swim before you moor. Perdika does simple, honest food well, straight-off-the-boat fish and a carafe of local white. Across the water sits little Moni, an islet reserve where deer and peacocks wander; worth a dinghy poke if there's light left.
thingsToDo: Eat fresh fish on the Perdika quay
Swim at Marathonas beach on the way in
Dinghy over to Moni islet's deer reserve
Visit the Agios Nektarios monastery
Watch the sunset from the harbour wall
5.mooringTip: Perdika is stern-to on a small, sheltered quay and calmer than Aegina town, usually 20-30 euros a night; it's popular for dinner so an early-afternoon arrival secures a spot. Marathonas offers a clean sand anchor at five metres for the swim stop.

6.shortDescription: A five-mile amble northwest to Agistri, the sleepy pine island most crews miss, with Aponissos for a swim and Skala for the night.
6.description: After the crowds of Hydra and Spetses, Agistri feels like the gulf let you in on a secret. It's a five-mile stroll from Aegina, barely enough to trim the sails, so there's no reason to hurry off. The island is small and thick with pine, and the pace drops the moment you arrive. Start at Aponissos on the southwest corner, a shallow turquoise cut behind a tiny islet where the bottom is pale sand and the water goes see-through. Skala on the east side is where you'll spend the night, a modest harbour with family tavernas a few steps from the quay. Order the lobster spaghetti if it's on. With the afternoon free, rent bikes and loop through the cypress woods, or walk the short climb up to old Metochi village for the view back over Aegina.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at Aponissos cove
Cycle the pine-and-cypress island loop
Eat lobster spaghetti at a Skala taverna
Walk up to Metochi hill village
Find the wild Chalikiada pebble beach
6.mooringTip: Skala harbour is small and stern-to, sheltered from the north and cheap at 20-30 euros; it doesn't take many boats, so don't leave it too late in peak weeks. Aponissos is a straightforward day anchor on sand in four to six metres.

7.shortDescription: Twenty miles back northeast to Alimos, with a last swim off uninhabited Moni possible before you refuel and hand the boat back.
7.description: Last day, and it's a straightforward twenty miles back up to Alimos. There's usually time for one more swim, so if the crew wants it, swing past Moni off Aegina and drop the hook in a quiet cove before pointing north. The important jobs are practical ones. Top up the fuel at the marina berth before you tie up, so the tanks are full for the handover and nobody's scrambling at check-in. Most bases want the boat back by early afternoon, so build the morning around that rather than a leisurely brunch. Once you're secured, Glyfada is a short taxi away for a proper farewell dinner, and the Faliro promenade is good for a last stroll and a souvenir before the transfers pull everyone in different directions.
thingsToDo: Last swim off uninhabited Moni islet
Refuel and rinse down at Alimos
Farewell dinner at a Glyfada taverna
Stroll the Faliro seafront promenade
7.mooringTip: Alimos is a big lazy-line marina, stern-to at 70-100 euros a night; take the fuel berth by the entrance first and confirm your handover time a day ahead so the slot's ready. Aim to be alongside by early afternoon.

corfu-kassiopi-sagiada.metaDesc: Sail from Corfu to the Diapontia islands, Sagiada and Sivota's Blue Lagoon on a 7-day north Ionian yacht charter: castles, coves and calm working ports.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 14-mile shakedown up Corfu's green east coast to Kassiopi, a working fishing harbour under a ruined castle and Mount Pantokrator.
1.description: Collect the boat at Gouvia and clear the marina by mid-morning; the maestro stays soft until early afternoon, so the first hours under sail are usually the calmest of the week. The coast slides past in olive terraces and the odd whitewashed monastery, the mountain growing at your shoulder. Kassiopi sits at the northeast corner, its harbour tucked below a Byzantine and Angevin fortress you can walk up to in twenty minutes. We usually swim first, at Bataria or Kanoni, two pebble coves a short stroll from the quay. Albania is close enough across the strait to pick out cars on the far road. Evening is for the harbour tavernas and a plate of pastitsada, the cinnamon-scented beef and pasta that Corfu does better than anywhere. Book a berth ahead in July and August.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Byzantine-Angevin castle ruins
Swim the pebbles at Bataria and Kanoni
Drive up Mount Pantokrator (906 m)
Pastitsada at a harbour-front taverna
1.mooringTip: The village quay is small and fills fast in season, so aim to arrive before four or pre-book. Avlaki bay, a mile east, gives good holding on sand in 4-6 m if the harbour is full.

2.shortDescription: Eighteen miles out from Corfu's northwest corner lies Ereikoussa, the least of the Diapontia trio, a place where working caiques still outnumber the tourists.
2.description: This is the first proper open-water leg, so leave early. Once you round the north cape the coast falls away and it is just the three Diapontia specks on the horizon. Ereikoussa is the nearest and the smallest, home to a few dozen people through winter and a scatter of houses around one harbour. There is little to do here, which is rather the point. Bragini, the island's sandy beach, shelves gently and stays warm; a path climbs to the hilltop chapel for a long view back toward the Albanian mountains. Snorkelling off the rocks is clear and unhurried. Ashore, the tavernas run on whatever the boats brought in, and bianco, the local white fish stew heavy with garlic, is the thing to order. Nights here are dark and very quiet.
thingsToDo: Swim and laze at Bragini beach
Walk up to the hilltop chapel
Snorkel the clear rocky shallows
Order bianco, the garlic white-fish stew
2.mooringTip: The little harbour quay is first-come and shallow, so sound your way in. In settled weather anchor on sand off Bragini beach in 4-6 m, well sheltered from the south and southeast.

3.shortDescription: A lazy day on Ereikoussa, with the option of a four-mile hop to Othonoi, Greece's most westerly inhabited island and home to Calypso's mythic cave.
3.description: A day to do very little, or to potter west to Othonoi if the crew wants a sail. Othonoi is the largest and most westerly of the Diapontia, four miles across open water, with a small harbour on its south side and a couple of tavernas at Ammos. The draw is Aspri Ammos, a white cove reached best by tender, and the sea cave beside it that locals tie to the Calypso myth. Back on Ereikoussa the rhythm is beach, shade, a long lunch of tomatoes and feta, and not much else. Come dusk the kafeneio by the water fills up with the island's handful of regulars over raki and cards. If you make the Othonoi run, plan it as a day trip and be back on your Ereikoussa berth before the evening breeze.
thingsToDo: Tender into Aspri Ammos and Calypso's cave
Picnic in the shade at Bragini
Optional 4-mile sail to Othonoi
Raki and cards at the kafeneio
3.mooringTip: Othonoi's harbour at Ammos is a small first-come quay best used by day. Hold your overnight berth back on Ereikoussa rather than trusting a late spot on Othonoi.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles back east to Sagiada, a working mainland fishing village on the Albanian border beside the bird-rich Kalamas river delta.
4.description: Turn back toward the mainland on a broad reach, twenty miles east to Sagiada. This is a genuine working port rather than a resort: nets drying on the quay, small boats coming and going, and a line of seafood tavernas facing the water. The Kalamas delta spreads just to the south, a reed-and-lagoon wetland that is one of the better birdwatching corners of the region; herons and, in the right season, flamingos work the shallows at first light. Mussel and oyster beds out in the estuary supply the tables, so eat shellfish here and eat it fresh. The village itself is a handful of lanes that empty by mid-evening. From Sagiada it is a short run to the border, and ancient Butrint over in Albania is within reach as a day trip if your paperwork is in order.
