thingsToDo: Dawn birdwatching over the Kalamas delta
Eat estuary mussels at a quayside taverna
Wander the working fishing harbour
Day-trip to ancient Butrint in Albania
4.mooringTip: Sagiada is a small fishing-port quay, first-come and best entered slowly. A crossing into Albania needs a transit log arranged in advance, so sort the paperwork before you sail if Butrint is on the plan.

5.shortDescription: A short coastal sail south to the Sivota islets and the Blue Lagoon, a shallow band of aquamarine over white sand that draws the fleet.
5.description: An easy leg down the coast to Sivota, where a knot of low islets shelters the water everyone comes for. The Blue Lagoon sits between the Mourtos islands, so shallow and pale that the boat seems to float on light; anchor off and spend the middle of the day in it, swimming or on a board. Bella Vraka, the sandbar linking two of the islets, is worth the wade across at low water. Come evening, move into Sivota itself and take a berth along the waterfront, where the tavernas run right to the quay. Bourdeto, a fierce red scorpionfish stew, is the local plate to try. The bay fills with masthead lights after dark and the front stays lively, so this is the sociable night of the week rather than the quiet one.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim the Blue Lagoon
Wade the Bella Vraka sandbar
Paddleboard between the Mourtos islets
Bourdeto scorpionfish stew on the Sivota front
5.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth along the Sivota waterfront for the night, arriving early in season for a front-row spot. The Blue Lagoon is a daytime swim anchorage only; it is exposed and busy, so do not plan to lie there overnight.

6.shortDescription: A longer 22-mile passage back to Corfu, with an optional stop at Paleokastritsa's cliff-backed coves, ending in the old town for the last night.
6.description: The way home is the longest sail of the week, so leave the Sivota front in good time. If the crew is willing, break the run at Paleokastritsa on Corfu's west coast, a string of coves under wooded cliffs with a hilltop monastery watching over them; Homer's readers have long placed the wreck of Odysseus somewhere along this shore. From there round the island for the final approach to Corfu Town. The old town is UNESCO-listed and rewards an aimless wander: the arcaded Liston built by the French, narrow Venetian lanes, and shops selling kumquat liqueur and gold-thread embroidery. For the last dinner, find a courtyard taverna and order sofrito, the Corfiot veal braised slow in wine, garlic and vinegar. Berth back at Gouvia for the night unless you have arranged a short-stay spot in town.
thingsToDo: Break the passage at Paleokastritsa's coves
Visit the hilltop Theotokos monastery
Wander the Liston and Venetian lanes
Sofrito veal at a courtyard taverna
6.mooringTip: Gouvia Marina is the practical overnight and the base you hand back to. The town quays are short-stay only and get congested, so treat Corfu Town as a walk-and-dine stop rather than a berth unless you have booked ahead.

7.shortDescription: A final morning at Gouvia for the handover, with time for a last coffee on the Liston and a turn around the Spianada.
7.description: The last morning belongs to the handover. Have the boat cleaned and the tanks topped the evening before so check-out at Gouvia is unhurried, and confirm your fuel slot early. If there is time before you leave, take the bus or a taxi into town for a proper Corfiot send-off. Sit under the arcades of the Liston with a coffee and watch the Spianada, the vast green esplanade where, thanks to the British, cricket is still played through summer. The Museum of Asian Art in the old palace is a genuinely good hour if the weather turns. Pick up olive-wood or a bottle of kumquat on the way back. It is a soft end to a week that mixed offshore islands, a working border coast and the busiest lagoon in the Ionian.
thingsToDo: Coffee under the Liston arcades
Stroll the Spianada esplanade
Visit the Museum of Asian Art
Buy kumquat liqueur and olive wood
7.mooringTip: Handover is at Gouvia Marina. Clean the boat and settle the fuel the evening before so the morning check-out and inventory go quickly.

corfu-lefkas-ithaka-14-days.metaTitle: 14-Day Corfu to Ithaka Ionian Loop | Boat4You
corfu-lefkas-ithaka-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks under sail from Corfu: Paxos, Antipaxos, Lefkas, Meganisi, Kefalonia and Ithaka, then the quiet Diapontia outliers on the way home.

1.shortDescription: Thirty miles down the Corfu Channel to Gaios, a Venetian inlet guarded by two islets; settle the lines before the afternoon rush.
1.description: Leave Gouvia early and let the southwesterly fill in as you clear the harbour mouth. The run down the channel is straightforward, and by the time Paxos rises ahead the slopes read greener than you expect, with olive groves right down to the rock. Gaios is the port everyone aims for. Two low islets, Agios Nikolaos and Panagia, screen the entrance and turn it into a channel barely wide enough for two boats to pass. The old town wraps the inner basin in stone, the layout unchanged since the Venetians set out the campiello. Get your lines made fast, then find a table on the quay. Ask for ladotyri, the local cheese kept under olive oil, and grilled sea bream if the day's catch runs to it. Tomorrow's leg is tiny, so there is no reason to hurry the evening.
thingsToDo: Dinghy out to the Tripitos sea arch
Espresso on the pastel-blue harbour front
Sample ladotyri, the oil-cured island cheese
Stroll the cobbles to Panagia chapel
Fresh grilled bream on the town quay
1.mooringTip: The town quay gives you lazy lines and stern-to berthing for a small fee, with water on the pontoon, but get in before 15:00 in high summer — it's a tight basin that fills quickly. Shut out? Anchor on sand at Mongonissi cove, a couple of miles south, or grab a free buoy over at Lakka.

2.shortDescription: A four-mile drift south to Antipaxos, where sand bottoms turn the water to aqua; swim, lunch, then back to Paxos before dark.
2.description: Barely four miles separate the two islands, so this is a swimming day more than a sailing one. Antipaxos has no harbour and no overnight, which keeps it quieter than the crowds elsewhere suggest. Anchor in Vrika or Voutoumi, both floored with pale sand at three to seven metres and free of the weedy patches that dull the colour along other coasts. Sunlight comes straight back up off the bottom and gives you the turquoise the island trades on. There is a single taverna above Voutoumi beach with a handful of tables and a lobster pasta that people plan the day around. Above Vrika, a small vineyard still makes Verdea, the amber house white you will not find bottled anywhere else. Work back along the west coast in the afternoon and look into the cliff caves before the light goes and you have to be off the hook.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Voutoumi for a long swim
Hike up to the Verdea vineyard
Lobster spaghetti at the Voutoumi taverna
Paddle across the shallows to Vrika
Shoot the west-coast cliff caves
2.mooringTip: Anchoring only. Both Vrika and Voutoumi hold well on sand in three to seven metres, but there is no staying the night, so be back at Gaios, Mongonissi or Lakka by dusk. Both bays open to the south, so if a southeasterly is forecast, take your swim early and move on.

3.shortDescription: The one big passage: forty miles southeast across open water to Nydri, with a mid-crossing swim off Kastos or Kalamos to break it up.
3.description: This is the long haul of the fortnight, and the only leg the Ionian really makes you work for. There is no comfortable halfway harbour, so plan to slip the anchor by eight and use the calm early hours. Run south in the lee of Paxos first, then bend east toward Kastos or Kalamos, where a lunch stop and a swim sit almost exactly mid-passage. The west-going thermal usually arrives around one o'clock; on the rare day it pushes past eighteen knots you will want Lefkas already on the bow. Nydri is unashamedly a working resort, all water taxis and tavernas that could set your lines in their sleep. If you reach the channel with daylight to spare, ease past Skorpios on the way in. Onassis's old island still keeps its rope across the one small beach, but the water off the cape is anyone's.
thingsToDo: Swim stop off Kastos or Kalamos
Ease past the Skorpios anchorage
Sofrito veal on the Nydri waterfront
Walk the Nydri waterfalls trail
Kafenio coffee on the quay
3.mooringTip: Nydri's long town quay takes 20-plus boats stern-to (small fee, water and power) but expect it full by four in season. Quieter alternatives are Tranquil Bay a mile south on 5-8 m sand, or the little marina at Vlicho another mile on.

4.shortDescription: A lazy six-mile hop to Meganisi and its three sheltered bays; pick where you will sleep by lunch and give the afternoon to the water.
4.description: After yesterday, six miles feels like a rest day, and Meganisi is built for it. The island lies just across a mile-wide channel from Nydri, folding three deep, well-protected bays into a coastline you could drive round before lunch. Papanikolis Cave on the western point is the obvious first stop, a wartime submarine hideout the Greek navy used to duck German patrols, now a ten-minute poke about by dinghy. Above the western bay, Spartochori climbs the cliff in white terraces; the stair-walk up rewards you with the whole of Lefkas laid out behind. The bay tavernas run their own moorings, Niagas in Atheni and Errikos in Vathy among them, and the buoy comes free when you eat ashore, so wander up in the morning and reserve one. Settle on your bay early. The rest of the day is swimming, snorkelling and doing very little.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Papanikolis submarine cave
Climb the steps to Spartochori village
Grilled octopus on the Atheni quay
Dinghy a lap around Skorpios
Swim off a south-coast pebble cove
4.mooringTip: Vathy, Atheni and Spilia all have restaurant moorings; the buoy is yours for the price of dinner, booked in the morning. For a formal berth, Vathy's small marina has lazy lines and a fee. Holding is reliable on 4-8 m sand and shelter is excellent in any northerly.

5.shortDescription: Eighteen miles south to Fiskardo, the one Kefalonian village the 1953 quake spared, its pastel harbour now the most-photographed in the Ionian.
5.description: Eighteen miles down the sound brings you to Fiskardo, tucked under the northern cape of Kefalonia. When the 1953 earthquake flattened almost every village on the island, this one came through, and its row of pastel neoclassical houses around a tight inner harbour has been photographed half to death ever since. The quay is free but ruthless in August; call the port on Channel 12 before one, or anchor round in Foulis Bay a mile south on 4-6 m sand. Mind the wash off passing yachts in the basin. With a hire car you can reach the Myrtos overlook and the white limestone crescent below, or the flooded cavern at Melissani near Sami, each an easy half-day. Out on the water, the little wreck off Emplisi cove makes a good snorkel. Dinner at Tselenti up the hill or Lagoudera on the front.
thingsToDo: Walk the pastel neoclassical waterfront
Drive up to the Myrtos Beach overlook
Tour the Melissani underground lake
Snorkel the small wreck off Emplisi
Sundowner at Tselenti up the hill
5.mooringTip: Fiskardo's town quay is free but packed by early afternoon in August, and the wash from passing traffic makes it lively, so raise the port on Channel 12 before 13:00. Foulis Bay, a mile south, is the calm fallback on 4-6 m sand with good holding.

6.shortDescription: A five-mile skip east to Frikes, the smallest of Ithaka's ports, where the tavernas still cook the morning's catch under an old windmill.
6.description: Frikes is the least visited of Ithaka's three ports, the one crews making for Vathy pass over — five miles of open water and you drop in. It stays a fishing harbour first and a yacht stop second, an old windmill on the point above it and a handful of tavernas where the kitchen still cooks whatever came in that morning. Order the octopus stifado; Symposium and Penelope both do it well. Inland, the dig at Stavros lays claim to the foundations of Odysseus's palace, and the small museum in the same village is honest about how contested that still is. If your legs are willing, the goat track north to Kioni is about forty-five minutes each way and gentler than the map suggests. Finish the evening back under the windmill with a glass of Robola.
thingsToDo: Walk inland to the Stavros dig
Hike the goat path to Kioni
Swim at Polis Bay south of town
Robola wine flight at an ouzeri
Sundowner under the harbour windmill
6.mooringTip: Frikes is stern-to on your own anchor, free but with no services, and takes only 8-10 boats, so it pays to arrive by early afternoon. If it is full, Kioni two miles south is another free quay, or drop the hook on sand in Polis Bay. Shelter is solid in everything but a strong southerly.

7.shortDescription: Fourteen easy miles back north to Nydri, the mid-charter reset stop for fuel, laundry and provisions, with a Skorpios swim on the way.
7.description: Fourteen miles north under the shelter of Lefkas's east coast is a gentle afternoon's work, and a good one to break the trip in half. If the sea is kind, pause at Skorpios as you come through; the small western beach is the only place you are allowed to land, but nobody minds you swimming off the cape. Nydri is the practical stop of the two weeks. It is busy and a touch touristy, and that is the whole point: the laundry, the fuel berth, the supermarket and the phone shop all sit within a couple of hundred metres of the quay, so the chores take an hour rather than a morning. In July and August the mezedopoleia along the front lay on live music most nights, laouto and bouzouki, sometimes a violin. Pick one with a courtyard out back. Take on fuel before six so the morning stays clear.
thingsToDo: Swim off the Skorpios cape
Refuel at the Nydri fuel dock
Restock at the harbour supermarket
Run a load at the laundromat
Live laouto at a mezedopoleio
7.mooringTip: Nydri's quay is stern-to for a small fee with water, power and fuel right alongside, and the berthing is easy on a long wall, but it books out by five in August. Tranquil Bay a mile south is the quiet overnight option on 5-8 m sand.

8.shortDescription: Twenty-five miles up the mainland to Parga, timed around the Lefkas Canal bridge, ending under a Venetian castle with prawns on the quay.
8.description: The day starts with the swing bridge across the Lefkas Canal, which opens on the hour, every hour, so aim to slip your lines around eight and join the queue early; the holding basin north of it takes thirty-odd boats and the crew there feed you through in good order. Once you are out, the southwesterly usually sets up a comfortable beam reach up the Epirus coast, past the Sivota inlets toward Parga. The town spills down a hillside under a fourteenth-century Venetian castle, pastel houses stacked above a curved bay. Anchor off Valtos beach a mile west for the swim, then move into the town anchorage or take a buoy off Krioneri for the night. Saganaki prawns are the order on the harbour front, and the walk up to the castle walls at sunset earns every step of the cliche.
thingsToDo: Swim in the Valtos Beach shallows
Climb to the Venetian castle for sunset
Saganaki prawns on the harbour front
Wander the lantern-lit old-town stairs
Coffee at the Krioneri beach cafe
8.mooringTip: Anchor in Parga bay on 5-10 m sand with good holding, or take one of the few stern-to slots on the small town quay. The bay is open to the south and southwest, so if a southerly is on the way, tuck round into Valtos cove a mile west.

9.shortDescription: Fourteen miles up the coast to mainland Sivota and its Blue Lagoon, where a narrow gap between two islets turns the water electric turquoise.
9.description: Fourteen miles further north lands you at Sivota, the mainland one in Thesprotia rather than its namesake over on Lefkas, and a good deal smaller. The draw is the Blue Lagoon, a two-hundred-metre channel between the Mavro Oros and Mourtemeno islets where the water goes the improbable shade the name promises. Anchor in five to eight metres of sand off Bella Vraka for a long swim, with the sandbar between the islets as the backdrop everyone photographs. Move onto the town quay for the night, where there is water, power and a small fee, and a line of tavernas that make bourdeto, the fiery scorpionfish stew, their calling card. Before dinner, the pine path above Mega Trafos cove is a quiet hour's walk with the bay opening up below you. A mastiha over ice on the seafront rounds it off.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Blue Lagoon between the islets
Bourdeto fish stew on the quay
Dinghy over to the Bella Vraka sandbar
Walk the pine path above Mega Trafos
Mastiha nightcap on the seafront
9.mooringTip: Stern-to on the town quay for a small fee with water and power, or anchor off Bella Vraka on 5-8 m sand. It is well sheltered from the north and northwest and fine for the night, but weekends get crowded; call the port on Channel 9 if the quay looks tight.

10.shortDescription: Eighteen miles north to Sagiada, a working fishing village at the very edge of Greece, with a birdwatchers' delta and Albania just beyond.
10.description: Sagiada sits about as far north as Greece runs on this shore, eighteen miles up the coast, with the Albanian frontier a couple of miles on and the Kalamas River wetlands spread out just to the south. The change of gear from the Corfu marinas is the reason to come: a quay lined with working caiques, a row of ouzeria with a single table each, and a stillness that explains a lot about the map. This bank of the channel was always the poorer neighbour of the island opposite. Birdwatchers make for the Kalamas Delta at first light, when flamingos and herons work the reedbeds and a boardwalk runs south from the village along the reserve. With your transit paperwork in hand, the crossing to Butrint on the Albanian side reaches one of the Mediterranean's best-kept Hellenistic and Roman sites inside an hour.
thingsToDo: Dawn birdwatching at the Kalamas Delta
Walk the working caique harbour
Fresh mussels at a quayside ouzeri
Cross to the Butrint ruins in Albania
Sundowner over the Albanian coast
10.mooringTip: The little fishing quay is first-come, free and basic, with excellent shelter from any direction. To cross into Albania for Butrint you need a transit log arranged in advance, so lodge it with the marina office back in Corfu before you sail, not on the day.

11.shortDescription: A dozen miles out to Ereikoussa, tiniest of the Diapontia group, with a lone harbour and beach, two tavernas and barely fifty permanent residents.
11.description: Twelve miles out from the mainland lifts you clear onto the Diapontia islands, a scatter of three, Ereikoussa, Othonoi and Mathraki, that sit northwest of Corfu, nearer Italy than Athens and quiet in a way the big island stopped being decades ago. Ereikoussa is the smallest and the easiest to reach: one harbour, one cedar-shaded beach at Braghini, two tavernas and under fifty people wintering here. The walk up to the hilltop chapel takes about forty minutes and, on a clear day, opens the whole Strait of Otranto, with the Albanian mountains one way and the heel of Italy the other. Othonoi, four miles west, makes a good afternoon add-on, with the so-called cave of Calypso on its south shore and a small harbour of its own. Come back for the night; raki and dominoes at the kafeneio is how the evening goes out here.
thingsToDo: Walk up to the hilltop chapel
Laze on Braghini's cedar-shaded beach
Sail four miles west to Othonoi
Bianco garlic-fish stew at a family taverna
Late raki at the harbour kafeneio
11.mooringTip: Ereikoussa's harbour quay is tiny and first-come, or anchor on sand off the main beach in 4-6 m, sheltered from the south and southeast. There is no fuel and no shop worth the name out here, so top up tanks and provisions in Corfu Town before the crossing.

12.shortDescription: Sixteen miles southeast to Corfu's north coast and Kassiopi, a harbour village under a ruined Byzantine fort with Albania close across the strait.
12.description: Back on the Greek side, sixteen miles southeast, Kassiopi wraps around its headland below a thirteenth-century Byzantine fort. The castle is a ruin: the Venetians pulled down the upper walls in 1386 so the Genoese could not reuse them, though the bailey and lower ramparts still stand and make an easy walk. The village quay is small, eight or ten boats stern-to on their own anchor, and it books up in season, so if your agent has not held a slot, drop into Avlaki Bay a mile east on 4-6 m sand, safe in anything short of a hard northeasterly. The pastitsada up in the plane-shaded squares, cinnamon-spiced beef over bucatini, is the plate to find. If you have the legs, the path up Mount Pantokrator reaches the 906-metre summit and its monastery, with the view running clear across to Albania and back over Corfu.
thingsToDo: Explore the Byzantine castle ruins
Swim off pebbly Bataria Beach
Hike the Mount Pantokrator trail
Pastitsada beef over bucatini in the old village
Sundowner at the Avlaki beach taverna
12.mooringTip: Kassiopi's harbour is small and worth pre-booking in summer, which charter agents usually handle. If there is no slot, the standard overflow is Avlaki Bay a mile east, anchored on sand in 4-6 m and sheltered from the north.

13.shortDescription: A short twelve miles south back to Gouvia, with an optional swing round to Paleokastritsa, and a last night in Corfu's UNESCO old town.
13.description: The last passage is a kind one, just twelve miles down the east coast into Gouvia, which leaves most of the day free. If the weather holds and you fancy it, the run round the northwest tip to Paleokastritsa is worth half a day: limestone coves at Agios Spyridon and the cliff climb to the Theotokos monastery, on the headland where Homer wrecked Odysseus among the Phaeacians. If not, drop straight south, berth by mid-afternoon and take a taxi across to the old town for the evening. The Liston arcades, French-built under Napoleon in 1807, run along the Spianada opposite the Old Fortress; take a table on that side for sofrito, veal braised in white wine and garlic, and finish with a kumquat liqueur from one of the shops on N. Theotoki. The fortress sound-and-light show starts around nine in summer.
thingsToDo: Optional cliff-coast swim at Paleokastritsa
Stroll the Liston under the arcades
Tour the Old Fortress at sunset
Sofrito veal on the Liston
Kumquat liqueur from the Old Port
13.mooringTip: Gouvia is the usual last-night berth, with full services, fuel alongside and a taxi rank for the handover run. For dinner ashore instead, the NAOK quay opposite the Old Fortress is a short-stay option, but there is no staying the night there.

14.shortDescription: The last morning at Gouvia: hand the boat back, then a quick taxi into Corfu Town for cappuccino under the Liston before the airport.
14.description: Handing the boat back at Gouvia goes smoothly when the prep is done: refuelling slot reserved the evening before, a cleaning window agreed with the base manager, paperwork settled in the office. The technical check wants you alongside by nine on Saturday. Spare hours afterwards are well spent in Corfu Town, a quarter-hour by taxi. On summer Sundays the Spianada — the Balkans' biggest public square — stages cricket beneath the Old Fortress, a quirk that outlived British rule. Inside the palace of St Michael and St George, the Asian Art Museum keeps the country's richest trove of Chinese and Indian pieces. For coffee and loukoumades under the Liston arcades, nobody does it better. Grab a cake of olive-oil soap before you head back to the marina; from there the airport gate is a twenty-minute taxi ride.
thingsToDo: Cappuccino under the Liston arcades
Watch cricket on the Spianada
Visit the Corfu Asian Art Museum
Buy olive-oil soap as a parting gift
Pack and clean for hand-back
14.mooringTip: This is a Gouvia handover: confirm your fuel slot and cleaning time with the base manager the evening before, and have the boat back by 09:00 Saturday for the technical check. The airport is about twenty minutes by taxi from the marina gate.

corfu-paxos-parga.metaTitle: 7-Day Ionian Loop: Corfu, Paxos & Parga | Boat4You
corfu-paxos-parga.metaDesc: A 7-day yacht charter from Corfu south to Paxos and Antipaxos, then the mainland coast at Parga, Sivota and Plataria before the sail back.

1.shortDescription: The long 30-mile run south from Gouvia to Gaios, Paxos's snug capital tucked behind two wooded islets on a natural channel.
1.description: This is the longest leg of the week, so we usually slip the lines at Gouvia by nine. The maistro, the Ionian's afternoon northwesterly, builds after lunch and sits on the quarter as you run south, which makes the miles pass easily under sail. Gaios waits behind Panagia and Agios Nikolaos, two green islets that turn the approach into a sheltered channel rather than an open harbour. Berth stern-to along the town quay if there's room; the pastel houses and the old Venetian square are a two-minute walk from the passerelle. Paxos lives on its olive oil, and the local ladotyri, a cheese matured in oil, is worth hunting down at a quayside taverna. Arrive early. The quay is short and fills fast in July.
thingsToDo: Walk the Venetian square in Gaios
Dinghy to Panagia islet chapel
Buy ladotyri cheese along the quay
Sunset from the Agios Nikolaos fort
1.mooringTip: The Gaios quay is stern-to with laid lines but short, so aim to be alongside by mid-afternoon before the day boats return. If it's full, Lakka's big northern bay or the quay at Loggos both make calm alternatives.

2.shortDescription: A three-mile skip south to Antipaxos, whose two beaches, Vrika and Voutoumi, hold some of the clearest water in the Ionian.
2.description: Barely an hour under engine or a lazy morning beat, Antipaxos is really a day trip rather than a destination in its own right. There's no harbour, so we anchor off Vrika or Voutoumi on clean sand in four to six metres and swim before the tripper boats arrive from Gaios around eleven. The water here has a pale, backlit quality that photographs rarely catch. Behind the beaches the island is one big vineyard; the Paxiots have made the amber Verdea wine here for generations, and a jug of it at the taverna above Voutoumi rounds off the swim nicely. Come early or linger late to have the bay to yourself. When the anchorage empties in the afternoon, it's a short sail back to Paxos for the night.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim off Vrika beach
Walk the path to Voutoumi cove
Verdea wine at Bella Vista taverna
Snorkel the pale turquoise shallows
2.mooringTip: Antipaxos is a daytime anchorage only, with good holding on sand in four to six metres off either beach. Set the hook well; the afternoon breeze can swing you, and there's nowhere to overnight, so plan to be back on Paxos by dusk.

3.shortDescription: An easy twelve-mile reach east to the mainland and Parga, a tumble of pastel houses under a sixteenth-century Venetian castle.
3.description: The crossing to Parga is one of the prettier legs of the trip, a twelve-mile reach with the mainland mountains growing ahead. The town stacks up a steep bowl of a bay, all ochre and terracotta, crowned by the Venetian fortress the locals call the Kastro. Climb it in the late afternoon when the heat drops; the ramparts give you the whole coastline and the island chapel of Panagia sitting just offshore. Over the headland lies Valtos, a long sweep of sand that stays swimmable into the evening. If you have a spare morning, the Acheron springs and the ancient oracle of the dead are a worthwhile shore trip inland. Dinner is unfussy and fish-led here; a plate of saganaki and grilled fresh catch at a harbour table does the job.
thingsToDo: Climb the Kastro Venetian fortress
Swim the long sands of Valtos
Boat trip to the Acheron springs
Grilled catch at a harbour taverna
3.mooringTip: Parga has no marina and the town quay is short-stay only, so most crews overnight at anchor. Valtos Bay to the west or Lichnos Bay to the east both hold well on sand in five to seven metres; pick whichever is sheltered from the afternoon breeze.

4.shortDescription: Sivota is only a short hop north, its cluster of pine-topped islets sheltering the pale sandbar water everyone photographs.
4.description: Sivota is a gentle morning's sail up the coast, and the payoff is one of the sweetest anchorages on the mainland. A knot of low, pine-covered islets sits just off the village, and the shallows between them glow that improbable milky blue. We usually anchor off Bella Vraka, the sandbar you can wade across at knee depth between two of the islets, and spend the middle of the day swimming and messing about with the paddleboards. The village itself is small and low-key, a curve of quay lined with fish tavernas. Order the bourdeto if it's on, a peppery tomato stew of scorpionfish that the whole region does well. For the night, either take a stern-to berth on the village quay or tuck into one of the calmer bays a little further in.
thingsToDo: Wade the Bella Vraka sandbar
Anchor in the islet-sheltered lagoon
Paddleboard between the pine islets
Bourdeto fish stew on the quay
4.mooringTip: You can lie stern-to on the Sivota village quay, though it fills in season, so arrive by mid-afternoon or plan to anchor. The lagoon between the islets is a swim stop rather than an overnight; choose a deeper, better-protected cove once the day boats leave.

5.shortDescription: Eight quiet miles north brings Plataria, a working fishing bay near Igoumenitsa that most charter crews sail straight past.
5.description: After the crowds at Sivota, Plataria feels like a return to the ordinary Greece, and that's the appeal. It's a short leg north, a working fishing village a few miles short of the Igoumenitsa ferry port, with a bay that opens west toward Corfu and takes the afternoon northwesterly kindly. Not much happens here, which is the point after a busy few days. There's a small marina and a length of town quay, and the tavernas along the front are family-run and priced for locals rather than yachts. Have the fasolada, the slow-cooked bean soup that counts as Greece's everyday national dish, and watch the fishermen sort their nets. It also sets you up well for tomorrow: the sail back across to Corfu is best done in the morning calm, so an early, quiet night suits.
thingsToDo: Fasolada bean soup at a family taverna
Walk the quiet olive-grove hills
Watch the fishermen mend their nets
Sundowner looking west to Corfu
5.mooringTip: Plataria has a small marina alongside the town quay, and both are calm in the prevailing northwesterly, but berths are limited. In July and August it pays to call ahead or arrive early for a stern-to spot.

6.shortDescription: The eighteen-mile crossing back to Corfu, timed for the morning calm, finishing under the ramparts of the UNESCO old town.
6.description: Get this crossing done early. The channel back to Corfu is straightforward, but the northwesterly can kick up a short, awkward chop by afternoon, so a nine-o'clock departure keeps it comfortable. Leave the boat at Gouvia and take the short bus or taxi into Corfu Town for the last night, because the old town earns it. The two Venetian fortresses, the French-built Liston arcade with its cafe tables, and the wide green sweep of the Spianada are all a short walk apart. Corfu's kitchen is its own thing, shaped by centuries of Venetian rule: try sofrito, thin veal in a garlic and white-wine sauce, or the tomato-rich pastitsada. Finish with the local kumquat liqueur. Crews with an extra day often add the west-coast coves at Paleokastritsa, which is worth the detour if the schedule allows.
thingsToDo: Walk the Old Fortress ramparts
Coffee under the Liston arcades
Sofrito veal in the old town
Kumquat liqueur tasting after dinner
6.mooringTip: Gouvia Marina is the base and where the boat is handed back, so book your return berth in advance. The Corfu Town quays are short-stay and exposed to wash, better for a lunch stop than a night.

7.shortDescription: Handover morning at Gouvia, with time for a last espresso in town and cricket on the Spianada if the flight allows.
7.description: Checkout at Gouvia is usually mid-morning, so the useful work happens the night before: top up the fuel, run the boat clean, and pack what you can. That leaves a slow last morning. If there's time before the transfer, it's worth a final walk into Corfu Town for a coffee at the old Café Liston under the arcades, where the tables spill onto the Spianada. On summer weekends you might catch a cricket match on the green, a leftover from the island's spell under British rule and a properly odd thing to watch in the Ionian. The covered market is the place for a jar of Greek thyme honey to carry home. Then it's back to the marina, the paperwork, and the handover. The northern Ionian tends to pull crews back for a second season.
thingsToDo: Espresso at Café Liston
Watch cricket on the Spianada green
Buy thyme honey at the market
Fuel, clean and pack for handover
7.mooringTip: This is a Gouvia Marina handover, so sort the fuel top-up and the cleaning slot the evening before rather than the morning. Confirm your checkout time with the base and leave a margin for the fuel-dock queue in high season.

kos-14-days.metaDesc: Round-trip from Kos through Kalymnos, Patmos, Astypalaia, Nisyros, Rhodes and Symi: two weeks across the wilder, quieter islands of the Dodecanese.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 15-mile hop north to get the crew settled, with sponge-diving history in Pothia and limestone cliffs across the water.
1.description: The first leg is short on purpose. Fifteen miles due north gives everyone time to find the winch handles and remember which line does what before the wind builds in the afternoon. Pothia, on the south coast, is the working town most crews tie up in: sponge boats, a decent fish market, and the Sponge Diving Museum tucked back from the quay. Look west and you'll see Telendos rising out of the channel, its climbing routes drawing people from all over Europe each October. If you fancy a swim rather than a town night, Vathy on the east coast folds back into a narrow green inlet that stays flat in almost any breeze. We usually settle for an ouzeri table and a plate of mououri, the local stuffed squid, before an early night.
thingsToDo: Tour the Sponge Diving Museum in Pothia
Swim the sheltered green inlet at Vathy
Watch climbers on Telendos across the channel
Order mououri stuffed squid at an ouzeri
1.mooringTip: Go stern-to on Pothia quay, roughly €30–50 a night; the harbour fills with local boats so aim to arrive by mid-afternoon. For a quiet night at anchor instead, Vathy's inlet on the east side holds well in most conditions.

2.shortDescription: Twelve miles up to Leros, choosing between the vast sheltered bowl of Lakki and the pastel quay at Agia Marina.
2.description: Leros gives you two very different overnights, and the wind usually makes the choice for you. Lakki, on the south side, is one of the biggest natural harbours in the Mediterranean, a huge protected basin lined with 1930s Italian Art Deco buildings that feel oddly grand for a small island. It holds flat even when the meltemi is honking. Agia Marina, on the north coast, is prettier at the waterfront but takes the north wind on the nose once it gets above twenty knots. Ashore, the climb up to Panteli castle repays the effort with a view over both harbours at once. Down at Xerokambos there's an easy swim beach on the south coast. Order pitaroudia, the local chickpea fritters, at a quay taverna and you'll understand why crews linger here.
thingsToDo: Walk the 1930s Art Deco quay at Lakki
Climb to Panteli Castle for the view
Swim the south-coast sand at Xerokambos
Try pitaroudia chickpea fritters at a taverna
2.mooringTip: Lakki Marina takes you stern-to on lazy lines for around €40–60 and stays calm in almost anything. Agia Marina's town quay is cheaper at €25–40 but gets rolly once the north wind pushes past twenty knots, so read the forecast before committing.

3.shortDescription: A short run north to Patmos, where the road up to Chora climbs past the Cave of the Apocalypse to a UNESCO monastery.
3.description: Patmos carries its history lightly for such a holy place. Skala, the port, has a long quay with good shelter from the north, so most crews tie up there and then head uphill. The road to Chora passes the Cave of the Apocalypse, where St John is said to have received his visions, before reaching the fortress-like Monastery of St John, an eleventh-century UNESCO site that still functions as a working community. The whitewashed lanes of Chora reward an hour of aimless wandering, ideally with a mastiha cocktail at the end of it. If the crew wants sand, Psili Ammos on the south coast is a golden-sand cove reached on foot or by tender. Evenings on the quay are quiet and unhurried, which is exactly the point of coming this far.
thingsToDo: Visit the Cave of the Apocalypse
Tour the 11th-century Monastery of St John
Wander the white lanes of Chora
Tender to golden-sand Psili Ammos beach
3.mooringTip: Skala's quay is long and well protected from the north, with stern-to berths around €30–50. Space is rarely a problem, but tie up before the afternoon ferries start their shuffle if you want a settled spot.

4.shortDescription: A longer 24-mile reach up to the old pirate islands of Fourni, well off the usual charter track and all the better for it.
4.description: This is the leg that takes you out of the crowds. Fourni sits in the channel between Ikaria and Samos, a scatter of small islands that once sheltered pirates and now shelters almost nobody. Twenty-four miles northwest brings you to Fourni Korseon, the one harbour with a proper quay and good cover from the north. Few charter boats push this far, so the waterfront still runs on its own rhythm: fishermen mending nets, one or two tavernas, a craftsman or two carving wooden boat models. Ashore, the walk up to the Christos chapel gives you the whole archipelago laid out below. Petrokopio, reachable only from the water, is worth the tender ride for a swim. In the evening, order astakomakaronada, the lobster pasta the islands are known for, and let the day slow right down.
thingsToDo: Hike to the Christos chapel viewpoint
Tender to Petrokopio Bay for a swim
Eat astakomakaronada lobster pasta at Kambi
Buy a hand-carved wooden boat model
4.mooringTip: Fourni Korseon is the only practical overnight, stern-to on the quay for about €20–30 and sheltered from the north. If it's full or you want to swim off the boat, drop the hook on sand in Kambi Bay in four to six metres.

5.shortDescription: Back south to little Lipsi, an island of seven hundred people, one bakery and a snorkel bay a couple of miles east.
5.description: Lipsi runs at a walking pace and asks you to do the same. Around seven hundred people live here, served by a single bakery and a square with one church clock tower, and the waterfront tavernas are the sort where the owner cooks what the boats brought in that morning. The town quay is the only place to tie up, stern-to and well covered from the north. Come morning, it's worth motoring the two miles east to Aspronissi, where the water over the sand turns an almost unreal blue and the snorkelling is the best on the route so far. Back in town, ask for lipsiako cheese drizzled with thyme honey, and grab a bag of fresh koulouria from the bakery before the day-boats clear the shelves. There is nothing to rush toward, and that's the appeal.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the blue water at Aspronissi Bay
Walk out to the Panagia chapel rock
Try lipsiako cheese with thyme honey
Buy fresh koulouria from the village bakery
5.mooringTip: The town quay is your only berth here, stern-to for a modest €15–25 and sheltered from the north. It's small, so arrive early in season; if the swim is calling, anchor on sand off Aspronissi Bay instead.

6.shortDescription: A 22-mile crossing west to Levitha, home to six people, one taverna and the darkest sky you'll find in the Aegean.
6.description: Levitha is as remote as this trip gets. Six people live on the whole island, there's no ferry, no ATM and no shop, and one goat-herding family runs the single taverna above the anchorage. You come here for the emptiness. Ammoudi Bay bites deep into the south side and holds you on sand in five to seven metres, tucked well out of the north wind, and by evening yours may be one of only a handful of boats swinging on the hook. Walk up to the farm, lend a hand with the goats if the family will have you, and settle in for astakomakaronada cooked by the same people who caught the lobster. Then stay on deck after dinner. With no lights for miles, the star field overhead is the reason skippers remember this night for years.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in Ammoudi Bay
Help the family herd their goats
Eat lobster pasta at the island's only taverna
Stargaze on deck with zero light pollution
6.mooringTip: There's no charge and no marina here: anchor on sand in Ammoudi Bay in five to seven metres, fully sheltered from the north. Bring enough cash and provisions, because there is no ATM and no shop on the island.

7.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles south to Astypalaia, the westernmost of the Dodecanese, its hilltop crowned by the Venetian Querini castle above Pera Gialos harbour.
7.description: Astypalaia marks the far western corner of the Dodecanese, and it feels more Cycladic than its neighbours, all white cubes and blue domes. The overnight is Pera Gialos, the harbour below the kastro on the eastern side, sheltered from the north and within walking distance of everything worth seeing. Above it sits the Querini castle, a Venetian fortress the whole town seems to lean into; the climb up through Chora at dusk is the thing to do here. For a swim, Vatses on the south coast mixes sand and pebble under low cliffs. Food-wise, the drive out to Kaminakia taverna for ladenia, the island's tomato and onion flatbread, is a small pilgrimage crews rarely regret. History buffs can hunt down the Roman mosaics near Mesa Nisi. It's a satisfying halfway marker before the run to the volcano.
thingsToDo: Climb through Chora to the Querini castle
Swim the sand and pebble at Vatses
Eat ladenia flatbread at Kaminakia Taverna
Find the Roman mosaics near Mesa Nisi
7.mooringTip: Pera Gialos takes you stern-to for around €25–40, tucked in below the castle and protected from the north. If the harbour is busy or the wind swings, Maltezana on the south coast is a calm anchoring alternative.

8.shortDescription: A long 38-mile beam reach southeast to Nisyros, an active volcano you can walk right down into on the caldera floor.
8.description: Today is the longest single hop of the fortnight, thirty-eight miles on a beam reach if the meltemi cooperates, so we usually slip the lines by nine. Nisyros is the payoff: a genuinely active volcano, and the walk across the caldera floor to the Stefanos crater is unlike anything else on this route. You descend into a wide grey bowl that hisses steam and smells of sulphur, with the crust crackling underfoot. Pali, on the north coast, is the harbour to aim for, sheltered from the north and an easy base for the trip inland. Wander the cobbled lanes of Mandraki afterwards, or soak tired legs in the Loutra thermal springs. Pick up a jar of the island's capers on the way back to the boat; grown in volcanic soil, they carry a punch you won't forget in a hurry.
thingsToDo: Walk into the steaming Stefanos crater
Wander the cobbled lanes of Mandraki
Soak in the Loutra thermal springs
Buy volcanic-soil capers to take home
8.mooringTip: Pali on the north coast is the settled choice, stern-to for about €25–35 and sheltered from the north. Mandraki on the west side is closer to the sights but smaller and exposed when the wind has any northwest in it.

9.shortDescription: Fifteen gentle miles eastward brings Tilos, the first island in Greece to go zero-waste, where a deserted hamlet reopens each evening as a taverna.
9.description: Tilos wears its green credentials openly: it runs on solar and wind, and plastic bags have been banned here since 2018, making it the first island in Greece to go zero-waste. After the volcano, the fifteen miles east feel like a gentle recovery leg. Livadia, on the east coast, is the harbour, sheltered from the north and quiet in the evenings. The set-piece here is Mikro Chorio, a nineteenth-century village that emptied out decades ago and now flickers back to life on summer nights as a single hilltop taverna and bar among the ruins. Go up for a late drink and the atmosphere does the rest. By day, the hike to the Agios Panteleimon monastery is the classic walk, and the island's small wineries are turning out organic bottles worth a tasting. Try mizithra cheese with thyme honey at a village kafeneio.
thingsToDo: Drink among the ruins at Mikro Chorio
Hike to Agios Panteleimon monastery
Taste Tilos organic wine
Try mizithra cheese with thyme honey
9.mooringTip: Most crews tie up stern-to at Livadia on the eastern shore, around €20–30 a night and tucked away from northerlies. Prefer to lie at anchor and swim? Head for Eristos Bay, a wide sandy sweep on the south coast.

10.shortDescription: Fifteen miles south to car-free Chalki, three hundred residents, a knights' castle on the ridge and neon-blue snorkelling a mile east.
10.description: Chalki is tiny and almost entirely car-free, home to some three hundred people who live around the horseshoe of Emporio harbour. The mansions ringing the bay were built by nineteenth-century sponge merchants, and the whole waterfront has been gently restored rather than rebuilt. Tie up stern-to on the Emporio quay, sheltered from the north. A short walk or motor east brings you to Pontamos, a cove where the water glows an almost artificial blue over pale sand, and the snorkelling there is the reason many crews divert to Chalki at all. Ashore, the climb up to the old Knights' Castle on the ridge gives you Rhodes on the horizon. For dinner, seek out pougia, the local sun-dried octopus, at Taverna Giorgos, and finish with a shot of souma, the fig spirit distilled by Yiannis just up the lane.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the neon-blue water at Pontamos Cove
Climb to the Knights' Castle on the ridge
Eat pougia sun-dried octopus at Taverna Giorgos
Taste souma fig spirit from Yiannis
10.mooringTip: Emporio quay is your berth, stern-to for around €20–30 and sheltered from the north. Space is limited on this small island, so if you'd sooner swim, anchor on sand off Pontamos in four to six metres.

11.shortDescription: Thirty-two miles east to Rhodes and its walled medieval old town, the largest in Europe, with a swim at Anthony Quinn Bay en route.
11.description: The scenery changes gear at Rhodes. Thirty-two miles east brings you to Mandraki, the marina right under the medieval city, and it's worth breaking the passage with a swim at Anthony Quinn Bay just to the south, where the pines run down to the water. The old town itself is the largest inhabited medieval town in Europe, a UNESCO-listed maze inside massive walls. Walk the Street of the Knights up to the Palace of the Grand Master, then let yourself get lost in the back lanes. When the day-trippers thin out in the evening, the courtyards come into their own; the tucked-away garden bar at Socratous is a good place to end up. After a week of near-empty islands, the buzz of a real city is a jolt, though a welcome one for most crews.
thingsToDo: Walk the Street of the Knights
Tour the Palace of the Grand Master
Swim at pine-fringed Anthony Quinn Bay
Find the garden bar at Socratous
11.mooringTip: Mandraki Marina puts you stern-to on lazy lines for €60–90, right under the walls. Top up the fuel at the entrance berth before you moor, and reckon on the marina being busy in high summer.

12.shortDescription: Twenty-four miles up to Symi, its tiers of ochre and peach mansions rising straight off the harbour in the Dodecanese's postcard image.
12.description: Symi is the showstopper of the Dodecanese, and it knows it. The harbour at Gialos climbs the hillsides in tiers of ochre, peach and faded blue mansions, most of them built on the wealth of the old sponge trade. Getting a berth is the catch: the inner quay is small and fills fast, so in peak season we push to arrive by four in the afternoon, or take the easy anchoring option in Pedi Bay a couple of miles east instead. Once you're settled, the town is all steps and cats and jewellers. Buy a natural sponge straight off a harbour stall, order the sweet little Symian shrimp fried whole at a quayside taverna, and if there's time, take the tender round to Nanou for a swim you can only reach from the water. The Panormitis monastery down the coast rewards the detour.
thingsToDo: Buy a natural sponge from a harbour stall
Eat whole-fried Symian shrimp at the quay
Tender to Nanou Beach for a swim
Visit the 18th-century Panormitis Monastery
12.mooringTip: The inner quay is stern-to for €30–50 but tight, and it's usually full by around four in the afternoon in season, so arrive early. If it's gone, anchor on sand in Pedi Bay two miles east in five to seven metres.

13.shortDescription: A long final passage of thirty-eight miles back to Kos, ending under a plane tree that's stood for well over two thousand years.
13.description: The last real sailing day is a long one, thirty-eight miles northwest on what's usually a comfortable beam reach back to Kos. Kos Marina is the biggest charter base in the Dodecanese, with full services and a fuel berth, so top up before you tie up to save hassle in the morning. The town rewards a final wander: the ancient healing sanctuary of the Asklepion sits on the hillside above, and Therma Beach to the east has a natural hot spring that runs straight into the sea. For the farewell dinner, the square at Platia Platanou is the place, where you eat kleftiko, slow-cooked lamb, beneath a plane tree said to be around two and a half thousand years old. Give the boat a clean tonight; it makes the handover tomorrow far quicker.
thingsToDo: Walk the ancient Asklepion healing sanctuary
Swim the hot spring at Therma Beach
Eat kleftiko lamb on Platia Platanou
Refuel and clean the boat at the marina
13.mooringTip: Kos Marina is stern-to on lazy lines for €60–90, with a fuel berth at the entrance to fill before you moor. Confirm your handover slot with the base a day ahead so the morning runs smoothly.

14.shortDescription: Handover before nine, a last swim at Tigaki if there's time, and a twenty-five-minute run to Kos airport.
14.description: Disembarkation days tend to feel a little flat, but Kos makes the ending easy. The base wants the boat back before nine, so it pays to have bags packed and the deck tidy the night before. The handover itself is straightforward: you walk the boat with the base manager, they check her over, and the security deposit is normally released within seven days once everything is signed off. If your flight is later in the day, there's time for one last swim; Tigaki, a long shallow sand beach on the north coast, is the easy choice, or you can linger over a frappé at a café in Kos town and watch the next crews arrive. The airport is only about twenty-five minutes away by road, so there's no need to rush the last coffee. Two weeks and a full circle of the Dodecanese behind you.
thingsToDo: Walk the boat with the base manager
Take a last swim at Tigaki Beach
Have a frappé in Kos town
Transfer to Kos airport, 25 minutes away
14.mooringTip: Hand the boat back at Kos Marina before nine, then let the base run the inspection. The deposit usually clears within seven days once the checklist is signed off.

kos.metaDesc: Sail from Kos to Leros, Patmos, Lipsi, Arki and Kalymnos on a week-long northern Dodecanese loop of quiet quays and clear anchorages.

1.shortDescription: A 32-mile shake-down up the Kalymnos coast to Leros, where Lakki gives you one of the Mediterranean's biggest natural harbours.
1.description: The first leg runs roughly 32 miles NNW, tracing the pale limestone flank of Kalymnos before Leros opens up ahead. Leave early. The Meltemi here fills in from the north by early afternoon, often 18 to 25 knots by the time you clear the channel, so a nine o'clock departure buys you calmer water. Lakki, on the south coast, is your safe bet for the night. It is one of the largest natural harbours in the Med, ringed by wide boulevards and pale 1930s Italian architecture that feels oddly grand for a small Greek island. If the forecast is soft you can instead tuck into Agia Marina on the north side, with its pastel quay and the castle above. Above 22 knots of northerly, though, Agia Marina turns lumpy and I'd keep south.
thingsToDo: Wander Lakki's Art Deco waterfront boulevards
Hike up to Pandeli Castle viewpoint
Swim at Xerokambos on the south coast
Try pitaroudia chickpea fritters at a taverna
1.mooringTip: Lakki's marina takes you stern-to on lazy lines for about EUR 40 to 60 a night, and it stays flat in almost any weather. The Agia Marina town quay is cheaper at EUR 25 to 40 but gets uncomfortable once the northerly pushes past 22 knots.

2.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles north to Patmos, the pilgrimage island where St John wrote Revelation, with Skala's big sheltered quay for the night.
2.description: A steady 22 miles north brings you to Patmos, which carries more weight than its size suggests. This is where St John is said to have dictated the Book of Revelation in AD 95, and the island has been an Orthodox pilgrimage site ever since. Skala, on the east coast, is the working harbour and the usual berth: a long quay that shrugs off the northerly. From there a single road climbs to Chora, passing the Cave of the Apocalypse and the fortress-like Monastery of St John, both worth the walk up. Go late afternoon once the light softens and the coach crowds thin out. Back down in Chora, the shaded alleys hide a few good bars pouring mastiha cocktails. If you'd rather swim than sightsee, drop the hook in Grikos Bay to the south instead.
thingsToDo: Walk up to the Cave of the Apocalypse
Tour the 11th-century Monastery of St John
Hike the path to Psili Ammos beach
Sip mastiha cocktails in Chora's alleys
2.mooringTip: Berth stern-to along Skala's main quay for EUR 30 to 50 a night; it's roomy and sheltered from the north, so no need to book ahead in most conditions. For a swim-only night, Grikos Bay just south gives good holding on sand.

3.shortDescription: A short 12-mile hop southeast to Lipsi, an unhurried island of 700 people, one bakery and family tavernas around the quay.
3.description: Only 12 miles separate Patmos from Lipsi, so there's no rush to slip lines. Lipsi is the kind of place charter crews come back for: about 700 residents, a single bakery, and a village square built around a church clock tower. The town quay is the only place to tie up, and the tavernas along the front are family-run, the sort where the day's menu depends on what came in that morning. Try the local lipsiako cheese drizzled with thyme honey, and grab a bag of warm koulouria from the bakery for the next morning's passage. Two miles east lies Aspronissi, a shallow bay over pale sand that snorkels beautifully in flat light. Walk out to the little chapel of Panagia tou Charou too; the icon inside carries a well-loved local legend.
thingsToDo: Snorkel Aspronissi Bay two miles east
Visit the Panagia tou Charou chapel
Taste lipsiako cheese with thyme honey
Buy warm koulouria from the village bakery
3.mooringTip: Lipsi's town quay is your only berth, stern-to for a modest EUR 15 to 25 a night and well tucked from the north. If you'd sooner swim, anchor off Aspronissi on sand in 4 to 6 metres and hold there for the afternoon.

4.shortDescription: Just eight miles east to tiny Arki, home to 40 people and one taverna, and the turquoise shallows of Tiganakia Bay.
4.description: This is barely a passage, eight miles of easy sailing east, but the payoff is one of the finest anchorages in the Dodecanese. Arki has around 40 permanent residents, no scheduled ferry, and a single taverna on the harbour front. Come here to slow all the way down. Tiganakia, on the south side, is the reason most people make the detour: a scatter of islets sheltering water so clear and shallow the boat seems to hover over white sand. Anchor, swim, and let the afternoon go. Ashore, Stavento is the only kitchen going, run by a fisherman who lands the catch himself, so the astakomakaronada lobster pasta is about as fresh as it gets. If your legs need a stretch, the footpath up to the hilltop Panagia chapel rewards you with the whole archipelago laid out below.
thingsToDo: Swim the turquoise shallows at Tiganakia Bay
Eat astakomakaronada lobster pasta at Stavento
Hike to the hilltop Panagia chapel
Read on deck to the cicadas
4.mooringTip: Tie stern-to on Arki's small harbour quay for EUR 15 to 20 a night, sheltered from the north. Most crews prefer to anchor in Tiganakia itself, over sand in 3 to 5 metres, for the swim before moving into the port for dinner.

5.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles south to Kalymnos, the sponge-divers' island turned climbing mecca, with busy Pothia harbour and Telendos rising offshore.
5.description: Turn south now for 22 miles to Kalymnos, an island with two very different reputations. For generations its men dived for sponges across the eastern Med, and that history still shapes the place. More recently its sheer limestone has made it a magnet for climbers, who fill the west-coast crags each autumn for the October festival. Pothia, the main port on the south coast, is where you'll spend the night: a noisy, colourful working harbour stacked up the hillside. Across a narrow strait the bare pyramid of Telendos sits a mile offshore, best caught at sunset. If you climb, the sectors at Massouri and Armeos are a short taxi from the quay, and the Grande Grotta cave above them is the island's signature wall. Otherwise, take the dinghy round to Vathy, a green fjord of citrus groves cut into the east coast.
thingsToDo: See the Grande Grotta climbing cave above Massouri
Visit the Sponge Diving Museum in Pothia
Snorkel the narrow inlet at Vathy Bay
Try stuffed squid at a Pothia ouzeri
5.mooringTip: Berth stern-to in Pothia harbour for EUR 30 to 50 a night, sheltered from the north but lively with ferry wash by day. For a calmer, more scenic night, the fjord-like inlet at Vathy on the east coast is the alternative worth the extra miles.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen miles south back to base at Kos Marina, with an optional swim off little Pserimos on the way in.
6.description: The last real sailing day is a gentle 15 miles south back to Kos. Break it halfway at Pserimos if the wind allows: a low, sandy island with good holding in 4 to 6 metres and a sheltered bay for a final long swim. Kos Marina is the biggest charter base in the Dodecanese, with full services, lazy lines, and shelter from every direction, so coming alongside is straightforward. Top up the tanks at the fuel berth by the entrance before you tie up for the night, and confirm your handover slot with the base a day ahead so the morning runs smoothly. With the boat squared away, there's still time for Kos Town: the ancient Asklepion above the harbour, the thermal spring pooling into the sea at Therma, and slow-cooked kleftiko lamb under the old plane tree in the square.
thingsToDo: Swim off Pserimos on the way south
Walk the ancient Asklepion above Kos Town
Bathe in the sea springs at Therma
Eat kleftiko lamb by the Hippocrates plane tree
6.mooringTip: Kos Marina takes you stern-to on lazy lines at EUR 60 to 90 a night with full shelter. Refuel at the fuel berth by the entrance before mooring, and phone the base to confirm your handover time 24 hours out.

7.shortDescription: Handover at Kos Marina before nine, then a last swim at Tigaki before the 25-minute run to the airport.
7.description: The last morning is easy. Have the boat cleared and ready for handover at Kos Marina before nine, when the base manager runs the inspection walk-through. Assuming all is in order, your deposit is released within about a week. If the flight isn't until later, there's no reason to sit on the quay. Kos Town is a short stroll for a proper Greek coffee and a wander round the old harbour, and the long sandy beach at Tigaki, on the north coast, is a fine spot for one last swim before you pack the bags away. Kos airport is only about 25 minutes by road from the marina, so transfers are simple to time. A week among the northern Dodecanese leaves most crews already sketching out where the next charter should go.
thingsToDo: Final inspection with the base manager
Last swim at Tigaki's sandy north coast
Greek coffee at a Kos Town café
Twenty-five-minute transfer to Kos airport
7.mooringTip: Hand the boat back at Kos Marina before 09:00 for the inspection. The deposit is typically returned within seven days once the base has checked her over.

lavrion-kea-kythnos-sifnos.metaDesc: Sail a 7-day round trip from Lavrion through Kea, Kythnos, Sifnos and Serifos: thermal springs, quiet quays and the finest cooking in the Cyclades.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 18-mile reach southeast off the mainland to Kea, your first night either on the free Korissia quay or in sheltered Vourkari.
1.description: The first afternoon out of Lavrion is an easy one: eighteen miles east-southeast across open water to Kea, a warm-up leg to let the crew settle into the boat. Korissia, the main port on the island's northwest corner, offers a free town quay, though it sits open to the north and turns lumpy once the Meltemi climbs past eighteen knots. When that happens we duck a mile north into Vourkari, where holding is better and lazy lines make going stern-to painless. Aim to be tied up by four, because the swell at the Korissia mouth stiffens through the afternoon. Ashore, the seafood ouzeris along the Vourkari waterfront are why most crews stop here at all. Walk up to Ioulida before dinner and lose an hour among its marble lanes.
thingsToDo: See the 6th-century Stone Lion of Kea
Dive the Patris wreck off Koundouros
Octopus and ouzo on the Vourkari front
Wander the marble lanes of Ioulida
1.mooringTip: Korissia's town quay is free but wide open when the Meltemi turns northerly. Above eighteen knots, shift a mile north to Vourkari for lazy-line berths (roughly EUR 25-35) and steadier holding.

2.shortDescription: Twenty downwind miles south to Kythnos, where hot mineral water bubbles straight into Loutra harbour and Kolona's sandbar begs a lunchtime swim.
2.description: With the Meltemi sitting on the quarter, the twenty miles down to Kythnos is the sort of sailing you charter for: a steady following breeze and the boat rolling gently south. Break the passage at Kolona, the thin sand spit that joins the main island to the Agios Loukas islet, and drop the hook for a swim where the water shelves from turquoise to deep blue on either side of the bar. Loutra, over on the northeast coast, is the night's prize. Natural thermal springs run down into the harbour itself, so once you're berthed stern-to you can walk a few metres and sink into water sitting near thirty-eight degrees. Nothing loosens a day's sailing out of your shoulders quite like it. Berths go for around EUR 25-40 with lazy lines, well sheltered from the north.
thingsToDo: Soak in the Loutra thermal springs
Swim the Kolona double-bay sandbar
Ride the Chora-to-Driopida ridge road
Sfougato cheese pie in Driopida
2.mooringTip: Go stern-to with lazy lines inside Loutra's little harbour (EUR 25-40), well protected from the north. If it fills in high season, Merichas on the west coast is the fallback.

3.shortDescription: A fast 28-mile beam reach past Serifos to Sifnos, the Cyclades' kitchen island, anchoring in Kamares before the westerly swell finds it.
3.description: Twenty-eight miles today, a proper beam reach in the standard Meltemi that eats the distance quickly, with Serifos sliding past to starboard. Kamares, the ferry bay on the west coast of Sifnos, is where most crews aim. It shelters well from north and northeast, but the mouth faces west, and by late August, when the Meltemi picks up a westerly slant, swell curls in and the anchorage grows restless. On those nights we move round to Platys Gialos on the south coast and sleep properly. Sifnos earns its name at the table. This is the home of revithada, chickpeas left overnight in a wood oven, and mastelo, lamb slow-cooked in a clay pot with dill and red wine. Book a table up in Artemonas and eat the way the island has eaten for generations.
thingsToDo: Mastelo lamb up in Artemonas
Walk out to Chrysopigi monastery, Faros
Watch potters throwing clay in Vathy
Swim the long sands at Platys Gialos
3.mooringTip: Anchor on sand at five to eight metres in Kamares, or take the short quay stern-to for EUR 20-30. When the wind clocks west and swell rolls in, run round to Platys Gialos on the south coast.

4.shortDescription: Only fourteen miles north to Serifos: a snorkelling stop in Koutalas, then a watchful night at Livadi where the Meltemi funnels down hard.
4.description: A short day, just fourteen miles up the coast, which leaves the morning free. Anchor first at Koutalas on the south coast, where a headland blocks the northerly and the snorkelling over the rocks is worth an hour or two. Livadi, the main harbour, is the overnight, and it deserves respect: the bay acts like a funnel, accelerating the Meltemi as it pours off the hills. The holding on sand is good once you're properly dug in, but if there's one Serifos story every skipper trades, it's dragging out of Livadi in the small hours. Give it plenty of scope, dive down to see the anchor has actually bitten, and hold off sending anyone ashore until you're sure. When you do head up, the donkey path to Chora is half an hour of switchbacks, rewarded by windmills and a long view back over the bay.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the rocks at Koutalas Bay
Climb the donkey path to Chora
Revithada in a Chora kafeneio
Sunset by the Chora windmills
4.mooringTip: Day-anchor at Koutalas on sand in five to seven metres. Overnight in Livadi anchored at five to eight with long scope, or stern-to on the town quay for EUR 20-30; either way, dive the anchor before you relax.

5.shortDescription: Fifteen miles back north to Kythnos, this time to Merichas, with a long lunch swing into sheltered Apokrousi cove along the way.
5.description: Turning north again, fifteen miles brings you back to Kythnos, this time to Merichas on the west coast. It's a homelier stop than Loutra: a modest ferry quay, a row of tavernas run by the families who own them, and a berth that's a good deal simpler to handle than the exposed Kolona anchorage when a blow is on the way. Break for lunch at Apokrousi, a wide sandy cove just to the north that stays calm under a northerly, and swim off the boat before you drop down to the port. Later, walk or catch a lift up to Chora. The climb from Merichas is a lovely bit of back-country road, and the ridge at the top throws open a wide view across the strait. Quiet tavernas, cold wine, an early night.
thingsToDo: Lunch and a swim in Apokrousi cove
Drive the Merichas-to-Chora ridge road
Ridge-top views over the channel from Chora
Sunset drinks on the Merichas quay
5.mooringTip: Berth stern-to on the Merichas town quay (EUR 20-30), sheltered from the northerly Meltemi. It's a calmer, more forgiving night than Kolona if the wind is forecast over twenty-five knots.

6.shortDescription: Homeward at last, twenty-two miles northwest into Lavrion, breaking the run for a final dip below the Poseidon temple that crowns Cape Sounion.
6.description: The run home is twenty-two miles northwest, and it comes with one last treat. Cape Sounion sits on the approach to Lavrion, and the temple of Poseidon stands on the headland above a good anchorage half a mile offshore. Drop the hook on sand in five to eight metres, sheltered from the north, and take a final swim with two and a half thousand years of marble looking down on you. Time it for the middle of the day and you will have the water to yourself before the tour buses arrive. From there it is a short hop into Lavrion. Tie up at the fuel berth on the way in and fill the tanks, so the boat is ready to hand back. Most bases at the Olympic Marina want you alongside by five the evening before checkout.
thingsToDo: Swim below the Temple of Poseidon
Anchor off Cape Sounion at midday
Refuel and rinse down at Lavrion
Farewell dinner on the Lavrion harbourfront
6.mooringTip: Refuel at the entrance berth before you moor, then take a stern-to slot with lazy lines in the Olympic Marina (EUR 60-90). Confirm your handover time a day ahead and aim to be alongside by five the evening before.

7.shortDescription: Hand the boat back before nine, walk the inspection with the base manager, then a last harbour coffee before the fifty-minute run to Athens airport.
7.description: Checkout at Lavrion is painless if you've kept the boat tidy through the week. Handover is usually before nine, so pack the night before and strip the bunks early. Walk the inspection alongside the base manager rather than leaving them to it, and photograph anything that gets logged as damage before you put your name to the form; the deposit should be back in your account inside a week. Then comes the ritual that closes every charter: a slow coffee at one of the kafeneia on the Lavrion front while the crew trades the week's stories. Athens airport is roughly fifty minutes away by road, though Friday traffic can stretch that, so leave a buffer if you're flying out the same day. And tip the dock hands if they looked after you.
thingsToDo: Walk the final inspection with the base manager
Photograph any logged damage before signing
Last coffee on the Lavrion harbourfront
Fifty-minute transfer to Athens airport
7.mooringTip: Hand over before nine after a walk-through with the base manager. Photograph anything noted as damage before you sign, and expect the deposit back within seven days of a clean inspection.

lavrion-milos-sifnos-14-days.metaTitle: 14-Day Cyclades Grand Tour from Lavrion | Boat4You
lavrion-milos-sifnos-14-days.metaDesc: A two-week sail from Lavrion through Andros, Tinos and the Small Cyclades, looping back via Milos and Sifnos. Remote anchorages and real island tavernas.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 18-mile reach east from Lavrion to warm up the crew; tie up at Korissia's free quay or slip north to sheltered Vourkari.
1.description: The first afternoon is really a shake-down: eighteen miles east-southeast, sails up, everyone finding where the winches live. Kea sits closer to the mainland than any other Cycladic island, so it fills with Athenian weekenders, but midweek the quay stays civil. Korissia is the obvious tie-up and costs nothing, though the entrance takes a nasty chop once the Meltemi pushes past twenty knots from the north. When that's the forecast, we duck a mile up to Vourkari instead, where lazy lines and a proper fishing-village ouzeri make the evening easier. Get in before four if you can. Ashore, the walk up to Ioulida rewards you at dusk, when the marble lanes go gold and the tavernas start smoking. Snorkellers should ask a local to point out the Patris wreck off Koundouros.
thingsToDo: Climb to the 6th-century BC Lion of Kea
Snorkel the Patris wreck off Koundouros
Octopus and ouzo at a Vourkari ouzeri
Wander Ioulida's marble lanes after sunset
1.mooringTip: Korissia's town quay is free but rolls badly in a northerly; when the Meltemi tops 18 knots, take the lazy lines at Vourkari (about €25-35) a mile north. Aim to be secured by mid-afternoon before the entrance swell builds.

2.shortDescription: A longer 32-mile leg northeast to Andros, the greenest of the Cyclades; Gavrio's roomy harbour makes an easy, well-sheltered stern-to.
2.description: Thirty-two miles today, so we like to slip the lines by nine and let the morning wind do the work before the Meltemi hardens up. Andros surprises people. Where most Cycladic islands are bald and burnt, this one runs with water: streams, old stone footbridges, even a waterfall or two up in the hills. Gavrio on the west coast is where the charter fleet ties up, a big easy harbour that shrugs off wind from any quarter. The island's real pleasure is inland. The Andros Routes trail network threads past springs and slate-roofed villages, and the hike up to the Pithara falls is worth a whole afternoon. Chora, over on the far side, keeps a good contemporary art museum and marble walkways that stay cool at midday. Order froutalia, the local herb-and-potato omelette, at any village kafeneio.
thingsToDo: Hike inland to the Pithara waterfalls
Walk Chora's marble paths and art museum
Try froutalia omelette at a kafeneio
Swim the north-coast sands at Vitali
2.mooringTip: Stern-to in Gavrio is straightforward and holds well against any wind, roughly €30-50 a night with room to spare even in August. No need to book, but arrive by late afternoon for the calmer inside berths.

3.shortDescription: Just 20 miles southeast, but the Andros-Tinos channel accelerates the Meltemi; the short hop lands you at Greece's marble-carving and pilgrimage island.
3.description: The gap between Andros and Tinos acts like a wind tunnel, so expect the Meltemi to stand up mid-channel even on a mild day. The passage is short, though, and a reefed main handles it comfortably. Tinos wears two faces. Down at the port, pilgrims climb to the church of Panagia Evangelistria, some on their knees, and the waterfront hums accordingly. Head inland and the island goes quiet: more than a hundred ornate dovecotes stud the terraces around Falatados, and the marble workshops of Pyrgos still turn out carvers trained the old way. We usually berth in the new harbour, though the holding is only fair, so lay plenty of chain. When the wind really pipes up, the inner craft basin stays calmer. Save an evening for the boulder-strewn moonscape at Volax.
thingsToDo: Visit a marble-carving workshop in Pyrgos
Walk the dovecote trail above Falatados
Lunch on artichokes à la polita
Sunset among Volax's granite boulders
3.mooringTip: Stern-to in the new harbour runs about €25-40, but the sand holding is moderate, so dig the anchor in well with a long scope. If the forecast tops 25 knots, shift to the calmer inner small-craft basin.

4.shortDescription: The route's longest passage, 38 miles southeast to Donousa, most far-flung of the Small Cyclades, where a hundred people live year-round.
4.description: This is the big jump. Thirty-eight miles across open water into the Small Cyclades, so we're off the dock at first light and watching the barometer. Donousa is the outlier of the group, an island of maybe a hundred permanent souls with room for only a handful of visiting boats. The little harbour at Stavros tucks into the southwest corner and holds you snugly once you're in. Turn up late and it may be full, in which case the bay just outside gives good sand at five to seven metres. The reward is Kedros, a curve of pale sand and improbable turquoise a short walk south, sheltered when the north wind blows. Hikers can push on to the stone hamlet of Kalotaritissa. Come evening, look for gouna, mackerel sun-dried on lines and thrown on the grill.
thingsToDo: Swim the turquoise sand at Kedros
Hike to the Kalotaritissa stone hamlet
Snorkel the Cave of the Wall
Grilled gouna at a beach shack
4.mooringTip: Stavros harbour has only a few stern-to slots (around €15-25) with no lazy lines, so plan to anchor in the bay outside on sand at 5-7 metres if you arrive after mid-afternoon. Both spots shelter you from the northerly Meltemi.

5.shortDescription: A short 15-mile run southeast to Amorgos, where the white Hozoviotissa monastery clings to an ochre cliff and 300 steps lead up to it.
5.description: After yesterday's slog, fifteen easy miles feel like a gift. Amorgos rises steep and dramatic, the island Luc Besson used for The Big Blue, and its signature sight is the monastery of Hozoviotissa: a slash of whitewash pressed into the rock face, three hundred steps above the road, founded back in 1088. Bring covered shoulders and knees or you won't get past the monk at the door. Where you tie up depends on the wind. Aegiali in the north gives the better lee under a standard Meltemi, while Katapola down south comes into its own when the breeze swings around. Divers with a card aboard can drop onto the Olympia wreck off Liveros. In the hill village of Tholaria we settle in for slow-cooked goat and a glass of psimeni raki, the local spiced spirit.
thingsToDo: Climb 300 steps to Hozoviotissa monastery
Dive the Olympia wreck off Liveros
Slow-cooked goat and raki in Tholaria
Walk the Aegiali-Tholaria ridge path
5.mooringTip: Aegiali stern-to (roughly €25-40) sits in the lee of a northerly; switch to Katapola on the south coast if the wind clocks round. Charter berths are few, so confirm a slot ahead in peak weeks.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen miles west-southwest to Koufonissi, the busiest and most polished of the Small Cyclades, where the fishing fleet still lands the day's lobster.
6.description: Koufonissi is the one that's caught on. It's the largest of the inhabited Small Cyclades and, over the past decade, the smart-money anchorage of the cluster, so the harbour at Pano Koufonissi can feel busy by yacht standards. Get there early and it's still charming: a low white village, caïques nosing in with the morning catch, water so clear the keel throws a shadow on the sand. The swimming is the draw. Off the east end the shallows glow that unreal turquoise, and the uninhabited islet of Kato Koufonissi across the channel makes a fine lunchtime anchor with a single taverna ashore. Kayakers poke into the sea caves along the southeast cliffs. At dinner, order astakomakaronada, the island's lobster spaghetti, landed that morning by the same boats you watched come in.
thingsToDo: Lunch anchor and swim off Kato Koufonissi
Kayak the southeast sea caves
Astakomakaronada lobster spaghetti at the port
Walk Pano Koufonissi's whitewashed lanes
6.mooringTip: Harbour berths at Pano Koufonissi (about €25-40) fill by five in high season, so plan an early arrival. If it's full, anchor on sand at 4-7 metres east of the village, well tucked from the northerly.

7.shortDescription: Just five miles west sits Schoinousa, sleepiest of the Small Cyclades, its port tucked into Mersini while the clifftop Chora gazes down on sandy Tsigouri.
7.description: Barely five miles today, so there's no rush and no drama. Schoinousa is the smallest inhabited island in the group, home to a couple of hundred people, and it wears its quiet lightly. Boats tie up at little Mersini at the foot of the hill; the village of Chora sits above, a ten-minute climb that sorts the walkers from the loungers. Once you're up there, whitewash, a spine of a main street, and views back down over the anchored fleet. Tsigouri is the beach to aim for, shallow and sandy and sheltered from the north, an easy stroll from the port. Lunch is unfussy: tomato keftedes, a jug of the house red, a tamarisk for shade. If your legs are willing, the path out to the Panagia church rewards the effort with the island's widest view.
thingsToDo: Swim shallow, sheltered Tsigouri beach
Climb to the hilltop Chora village
Tomato keftedes under a tamarisk tree
Walk out to the Panagia church
