7.mooringTip: Mersini has only a few stern-to slots (around €15-25) and no lazy lines; if they're taken, anchor on sand at 4-6 metres just east of the harbour. Both give good shelter from the Meltemi.

8.shortDescription: Seven miles west to Irakleia, a hundred residents and one taverna; this is where the Small Cyclades go about as quiet as Greece gets.
8.description: Seven miles and you've arrived, which leaves the whole day for doing very little. Irakleia rounds out the Small Cyclades and takes the quiet crown: a hundred residents, one road, a single taverna down at the Agios Georgios harbour. The mainstream crowd simply never makes it out this far. Tie up, swim, walk. The cave above the village, the Blue Cave as everyone calls it, is a half-hour scramble uphill and worth a head-torch to see the stalactites properly. Livadi beach holds what may be the clearest water in the group. Up on the ridge, the crumbling kastro dates from the days when pirates worked these channels. Time your visit right and you'll land on a panigiri; the Saint Trinity feast in late August pulls in the whole island and half the boats at anchor.
thingsToDo: Hike to the Blue Cave stalactites
Swim the clear water at Livadi
Grilled gouna at the harbour taverna
Explore the pirate-era kastro ruins
8.mooringTip: Anchor in Agios Georgios bay on sand at 5-7 metres, well sheltered from the northerly Meltemi. The few quay berths (around €15-25) have no lazy lines, so most crews settle for the hook.

9.shortDescription: Thirty-two miles west on a fast beam reach to Folegandros, whose cliff-top Chora ranks among the most photographed in the Cyclades.
9.description: With the Meltemi on the beam this is a fast, exhilarating leg, thirty-two miles of the boat settling into her groove and eating the miles. Folegandros makes you work for its best bit. Karavostasi, the only real harbour, is a modest affair down at sea level, fine in a northerly and no place to be in a southerly. The town is another matter. Chora perches on the cliff edge a stiff kalderimi climb above the port, or a short taxi if the legs have had enough, and the walk out from the square to the Panagia church runs right along the drop with the southern Cyclades laid out behind you. Katergo, a boat-only cove, is the swim to save for the tender. At dinner, matsata, hand-rolled pasta under a goat ragù, is the plate to order.
thingsToDo: Cliff-edge walk from Chora to Panagia
Tender across to boat-only Katergo cove
Matsata pasta with goat ragù
Follow the kalderimi to Ano Meria
9.mooringTip: Karavostasi is small and best in a northerly only; stern-to runs about €25-40. If the quay's full, anchor east of the port on sand at 4-7 metres, and stay away entirely when the wind has any south in it.

10.shortDescription: Twenty miles northwest to Milos, where the long inlet to Adamas gives the calmest overnight in the western Cyclades whatever the wind does.
10.description: Twenty miles northwest and the character of the trip changes again. Milos is a drowned volcano, its crater flooded to form a huge natural harbour, and once you've motored up the long inlet to Adamas the water goes glassy no matter what's blowing outside. It's one of the surest overnights in the western islands. On the way in, if the sea is kind, drop the hook off Sarakiniko for a swim; the white volcanic rock there looks scoured smooth, closer to the moon than the Aegean, though the anchorage only works in calm air. Ashore, the old fishermen's syrmata at Klima glow in every colour at sunset, boat-garages built right at the waterline. Climb to the castle at Plaka for the long view, and set aside snorkel time for the Papafragas caves.
thingsToDo: Calm-day swim below the Sarakiniko cliffs
Snorkel the Papafragas sea caves
Climb to Plaka castle for the view
Photograph the coloured syrmata at Klima
10.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Adamas town quay (about €30-50) is protected from any wind direction, the best shelter for miles. Pollonia in the northeast is cheaper but takes the swell in a northerly.

11.shortDescription: Eighteen miles north to Sifnos, the Cyclades' kitchen, where you choose between the sheltered inlet of Vathi and the larger quay at Kamares.
11.description: Eighteen miles north brings you to the island Greeks send you to when the subject turns to food. Sifnos gave the country its most famous cookbook writer, and the cooking here still runs deep: mastelo, lamb or goat sealed in a clay pot with wine and dill, and revithada, chickpeas left overnight in the wood oven and eaten Sunday. Two anchorages compete for you. Kamares on the west is the bigger quay and the ferry port; Vathi, tucked into an almost land-locked inlet on the south coast, is the calmer swim, sand underfoot and salt flats behind the beach. When the Meltemi carries any west in it, we take Vathi every time. Walk the coast path at Faros to the little Chrysopigi monastery on its rock, and buy a piece of the island's celebrated pottery before you leave.
thingsToDo: Coast walk to Chrysopigi monastery at Faros
Mastelo lamb in clay pots at Artemonas
Visit a pottery workshop in Vathy
Swim the sheltered inlet at Vathi
11.mooringTip: In Kamares, anchor on sand at 5-8 metres or take the small quay (€20-30); at Vathi, drop on sand at 4-6 metres for full shelter. Choose Vathi whenever the wind has a westerly slant.

12.shortDescription: A long 32-mile beat north back toward the mainland to Kythnos, with a lunch anchor at Apokrousi and, wind allowing, the Kolona sandbar.
12.description: Today you pay for all that downwind fun. Thirty-two miles north into the teeth of the Meltemi, so we set up for two long port tacks and accept that lunch will be eaten heeled over. Break the beat at Apokrousi, a sandy cove on the west coast of Kythnos that gives a sheltered lee and a swim to reset the crew. Merichas, a little further on, is the practical overnight; it handles a building northerly far better than Loutra round the corner. If the clock and the wind are both generous, the sandbar at Kolona a few miles north is the island's showpiece anchorage, a ribbon of sand joining two bays, and worth every extra mile. Ashore in the old village of Dryopida, ask for sfougato, the local baked cheese-and-egg pie.
thingsToDo: Lunch anchor and swim at Apokrousi cove
Detour to the Kolona double-bay sandbar
Walk the Merichas-Chora ridge road
Sfougato cheese pie in Dryopida
12.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Merichas town quay (about €20-30) shelters you from the northerly; it's a steadier bet than Loutra when the Meltemi is building. For a night at anchor, Kolona's sandbar to the north is unbeatable in settled weather.

13.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles northwest closes the loop to Lavrion, with a final swim beneath the columns of Poseidon's temple at Cape Sounion.
13.description: The last real sailing day, twenty-two miles northwest, and it's worth leaving something in the tank for the finale. Cape Sounion sticks out from the Attic coast just short of Lavrion, and the temple of Poseidon still stands on its headland where sailors have watched for it for two and a half thousand years. Anchor half a mile off in sand at five to eight metres, in the lee of the northerly, and take the crew for one more swim with those columns above the bow. It's the kind of stop nobody forgets. From there it's a short hop into Lavrion. Top up the tanks at the fuel berth by the entrance before you berth, so the boat is ready to hand back in the morning without a scramble. Dinner tonight is on the harbourfront, wherever the grill smells best.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim beneath Poseidon's temple
Refuel at the Lavrion entrance berth
Farewell crew dinner on the harbourfront
Souvenir stroll through Lavrion town
13.mooringTip: At the Olympic Marina, take the lazy lines stern-to (roughly €60-90). Refuel at the entrance berth before you moor, and confirm your handover slot with the base 24 hours ahead.

14.shortDescription: Hand the boat back before nine, walk the inspection with the base manager, and let the deposit clear; Athens airport is under an hour away.
14.description: No sailing today, just the wind-down. Bases like the boat back on the quay by nine, so strip the beds, clear the fridge and square away the gear the night before rather than rushing it half-asleep. Walk the inspection alongside the base manager rather than leaving them to it: point out anything you flagged on handover, and photograph any mark before you sign for it, which keeps the conversation short if a question comes up later. With that done, the deposit typically clears back to your card inside a week. Lavrion is close to Athens airport, about fifty minutes by road, though on a Friday afternoon in season give it ninety to be safe. If there's an hour spare before the transfer, take a last Greek coffee at one of the harbour kafeneia and watch the next crew load their boat.
thingsToDo: Walk the final inspection with the manager
Greek coffee at a harbour kafeneio
Airport transfer, about 50 minutes
Tip the dock crew for good service
14.mooringTip: Have the boat cleared and on the quay by 09:00 for handover. Photograph any noted damage before signing the inspection sheet; the deposit is usually released within seven days.

lavrion-mykonos-paros.metaDesc: Sail a seven-day loop from Lavrion through Kea, Syros, Mykonos, Paros, Serifos and Kythnos, mixing famous harbours with quiet Cycladic anchorages.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 14-mile warm-up from the Lavrion base to Kea, where the fishing quay at Vourkari eases the crew into island time.
1.description: Most crews collect the boat at Lavrion the evening before, sort the paperwork and provisioning, then slip the lines mid-morning. The crossing to Kea runs about three hours on a broad reach when the Meltemi is behaving. Aim for Vourkari, the deep inlet on the island's northwest side, where local fishing boats share the water with charter yachts. Ashore, the waterfront tavernas grill whatever came in that morning. Save an afternoon for the climb up to Ioulida, the amphitheatre-shaped chora that cars can't reach, and the short walk out to the archaic Lion of Kea, cut straight into the bedrock some 2,600 years ago. Kea sits quieter than its famous neighbours to the south, which is rather the point on day one. Get the crew swimming and settled before the harder legs come.
thingsToDo: Grill night at a Vourkari fish taverna
Climb car-free Ioulida for sunset
Walk out to the archaic Lion of Kea
Swim the sheltered inlet before dinner
1.mooringTip: Vourkari gives good holding on sand in 5-7 m; go stern-to on the short quay or drop the hook further into the bay. It shelters from the northerly Meltemi but funnels gusts down off the hillside, so dig the anchor in well.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles southeast to Ermoupolis, the grand neoclassical capital of the Cyclades, where marble squares and a Catholic hill town replace bare island rock.
2.description: Syros surprises first-timers. Instead of sugar-cube villages you get Ermoupolis, a working port city of neoclassical mansions built when this was the richest harbour in Greece. Tie up along the town quay and the marble-paved Miaoulis Square is a two-minute stroll inland, ringed by cafes and the town hall Ernst Ziller designed. Give an afternoon to Ano Syros, the medieval Catholic quarter stacked up the hill behind the port; the lanes are too steep for wheels, and the Saint George cathedral at the top pays you back with the view. Down in the Vaporia district, sea-captains' houses drop straight into the water. Buy loukoumia and soumada to take back aboard. The island keeps a real year-round life, so the tavernas stay honest and the prices sensible.
thingsToDo: Coffee on marble-paved Miaoulis Square
Climb the steep lanes of Ano Syros
See Saint George cathedral at the top
Buy loukoumia and soumada for the boat
2.mooringTip: Ermoupolis has a town quay for stern-to mooring on your own anchor, first-come and often full by late afternoon in July. There is a small marina too; either way the Meltemi can push swell into the harbour, so a long stern line helps.

3.shortDescription: A short 14-mile hop east to Mykonos, trading Syros calm for windmills, Little Venice and the busiest quay in the Cyclades.
3.description: The leg to Mykonos is short, which is handy, because you'll want the extra hours for the harbour scramble. This is the loud, glossy stop on the loop, and the crew who came for one big night out will get it here. Chora rewards an aimless wander: the windmill row above the old port, the whitewashed maze behind it, and Little Venice, where the balconies hang over the water and the sunset bars fill early. If the party side isn't your speed, Agios Sostis in the north stays low-key, with no sunbeds and a single taverna above the sand. The real prize sits a short sail west. Delos, the sacred island and birthplace of Apollo in myth, holds one of the most complete ancient sites in the Aegean, and you can only visit it by day.
thingsToDo: Day-sail across to ancient Delos
Wander the windmills above the old port
Sunset drink in Little Venice
Escape to quiet Agios Sostis beach
3.mooringTip: Book a berth at the new port at Tourlos well ahead in summer; the old-harbour quay is short-stay only and exposed. The channel between Mykonos and Delos accelerates the Meltemi, so expect a lively approach and reef down early.

4.shortDescription: An easy downwind run of 18 miles south to Paros, where the Byzantine church at Parikia and the pastel harbour of Naoussa split your time.
4.description: With the Meltemi behind you this is a pleasant downwind slide, usually a relaxed three hours. Parikia, the capital, greets you with the Panagia Ekatontapiliani, a fourth-century church whose name points to a hundred doors; the courtyard is cool and worth an hour out of the heat. Paros balances its two harbours nicely. Parikia handles the ferries and the bustle, while Naoussa, up on the north coast, keeps the looks of the old fishing port it used to be, half-sunk Venetian fort and all. Rent a scooter and climb inland to Lefkes, a marble-laned mountain village most day-trippers skip. Come evening, order astakomakaronada, the lobster-and-spaghetti dish the Cyclades does well, at a table close enough to hear the water lap.
thingsToDo: Visit the fourth-century Ekatontapiliani church
Wander Naoussa's pastel fishing harbour
Scooter inland to Lefkes village
Order astakomakaronada by the water
4.mooringTip: Parikia's town quay fills fast and can be rolly when the ferries come and go; Naoussa harbour makes a calmer overnight, though space is tight. Across the channel, several Antiparos tavernas keep free moorings for diners.

5.shortDescription: The longest leg, 30 miles west into the wilder western Cyclades, ending under the whitewashed Chora that crowns Serifos above Livadi bay.
5.description: This is the day to leave early, ideally by nine. Thirty miles is the longest hop of the week, and out here the Meltemi has open water to build across, so a reef in the main is sensible. Serifos pays you back for the effort. It's one of the barest, most volcanic islands in the group, and its Chora is a proper eagle's-nest village, whitewashed houses spilling down from a Venetian kastro two hundred metres above the harbour. Walk up the old stepped path from Livadi rather than driving; the light near sunset makes it worthwhile. Down at the bay the water runs clear over pale pebbles. If it happens to be a Sunday, look for revithada, chickpeas slow-baked overnight in a wood oven, the kind of dish that only turns up where people still cook the old way.
thingsToDo: Hike the stepped path to Chora
Explore the Venetian kastro up top
Swim the clear shallows off Livadi
Try Sunday revithada from the wood oven
5.mooringTip: Livadi is well protected from the north and northeast; go stern-to on the quay or anchor on sand in 5-7 m out in the bay. Holding is good, but the afternoon wind can gust hard down the valley, so leave scope.

6.shortDescription: Eighteen miles north to under-visited Kythnos, home of the double-sided Kolona sandbar and the thermal springs that steam into the sea at Loutra.
6.description: Kythnos is the island most charter crews sail straight past, and they're missing something. The run up from Serifos is straightforward, leaving time for the main event: Kolona, a ribbon of sand with sea on both sides that ties the island to a small offshore islet. Anchor off and you can swim across from one bay to the other. Merihas, the main port on the west coast, keeps a working-harbour feel and a handful of tavernas where sfoungato, the local cheese fritter, is worth ordering. Over in the northeast at Loutra, thermal springs run warm into the sea; a soak there after a week of salt is a fine way to close things out. The night sky over these emptier islands stays genuinely dark, so linger on deck.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim across Kolona sandbar
Soak in the Loutra thermal springs
Order sfoungato at a Merihas taverna
Stargaze on deck in Apokrousi Bay
6.mooringTip: Merihas has a town quay for stern-to mooring, handy for provisions. For a quieter night, anchor on sand in 5-7 m at Kolona or nearby Apokrousi Bay, both well sheltered from the prevailing northerlies.

7.shortDescription: A final 18-mile passage back to Lavrion, with an optional swim beneath the cliff-top Temple of Poseidon at Cape Sounion.
7.description: The last leg turns back toward the mainland, and if the timing works it's worth breaking it at Cape Sounion. The Temple of Poseidon stands on the headland at the southern tip of Attica, and dropping the hook below the columns for a final swim beats motoring straight in. Give yourself a buffer, though, because charter handovers usually want the boat back by late afternoon with a full tank. From Sounion it's a short run into Lavrion Marina, the base you started from. Fuel up, rinse the salt off, and settle the logbook over a last plate of taramasalata on the quay. A week of Cyclades sailing does something to a crew, and most start plotting the next loop before the lines are even coiled.
thingsToDo: Swim below the Temple of Poseidon
Photograph the Sounion headland columns
Top up fuel before the Lavrion handover
Last taramasalata on the marina quay
7.mooringTip: Lavrion Marina is the standard turnaround base; book your fuel-dock slot 24 hours ahead, as the queue backs up on the Friday and Saturday changeover days. Arrive with time to clean and clear the boat before handover.

lavrion-mykonos-santorini-14-days.metaDesc: Sail 14 days from Lavrion through Syros, Tinos, Mykonos and Santorini, then loop home via Milos and Sifnos. Central Cyclades, minimal motoring.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 18-mile shakedown east from Lavrion to Kea, with Vourkari's seafood quay waiting at the end of the afternoon.
1.description: Starting from Lavrion rather than Alimos hands you a head start: the boat sits a dozen miles nearer the islands before you even slip the lines. There is no rush on day one. Load provisions, sort the cabins, and push off after lunch when the base has finished the handover. The crossing runs east past Cape Sounion, taking the northerly on the quarter for a fast, forgiving reach. Vourkari opens up on Kea's northwest corner, a long quay with room to lie stern-to and reliable cover from the prevailing north. Fishing boats still work off the same jetties, and the ouzeris behind them grill whatever came in that morning. We usually settle the crew here for the first night, with the hilltop capital of Ioulida a short taxi ride up for anyone still restless after dinner. An easy start sets the tone.
thingsToDo: See the archaic Stone Lion above Ioulida
Snorkel the Patris wreck off Koundouros
Grilled octopus at a Vourkari ouzeri
Evening stroll through Ioulida's marble lanes
1.mooringTip: Lie stern-to on Vourkari's quay with lazy lines, roughly EUR 25-35 a night, and you're well tucked from the northerly Meltemi. If the quay fills up, motor two miles east to Otzias and drop the hook on sand in 6-8 metres.

2.shortDescription: A 32-mile reach to Syros: light air off the blocks, then a proper Meltemi beam ride into grand Ermoupoli.
2.description: Leave early. Before about one in the afternoon the wind sits under twelve knots, and you want as many miles as possible banked before the Meltemi wakes up. Thirty-two miles of open water lie between Kea and Syros, so the second half of the passage tends to turn into a quick beam reach with a building sea. Tuck a reef in before you need it and the leg stays comfortable. Ermoupoli is the working capital of the Cyclades, its neoclassical houses stacked up the hillside in faded ochre and rose. The town quay is among the most forgiving stern-to berths in the islands, holding well in most conditions. Ashore, the marble expanse of Miaouli Square fills up after dark, and the climb to Ano Syros rewards you with the old Catholic quarter and the whole harbour laid out below.
thingsToDo: Climb to Ano Syros's Catholic quarter
Taste loukoumi at an old Ermoupoli confectioner
Cross Miaouli Square after dark
Swim the rock ledges at Asteria
2.mooringTip: Ermoupoli's town quay is free and you set your own anchor; holding is good and shelter is unusually broad for the Cyclades. If it's packed in high season, Manna Marina just north takes you for EUR 60-80 a night.

3.shortDescription: Only fourteen miles, though the Meltemi accelerates through the Syros-Tinos channel, so it's a quick passage — be tied up before four.
3.description: Short does not mean lazy. The gap between Syros and Tinos squeezes the northerly and can add five knots to whatever the forecast promised, and by late July a twenty-five-knot afternoon in the channel is routine. Ride it on the beam and the fourteen miles fly past, but aim to have lines ashore by four before the swell stacks up at the harbour mouth. Tinos wears two identities. It is the great Orthodox pilgrimage island, where the faithful climb to the church of Panagia Evangelistria, and it is the marble-carving heart of the Aegean, a craft you can still watch in the workshops of Pyrgos up north. Inland the hills are scattered with ornate Venetian dovecotes. Book nothing, but arrive early: the new harbour holds moderate sand, so lay out plenty of scope.
thingsToDo: Watch marble carvers at work in Pyrgos
Walk the dovecote trail above Falatados
Lunch on artichokes a la polita
Wander the boulder field at Volax
3.mooringTip: Go stern-to in the new harbour for EUR 25-40 a night, but treat the sand as moderate holding and set your anchor with long scope. When the Meltemi tops 25 knots, the inner small-craft basin rides much calmer.

4.shortDescription: Barely eight miles to Mykonos, but the strait kicks up rough; book Tourlos ahead or gamble on the exposed town quay.
4.description: The hop to Mykonos is the shortest sail of the fortnight, but do not let eight miles fool you. The Tinos strait reliably blows five knots over the forecast and throws up a short, jarring chop by mid-afternoon, so it earns its respect. In July and August the sensible plan is a pre-booked slot at Tourlos, the newer marina north of town; reserve it online a day ahead and the evening looks after itself. The old town quay is free but wide open to swell, tenable only when the northerly stays under eighteen knots. There's time before dinner for a swim, so anchor off Ornos or Psarou on the way in. Then it's a short ride into the whitewashed lanes, and the caique to ancient Delos runs from the old port if anyone wants the ruins.
thingsToDo: Catch the caique to ancient Delos
Beach lunch at road-free Agios Sostis
Sunset along the Little Venice waterfront
Hike the Armenistis lighthouse loop
4.mooringTip: Book Tourlos New Marina online 24 hours ahead for July and August; expect EUR 70-110 a night at peak. The free town quay only makes sense when the northerly is forecast below 18 knots, and even then it rolls.

5.shortDescription: A 22-mile downwind sleigh ride southwest to Naoussa, the prettiest fishing harbour on Paros, gusts spilling off Naxos en route.
5.description: This is the leg crews remember. Twenty-two miles of open Aegean run southwest with the Meltemi on the quarter, the boat surfing along at hull speed for four or five hours of pure downwind sailing. Keep an eye out as you pass the Naxos channel, which funnels the strongest gusts of the day and can catch a lazy helmsman out. Naoussa sits on the north coast of Paros, its little Cycladic fishing port ringed by tavernas that spill their tables right to the water. Boats come and go, nets dry on the quay, and the whole scene tips over into a lively dinner crowd once the sun drops. Slots are tight and tend to go by five in the afternoon. If the harbour is full or the Meltemi is pushing in, drop anchor just east in Plastira Bay on good sand.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the smooth granite of Kolymbithres
Taste local wine at a Marpissa vineyard
Ouzo on the Naoussa fishing quay
Walk the marble paths of Lefkes
5.mooringTip: Naoussa's small harbour goes stern-to for EUR 30-50 a night and usually fills by five. If you miss out, anchor half a mile east in Plastira Bay on sand in 5-7 metres, or fall back on larger Parikia.

6.shortDescription: The windiest 28 miles of the trip: a hard Meltemi reach south through the Naxos-Sikinos channel, reefed down, into Ios.
6.description: Today the Aegean shows its teeth. The twenty-eight miles south pass through the Naxos-Sikinos channel, one of the fiercest Meltemi stretches in the central Cyclades, where a steady twenty-two to thirty knots on a port-tack reach is normal fare. Shorten sail early, before the channel rather than in it, and the passage becomes exhilarating instead of frantic. Ormos, the port of Ios, gives good shelter once you're inside, and its marina has been growing; the older quay still does the job when berths are short. Save Mylopotas, the long sandy sweep a mile south, for a daytime swim only, since it offers nothing overnight in a blow. Ios has shaken off some of its party reputation, though the Chora windmills still draw a crowd at sunset and the little squares hum late into the night.
thingsToDo: Hike to the reputed tomb of Homer
Swim the south-coast sands of Manganari
Sunset drinks by the Chora windmills
Find a late rembetika bar in Chora
6.mooringTip: Ormos harbour takes you stern-to for EUR 25-40 a night; grab a new-marina slot in advance when one's available. Treat Mylopotas strictly as a lunch anchorage, since it becomes untenable overnight in any Meltemi.

7.shortDescription: 18 miles south to Santorini's caldera, which you cannot anchor; leave Ios at dawn and berth at Vlychada by lunch.
7.description: Santorini asks for good timing. The caldera is a drowned volcano with water hundreds of metres deep, so swinging at anchor is simply off the table, and you want a calm window and full daylight to take it all in from the water. Leave Ios at first light and the eighteen miles slip by easily on the morning breeze. Vlychada, on the south coast, is the one genuine overnight: a sheltered marina with road access and berths worth booking well ahead in high season. Tie up by lunchtime and the afternoon is yours. Most crews grab a taxi up to Oia for the sunset that everyone photographs and few forget, then work back down through the wine country. The volcanic soil here makes a bone-dry Assyrtiko you'll want to taste at the source.
thingsToDo: Taxi to Oia for the cliff-top sunset
Taste Assyrtiko at Santo Wines or Sigalas
Swim below the cliffs at Red Beach
Fava with capers in Megalochori
7.mooringTip: Vlychada Marina is the only reliable berth, stern-to at EUR 60-100 a night, and it needs booking ahead in peak weeks. The cruise-ship buoys off Thirasia are a poor backup, so don't plan around them.

8.shortDescription: Turn for home with the first real beat: 22 upwind miles northwest to Folegandros, Meltemi dead on the bow.
8.description: The pointy end of the trip starts here. Turning northwest to begin the loop home means putting the Meltemi on the nose for the first time, and the twenty-two miles to Folegandros are all beating and tacking rather than reaching. Pick your moment, keep the crew fed, and it's a satisfying sail rather than a slog. Karavostasi is the only working port, snug enough behind the northerly but wide open to anything from the south, so read the forecast before you commit. The real Folegandros waits high above on the cliff edge, where the Chora ranks among the loveliest in all the islands; it's a thirty-minute climb up the old kalderimi or a quick taxi. On the way in, drop the hook at Katergo, a boat-only cove with pale water, for a swim you'll have half to yourselves.
thingsToDo: Climb to Chora's cliff-edge Panagia church
Swim the boat-only cove at Katergo
Try matsata pasta with goat ragu
Walk the kalderimi to Ano Meria
8.mooringTip: Karavostasi goes stern-to for EUR 25-40 a night with only a handful of slots; anchor on sand in 4-7 metres in the bay just east if it's full. Steer clear altogether in southerlies, as the port has no cover from the south.

9.shortDescription: Another 20-mile beat northwest to Milos, where Adamas Bay tucks so deep the wind dies the moment you round in.
9.description: Another twenty miles into the wind, another pair of long tacks northwest, and the reward at the end is one of the most protected harbours in the western Cyclades. Adamas lies at the head of a deep bay that bites right into the heart of Milos, and once you clear the entrance the sea flattens and the breeze falls away to almost nothing. Shelter here is close to total, whatever the wind is doing outside. Time your run to pass Sarakiniko with a swim in mind: the wind-carved white rock looks like the surface of the moon, though the anchorage is exposed and only worth stopping at in settled air. Milos rewards a wander ashore too, from the catacombs near Plaka to the painted boat garages of Klima, where fishermen still keep their caiques.
thingsToDo: Swim beneath the white cliffs of Sarakiniko
Snorkel the sea caves at Papafragas
Take in the view from Plaka Castle
Photograph the syrmata boathouses at Klima
9.mooringTip: Adamas town quay goes stern-to for EUR 30-50 a night with excellent all-round shelter thanks to the long bay. Pollonia in the northeast is cheaper but lies exposed when the Meltemi blows from the north.

10.shortDescription: A short 18-mile leg north to Sifnos, the Cyclades' kitchen island, where dinner outshines the sailing.
10.description: Eighteen miles north, and the sailing eases off. Kamares Bay on the west coast is the standard overnight, well covered from north and northeast, though its mouth faces west. Come late August, when the Meltemi picks up a westerly slant, swell can roll straight in and unsettle the bay; that's the cue to shift round to Platys Gialos on the south coast instead. Whichever you choose, the real reason to stop is the food. Sifnos is where Greek islanders send you for dinner: chickpea revithada left to cook overnight in a wood oven, lamb baked slowly in clay mastelo pots, recipes that trace back to the island's own nineteenth-century cookbook tradition. Between meals, the walk out to the little monastery of Chrysopigi, marooned on its rocky spit near Faros, is worth the afternoon.
thingsToDo: Walk to Chrysopigi monastery near Faros
Eat mastelo lamb up in Artemonas
Visit a pottery workshop in Vathy
Swim the calm cove at Vathi
10.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in 5-8 metres in Kamares Bay, or take the small quay for EUR 20-30 a night when a slot's free. Once the wind clocks into the west, move round to Platys Gialos on the south coast for a settled night.

11.shortDescription: Just 14 miles north to Serifos, but Livadi Bay funnels the Meltemi, so set your anchor hard and dive to check it.
11.description: A gentle fourteen-mile hop on paper, but Serifos keeps you honest at the far end. Livadi Bay opens straight into the Meltemi and works like a funnel, and while the sandy bottom holds well in theory, the steady pull stretches out anchors and dragging is the classic Serifos mishap. Lay plenty of chain, dive down to check the hook is properly buried, and stay aboard for the first hour before anyone heads ashore. It pays off. Above the port, the Chora tumbles down the hillside in tiers of white, one of the most photogenic villages in the whole archipelago, reached on foot up the old donkey path or by taxi if legs are tired. The kafeneia in the little squares do a fine chickpea revithada, and the windmills above town catch the last of the light.
thingsToDo: Climb the donkey path up to Chora
Eat chickpea revithada in a kafeneio
Swim at south-coast Psili Ammos
Watch sunset from the Chora windmills
11.mooringTip: Anchor in Livadi Bay on sand in 5-8 metres and lay out long scope, or go stern-to on the town quay for EUR 20-30 a night. Above 22 knots the drag risk climbs, so always dive the anchor before you leave the boat.

12.shortDescription: 15 miles north to Kythnos and its showpiece: Kolona, a sand spit with swimming on both sides, anchor and stay to dine.
12.description: Fifteen miles north brings you to Kythnos, and the headline here is not the harbour but a beach you can only reach by boat. Merichas, the small ferry port on the west coast, is a fine enough fallback for a quiet night on the quay. The prize lies six miles further up the coast at Kolona, a slender two-hundred-metre ribbon of sand that ties the islet of Agios Loukas to Kythnos proper, with clear shallow water lapping both flanks of the spit. Anchor off, and the swimming becomes the whole point of the day; there's no village to walk to, no schedule to keep. We tend to drop the hook by mid-afternoon and stay put right through dinner, watching the light change over the sandbar. It's the kind of anchorage crews talk about for years.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim off the Kolona sandbar
Soak in the hot springs at Loutra
Cycle the ridge road to Driopida
Sfougato cheese pie in a Driopida taverna
12.mooringTip: Anchor for free on sand in 4-6 metres at Kolona, where the islet screens you from the northerly Meltemi. Merichas town quay runs EUR 20-30 a night, and Loutra has a few marina berths beside its natural hot springs.

13.shortDescription: The last 24 miles northwest home to Lavrion, Cape Sounion's temple abeam, sometimes an easy evening drift on a dying Meltemi.
13.description: The run home is one of the gentler final legs in the Cyclades, and that's the quiet advantage of a Lavrion base: sitting east of Athens, it saves you the longer haul that an Alimos charter faces. Twenty-four miles northwest, with Cape Sounion rising off to starboard about halfway across, the columns of Poseidon's temple marking the last real landmark before the marina. In a fading evening Meltemi the leg can turn into a lazy downwind drift, sails goose-winged, no one in a hurry to arrive. Stop the boat under the temple for a farewell swim if the timing works. Top up at the fuel berth on the way in so the tanks are full for handover, then find the crew a table at a harbourside taverna. Bases here usually want the boat alongside by five the evening before.
thingsToDo: Swim below the temple at Cape Sounion
Refuel and rinse down at Lavrion
Crew dinner at a Lavrion harbour taverna
Browse the shops in Lavrion town
13.mooringTip: Lavrion's Olympic Marina takes you stern-to with lazy lines for EUR 60-90 a night. Refuel at the fuel berth by the entrance before you moor, and confirm your handover slot with the base 24 hours ahead.

14.shortDescription: Handover before nine, deposit back within a week, and Athens airport just fifty minutes up the road from Lavrion.
14.description: The last morning is straightforward. The base likes the boat handed back before nine, and the check-out goes quickest with the skipper aboard while the manager walks through the inspection. Assuming nothing is flagged, the security deposit comes back within about a week. One of the quieter perks of a Lavrion finish is the run to the airport: roughly fifty minutes by road, against the longer, more traffic-bound crawl from the marinas closer to Athens. There's usually time for one last frappe and a bite at a harbour kafeneio before the goodbyes, and the coffee here costs a fraction of what you'd pay down in Glyfada. Settle up, photograph anything that needs noting before you sign, and thank the dock crew who've looked after the lines all fortnight. Then it's transfers, and already talking about next year.
thingsToDo: Final walk-through with the base manager
Last frappe at a Lavrion kafeneio
Airport transfer, 50 minutes from Lavrion
Tip the dock crew for good service
14.mooringTip: Hand the boat back before nine and expect the deposit within a week of a clean inspection. Photograph any existing marks and get them noted in writing before you sign the check-out sheet.

lefkas-ithaka-kastos.metaTitle: 7-Day Ionian Sail: Lefkas to Ithaka | Boat4You
lefkas-ithaka-kastos.metaDesc: Sail a 7-day yacht charter from Lefkas through Meganisi, Ithaka, Fiskardo, Kastos and Kalamos, the calm taverna-lined heart of the Ionian.

1.shortDescription: Day one is an easy six-mile warm-up, heading south out of the Lefkas canal to Meganisi, where three protected coves compete for your first night.
1.description: Most crews spend the morning sorting the boat at D-Marin, then slip out through the canal once the floating bridge swings open on the hour. The run down to Meganisi is short, so there is no rush. Spilia bay, tucked under the cliff village of Spartochori, has restaurant pontoons where a meal buys you the berth. Vathi on the north coast is smaller and friendlier, its quay lined with fish tavernas. If you want quiet, Porto Atheni bites deep into the east side and holds an anchor well. Take the dinghy round to Papanikolis, the big sea cave named after the submarine said to have sheltered inside during the war. Grilled octopus and a cold beer round off the first evening as the light goes soft over the water.
thingsToDo: Dinghy into Papanikolis submarine cave
Climb to Spartochori for the view
Anchor deep in Porto Atheni
Grilled octopus at a Vathi taverna
1.mooringTip: Spilia and Vathi both run restaurant moorings, so eat ashore and the berth is free. For a calm night on your own ground tackle, anchor and long-line into Porto Atheni.

2.shortDescription: Around eighteen miles southeast to Ithaka, the island of Odysseus, ending in the deep horseshoe harbour of Vathy.
2.description: This is the longest open stretch of the week, so we usually leave by nine while the sea is still flat and let the afternoon breeze push us the last few miles. Vathy sits at the head of a long inlet, a ring of terracotta roofs under bare grey hills. Berth stern-to on the town quay if there is room; it fills by mid-afternoon in July. Above Dexa Bay, a rough path climbs to the Cave of the Nymphs, the spot where Homer has Odysseus stash his gifts from the Phaeacians. Swim off Dexa in the flat morning water. Come evening, the waterfront tavernas put tables almost onto the quay, and a glass of Kefalonian Robola goes down well with the smell of woodsmoke and grilled fish.
thingsToDo: Hike to the Cave of the Nymphs
Swim off Dexa Bay in the morning
Drive up to Anogi mountain village
Kefalonian Robola on the Vathy quay
2.mooringTip: Vathy town quay is stern-to on your own anchor, first-come, and busy by early afternoon. If it is full, Kioni or Frikes on the north coast make quieter alternatives, both with a small quay.

3.shortDescription: A short coastal sail to Fiskardo, the one Kefalonian village left standing after the 1953 quake, and the prettiest harbour on the north coast.
3.description: Fiskardo is a genuinely lovely place, which is exactly the problem: everyone wants to be there. The sail across from Ithaka is easy, an hour or two of gentle work, but the quay is tiny and boats start circling from mid-afternoon. Aim to arrive by two, or take a line ashore to the olive trees on the north side of the inlet where it is calmer anyway. The village survived the 1953 earthquake almost untouched, so the waterfront keeps its Venetian shutters and faded neoclassical fronts. Tassia, the taverna run for decades by Tassia Dendrinou, is worth booking ahead. Order the kreatopita, the Kefalonian meat pie. If the crowds get much, Foki Bay a mile south is a swimming stop with shade under the cypress trees.
thingsToDo: Book a table at Tassia taverna
Kreatopita meat pie on the quay
Swim and shade at Foki Bay
Walk out to the Fiskardo lighthouse
3.mooringTip: The Fiskardo quay is small and med-mooring fills by early afternoon in season, so arrive by two or take a long line to the olive trees on the north shore. Foki Bay, a mile south, is a quiet anchoring fallback.

4.shortDescription: A longer leg back east across the channel to Kastos, a thread of an island with one village, under fifty winter residents, and real silence.
4.description: After the bustle of Fiskardo, Kastos feels like a different sea. It is a long, thin sliver east of Ithaka, barely a couple of hundred people in summer and far fewer once the season ends. The sail over takes most of the morning, so leave the quay early and enjoy the crossing. There is one small harbour on the west side, a handful of restaurant moorings, and a village that climbs the hill behind. Snorkel the clear water near the Blue Cave on the south coast before lunch. Walk the old donkey tracks up past dry-stone terraces and fig trees to the abandoned windmill on the ridge, where the whole island lays out below you. Dinner is briam, slow-baked summer vegetables, at one of the two tavernas above the port.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the shore by the Blue Cave
Hike the donkey paths to the windmill
Briam at a taverna above the port
Watch the fishing boats work the harbour
4.mooringTip: The quay at Kastos is tiny, and the handful of buoys off the village belong to the tavernas — book a table and yours is settled. Get there early, because the afternoon flotillas snap up what little room there is.

5.shortDescription: A quick hop to neighbouring Kalamos, bigger and greener, with pine-covered slopes, two small villages, and the ghost harbour of Port Leone.
5.description: Kalamos is a five-mile stroll from Kastos, so there is time for a swim first. The island rises steep and green, thick with pine, and the smell of it reaches you offshore. Port Kalamos, the main harbour on the southeast corner, is where George's Taverna has helped yachts moor for years; George himself often waves you in, and the mooring comes with dinner. Round on the southwest side lies Port Leone, a village emptied after the 1953 quake cracked its spring and left it without water. The stone houses and little church still stand, quiet above an anchorage. Swim at Asprogiali, where the pebbles have worn smooth and pale. In the evening try the savoro, small fish fried and steeped in rosemary and vinegar.
thingsToDo: Moor with George's Taverna at Port Kalamos
Anchor below the Port Leone ghost village
Swim the smooth pebbles at Asprogiali
Savoro vinegar-fried fish at the harbour
5.mooringTip: Port Kalamos quay is stern-to and George from the taverna will usually help you in; a meal ashore covers the berth. For a night at anchor, Asprogiali Bay holds well on sand in five to seven metres.

6.shortDescription: The long haul north back to base, roughly twenty-two miles up the coast, with the afternoon breeze usually filling in behind you.
6.description: This is the biggest single passage of the week, a little over twenty miles, so it pays to get away early and use the calm morning under motor. By early afternoon the northwest breeze, the maistros, usually fills in and you can shut the engine down for a good sail up the channel. There is time for one last long swim on the way; Sivota on southern Lefkada makes a sheltered lunch stop with tavernas at the head of the bay. Boats routing round the outside can look in at Porto Katsiki, the white cliff beach on the west coast, though it is exposed if the wind has any west in it. Aim to reach the canal before the bridge's evening closure and settle back into D-Marin for the night.
thingsToDo: Lunch and swim in Sivota bay
Look in at Porto Katsiki cliff beach
Sail the afternoon maistros up the channel
Time the Lefkas bridge before dusk
6.mooringTip: D-Marin Lefkas is a full-service charter base with laid lines on the pontoons. Book your fuel slot a day ahead, and check the last bridge opening so you do not get stuck outside the canal for the night.

7.shortDescription: A slow last morning in Lefkas Town before handover: coffee and loukoumades, the covered market, and the floating bridge across the lagoon.
7.description: Handover is usually mid-morning, so there is no need to rush the last day. Do the packing and a quick clean the evening before, and you can spend the morning ashore instead of scrambling. Lefkas Town is a good place to wind down. The covered market sells local honey, olives and the leather sandals the island is known for, and the pastry shops do loukoumades, little fried dough balls soaked in honey, best with a strong Greek coffee. Walk out along the floating pontoon bridge that crosses the lagoon toward the old Santa Maura fort. Hand the boat back with the fuel topped up, swap notes with the base staff on where you anchored, and you are done. The Ionian tends to pull crews back.
thingsToDo: Loukoumades and Greek coffee in town
Browse the covered market for leather sandals
Walk the lagoon bridge to Santa Maura fort
Clean and pack for the handover
7.mooringTip: For the D-Marin Lefkas handover, top up fuel and do the clean the evening before so the morning is unhurried. Confirm your check-out time and fuel slot with the base a day ahead.

lefkas-paxos-ithaka-14-days.metaTitle: 14-Day Ionian Odyssey: Lefkas to Ithaka | Boat4You
lefkas-paxos-ithaka-14-days.metaDesc: A two-week Ionian sailing route from Lefkas through Paxos, Corfu and Ithaka, with quiet island quays, ghost villages and some of Greece's clearest coves.

1.shortDescription: The season opens with a 30-mile run up the mainland, gated by the Lefkas swing-bridge and finished under a Venetian castle.
1.description: Your first day starts at a bridge, not a bay. The Lefkas canal swing-bridge lifts once an hour, and the holding basin on its south side packs in thirty-odd boats by mid-morning in July, so we usually aim for the nine o'clock opening and clear it before the queue builds. Once you're through, the southwesterly thermal fills in along the Epirus coast and pushes you north on a comfortable beam reach. Parga arrives like a stage set: ochre and cream houses spilling down to the water beneath a fourteenth-century Norman-Venetian fort. Valtos beach lies a mile west if the crew wants a swim before dinner. The climb to the ramparts takes about twenty-five minutes and pays off at sunset, when the light drops behind the offshore islet and the whole bay goes gold.
thingsToDo: Swim the golden crescent of Valtos beach
Climb to the Norman-Venetian castle ramparts
Saganaki prawns on the Bara taverna terrace
Coffee with a view at Krioneri kafenio
Wander the lantern-lit old-town stairs at dusk
1.mooringTip: Anchor off in 5-10 m of good-holding sand, or grab one of the few stern-to slots on the town quay. The bay is open to the south and southwest, so if a southerly is forecast, tuck round to Valtos cove a mile west instead.

2.shortDescription: A gentle 14 miles up the coast to mainland Sivota, where two cypress islets close off a horseshoe bay and a shallow blue channel.
2.description: Two villages in the Ionian answer to the name Sivota, and crews mix them up every week of the season. This is the mainland one, often written Mourtos to keep the peace. The village sits inside a horseshoe of water guarded by a pair of pine-and-cypress islets, Mavro Oros and Mourtemeno, with a narrow gap between them that the tourist boards sell as the Blue Lagoon. The name earns itself here: four metres of clean sand, no weed, water the colour of a swimming pool. Bella Vraka, the sandbar linking the islets, is where the dinghy ends up. Aim for the town quay before five if you want the inner berth with water and power. For dinner, order the bourdeto early at Stavros or Karnayio; the spicy fish stew needs an hour on the stove.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Blue Lagoon gap between the islets
Wade out to the Bella Vraka sandbar
Bourdeto fish stew at Karnayio
Pine path above Mega Trafos cove
Mastiha cocktail on the seafront
2.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth on the town quay for a small fee with water and electric, or anchor off Bella Vraka in 5-8 m of sand, sheltered from the north and northwest. It fills fast on summer weekends, so call the Port Police on Channel 9 before you arrive.

3.shortDescription: Cross the strait to Gouvia and give the evening to Corfu Town: arcades borrowed from Paris, fortresses from Venice, dinner in a courtyard.
3.description: Corfu shadows you the whole way over, and the wind tends to die under its lee, so most of us end up motor-sailing the back half of the crossing. If the clock allows, drop into Agios Stefanos on the Greek shore for a swim; the anchorage runs to five or eight metres of sand below pine-topped cliffs and buys you a quiet hour before the marina traffic. Gouvia is the working charter base, with fuel next door and a taxi rank for the fifteen-minute ride into town. The Old Town is the reason you came ashore. The Liston, a run of arcaded cafes thrown up under French rule in 1807, faces the cricket-green Spianada and does a fair impression of Paris in miniature. Sit down to sofrito, then hunt out kumquat liqueur on Nikiforou Theotoki street.
thingsToDo: Swim stop in Agios Stefanos cove
Stroll the arcaded Liston cafes
Pastitsada beef stew in a courtyard taverna
Buy kumquat liqueur in the Old Town
Sundowner below the Old Fortress
3.mooringTip: Gouvia takes you on lazy lines with full services for a fee; have your charter agent pre-book the berth in season. For dinner ashore only, the NAOK quay opposite the Old Fortress works as a short stay, but don't plan on staying overnight without calling first.

4.shortDescription: Thirty miles down the Corfu Channel to Lakka, the rounder and quieter of Paxos's two harbours, with blue caves on the doorstep.
4.description: Most boats barrel straight down to Gaios at the south end of Paxos. I'd point you at Lakka instead, on the northern tip. It's an almost circular bay ringed with tavernas, a swim platform strung out among the moorings, and enough shelter from the south and southwest to sleep soundly. Time the approach to take in the Blue Caves on the headland a mile to the west; from the dinghy the water at the cliff foot glows an unlikely electric blue. The bay holds forty-odd boats on restaurant buoys, so pick one up and let dinner ashore settle the fee, or anchor in four to six metres of sand toward the southern shore. Romanzo on the front and Diogenes a street back are the reliable tables. Ask for ladotyri, the local cheese cured in olive oil.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Lakka Blue Caves on approach
Walk the olive groves above the village
Browse the Saturday morning market
Try ladotyri cheese cured in oil
Sundowner at the harbour-side Romanzo
4.mooringTip: Anchor in the bay in 4-6 m of sand; the holding is excellent if you drop well clear of the moored boats and back down properly, and it's free and sheltered from the south and southwest. For a restaurant buoy, pick it up and settle the fee when you eat ashore.

5.shortDescription: A layover with a choice: run four miles south to the pale sands of Antipaxos, or trace the limestone cliffs of the west coast.
5.description: An idle day on Paxos splits neatly two ways. South lies Antipaxos, four miles below Gaios, where Voutoumi and Vrika hold the best swimming water of the fortnight: three to seven metres of pure sand, no weed underfoot, the sort of transparent turquoise that people photograph and nobody believes. A single vineyard on the slope above Vrika bottles Verdea, an amber-tinged white you won't find anywhere off the island, so drink it while you can. The other option runs up the wild western shore. The Erimitis cliffs stretch three unbroken miles of limestone north of Lakka, the sea arch at Tripitos makes the obvious photograph, and Levrecchio cove on the way back is where the beach glitters with old sea glass. Whichever you pick, come back to Paxos for the night; Antipaxos has no harbour after dark.
thingsToDo: Long midday swim at Voutoumi beach
Taste Verdea white at the hillside vineyard
Lobster pasta at an Antipaxos taverna
Walk the Erimitis cliffs and Tripitos arch
Comb Levrecchio cove for sea glass
5.mooringTip: Anchor off Voutoumi or Vrika in 3-7 m of sand for the day; the holding is excellent, but there's no overnight harbour on Antipaxos, so head back to Paxos before dark. If you'd rather move to Gaios, expect a tight lazy-line berth on the town quay for a small fee.

6.shortDescription: The big one: forty miles southeast across open water to Vasiliki, racing the afternoon thermal that made this bay a windsurfing name.
6.description: This is the long leg, and it rewards an early alarm. Forty miles of open Ionian separate Paxos from Vasiliki, and the wind here runs to a schedule. The westerly that funnels between Lefkas and Kefalonia switches on around one in the afternoon and can be blowing twenty-five knots by four, which is exactly why the windsurfers colonised the place. Leave by eight and you ride it as a friendly beam reach rather than fighting a beat into the teeth of it. Break the passage with a swim at Atokos, the empty island roughly halfway, or Arkoudi a few miles on; both sit near enough the rhumb line to cost you nothing. Vasiliki itself is a fishing village that grew a watersports habit. The quay lies on the eastern, quieter side. Get tied up before two, before the wind makes backing in a wrestling match.
thingsToDo: Mid-passage swim at Atokos or Arkoudi
Watch the windsurfers work the bay
Sofrito at a quayside village taverna
Cliff walk to the Porto Katsiki overlook
Sundowner over the open Ionian
6.mooringTip: Berth stern-to on the town quay for a small fee; it's basic, with water but no electric, and the holding in the bay is good over 4-6 m of sand. The westerly thermal builds hard through the afternoon, so grab your slot before two while backing in is still manageable.

7.shortDescription: An easy 14 miles to Vathy, Ithaka's horseshoe capital, with the Cave of the Nymphs on the hill and Odysseus everywhere in the story.
7.description: A short fourteen miles brings you round into Vathy, the island's main town and the harbour where every Homer-themed cruise eventually ties up. The bay is a tidy horseshoe, pinched at the entrance and opening into a still inner basin lined with cypress and pale plaster. Above the village sits the Cave of the Nymphs, where the poem has Odysseus stash his gold on getting home. It's twenty-five minutes up a dirt track, and the dripstone chamber inside is worth carrying a head-torch for. On the way back, stop at Dexa Bay to swim off the pebbles where the Phaeacians supposedly laid the sleeping hero ashore. Dinner is grilled sardines under the plane trees at To Trehantiri or Sirenes. If you want the stroll, the little Venetian gun battery at the harbour mouth catches the last of the light.
thingsToDo: Hike up to the Cave of the Nymphs
Swim Dexa Bay's landing pebbles
Grilled sardines under the harbour plane trees
Wander Vathy's cobbled old-town lanes
Sunset at the harbour-mouth battery
7.mooringTip: Tie up stern-to on the Vathy town quay for a small fee, with water and electric on the wall, and set the anchor firmly in 5-8 m of mud since the bay is narrow and the entry channel steep. It fills early in mid-August, so Kioni, three miles north, is the calmer fallback.

8.shortDescription: A lazy day working north up the coast to Kioni, with detours to snorkel Polis Bay and eat octopus on the Frikes quay.
8.description: With no schedule to keep, spend the day hopping short distances up the sheltered north coast. Kioni, six miles from Vathy, is the prettiest harbour on the island: three Venetian windmills stand on the headland, one restored and two left to ruin, and the pastel houses crowd into a tight green bowl above a free quay that takes eight or ten boats. Two miles short of it lies Polis Bay, the swim of the day. A cave at the back once gave up Mycenaean pottery, and the shallows by the entrance show clear stone foundations that archaeologists still argue over. Frikes, the working port in between, does the best octopus stifado around; Penelope and Ulysses are the tavernas to look for. If the crew has legs left, the goat path from Frikes to Kioni takes about forty-five minutes and opens a fine view across to Kefalonia.
thingsToDo: Sail north to Kioni harbour
Snorkel the submerged ruins in Polis Bay
Octopus stifado on the Frikes quay
Walk the goat path from Frikes to Kioni
Visit the Stavros archaeological museum
8.mooringTip: At Kioni, go stern-to on the small free quay, which fills by four in the afternoon in season, or anchor off in 4-6 m of sand, with Frikes as the overflow if it's full. The whole north coast is well protected from the north and northwest, so either sleeps easy.

9.shortDescription: Just five miles across the channel to Fiskardo, the one Kefalonian village the 1953 earthquake spared, and the most photographed quay in the Ionian.
9.description: Five miles is barely a passage, so there's no rush to leave Ithaka. Fiskardo is the only village on Kefalonia the 1953 earthquake left standing while it flattened the rest of the northern peninsula, and the run of Venetian-era neoclassical houses along the front is the reward. It's been under heritage protection since the sixties, which is why it still looks the part. The catch is the quay: free, but packed by early afternoon in high summer, so raise the Port Police on Channel 12 and aim to arrive before one. A mile east, Emplisi beach hides Roman villa ruins in the shallows, with walls and potsherds scattered across the sand in three to five metres. Book a hillside table at Tselenti or a front-row one at Lagoudera, and ask for kreatopita, the Kefalonian meat pie under its thin pastry lid.
thingsToDo: Walk the cypress-lined Fiskardo quay
Snorkel the Roman ruins off Emplisi beach
Kreatopita meat pie at a waterfront taverna
Sundowner above the harbour at Tselenti
Dinghy to the cove shipwreck
9.mooringTip: Go stern-to on the free town quay, but it's full by two in August, so call the Port Police on Channel 12 well ahead. Foulis Bay, a mile east, is the overflow anchorage in 4-6 m of good-holding sand, though the ferry wash can make a crowded wall lively.

10.shortDescription: Eighteen miles back northeast to Kastos, a sliver of an island with fifty residents, one quay and one taverna: about as quiet as chartering gets.
10.description: Eighteen miles northeast puts you back across the channel and onto Kastos, a long splinter of rock east of Ithaka with fewer than fifty people living on it year-round. The quiet takes some adjusting to. There's one village around the harbour, one free quay with space for eight or ten boats, and one taverna under a fig tree, run by the same family for three generations. Walk the donkey path up to the derelict windmills on the ridge, about half an hour, and the view opens back over the water to Ithaka. On the south coast, the Blue Cave with its sandy floor and skylight makes a good snorkel; the local fishermen ferry visitors across for a few euros a head. Dinner is slow-cooked chickpeas in tomato and rosemary, mopped up with bread. There's no fuel and no shop, so stock up before you come.
thingsToDo: Climb the donkey path to the windmills
Snorkel the Blue Cave on the south side
Tomato-stewed chickpeas at the harbour taverna
Walk up to Kastos Village
Sundowner at the single quayside table
10.mooringTip: There's just the one free quay with no services, tight at eight to ten boats, so drop the anchor well clear in 5-7 m of sand and back in carefully, since the holding is only fair. It's sheltered from every direction, but top up fuel and provisions at Fiskardo or Lefkas first, because Kastos has neither.

11.shortDescription: No passage today, just short dinghy runs to empty coves and a slow afternoon in the shade: the reason you booked two weeks, not one.
11.description: There's nothing to clear and nowhere to be. A free day on Kastos is the one that tells you whether you booked the fortnight for the right reasons. The dinghy runs are all short, a mile or two at most: Sarakiniko Bay to the south, a white-pebble strand with no road to it and often nobody on it, then round to the Blue Cave for a second snorkel, and a small cove on the east side where the donkey track drops to the sea. Late in the afternoon, walk twenty minutes up to the abandoned village behind the port, its roofless stone houses a quiet record of the families who left for America in the fifties. Come evening, the kafeneio pours raki by the carafe and hands you a dominoes set for the table outside. This is the night that earns the second week.
thingsToDo: Kayak south to Sarakiniko Bay
Fish off the rocks at the harbour mouth
Read in the shade of the fig tree
Hike to the abandoned hillside village
Raki and dominoes at the kafeneio
11.mooringTip: Stay put on the Kastos quay, or shift a mile south to Sarakiniko Bay and anchor in 4-6 m of sand for a quieter night. Either way, remember there's no fuel or shop here; whatever you carried from Lefkas is all you've got before the run east.

12.shortDescription: A four-mile hop east to Kalamos, greener and taller than Kastos, with a ghost village at Port Leone and goats on the shoreline at dusk.
12.description: Four miles is the shortest hop of the trip, usually done under engine across flat water, hardly worth hoisting the main. Kalamos is Kastos's bigger sibling: still small and unhurried, but pine-clad and mountainous, with two settlements rather than one. Port Kalamos sits by the harbour, Episkopi higher up the ridge. The set piece is Port Leone on the north coast, an entire stone fishing village left empty after the 1953 earthquake wrecked the spring that watered it. Nobody moved back, and the houses are folding slowly into the bay. Reach it by dinghy or on a ninety-minute coast walk. South of the harbour, Asprogiali beach is the swim, its grey-blue moonstone pebbles a direct clue to the Pindus geology underfoot. At the harbour taverna, order savoro, small fish fried and steeped in vinegar and rosemary.
thingsToDo: Hike to the Port Leone ghost village
Swim the moonstone pebbles at Asprogiali
Climb up to Episkopi village
Savoro vinegar-fried fish at the harbour
Watch goats wander the shoreline at dusk
12.mooringTip: Berth stern-to on the single Port Kalamos quay for a small fee; it's basic, water but no power, and holds about a dozen boats. The holding is excellent over 5-8 m of sand, and you're sheltered from the north and northwest, though the channel between the two islands turns gusty in the afternoon.

13.shortDescription: Eighteen miles home through the Meganisi Channel, a last swim under the white cliffs of Agiofili, then back under the swing-bridge to base.
13.description: The last full day runs eighteen miles north, threading the Meganisi Channel and the inner waterway back to D-Marin Lefkas. Save time for one more swim at Agiofili, a mile south of Vasiliki, a pocket cove under white cliffs with a coarse-pebble beach and a bright turquoise patch in the middle worth the last photo. From there it's the canal swing-bridge that governs your arrival. It lifts on the hour, and the waiting basin backs up by half past four in August, so plan to be there by half past three for the four o'clock opening. D-Marin handles the second handover, so refuel before six and drop laundry with the marina desk for the morning. Lefkas Town is a fifteen-minute walk over the lagoon; the floating bridges are a local curiosity, and bakaliaros with garlicky skordalia is the right way to close the trip.
thingsToDo: Last swim at Agiofili's white-cliff cove
Dinghy stop off Skorpios island
Bakaliaros salt cod at a marina taverna
Walk the Lefkas Town floating bridges
Refuel at the D-Marin fuel dock
13.mooringTip: Berth back at D-Marin on lazy lines with full services for a fee, pre-booked through your agent. Leave plenty of margin for the canal swing-bridge, which opens on the hour and queues up early in August, and top up fuel before the technical hand-back.

14.shortDescription: A last morning at D-Marin: hand the boat back, wander the Lefkas Town market and floating bridges, then a short taxi to Aktion airport.
14.description: The hand-back at D-Marin is painless if you've done the groundwork: papers signed at the desk, fuel receipt filed, cleaning slot agreed the evening before, and the boat alongside by nine for the Saturday changeover. If your flight leaves you a spare hour, spend it in Lefkas Town. The covered market on the east side of the old centre sells leather sandals, village embroidery and jars of thyme honey, and any kafenio on the square will set you up with a Greek coffee and a plate of loukoumades, the little honey fritters dusted with cinnamon. The floating bridges across the lagoon are the last local oddity: they swing aside rather than lift, and it's a twenty-five-minute stroll over them to Agios Ioannis beach for a final dip. Aktion airport sits eight miles south, about twenty-five minutes by taxi or the KTEL bus from town.
thingsToDo: Greek coffee with honey-soaked loukoumades
Browse the leather-sandal market stalls
Cross the lagoon floating bridges on foot
Last swim at Agios Ioannis beach
Taxi to Aktion airport across the canal
14.mooringTip: For the D-Marin hand-over, confirm your fuel slot and cleaning time with the base manager the night before, and have the boat alongside by nine on Saturday for the technical check. From there it's a direct taxi or the KTEL bus to Aktion (PVK) across the canal.

mykanos-siros-milos-ios-14-days.metaTitle: 14-Day Cyclades from Paros: Milos & Ios | Boat4You
mykanos-siros-milos-ios-14-days.metaDesc: A 14-day round-trip from Paros through Sifnos, Syros, Mykonos, Naxos, Ios, Santorini, Folegandros and Milos: the full Cyclades grand tour.

1.shortDescription: The first leg runs southwest to Sifnos, a 20-mile settling-in sail to the island Greeks rate above all others for its cooking.
1.description: Sifnos sits about twenty miles off the bow, and the passage down gives everyone a chance to find their feet before the harder legs later in the trip. Kamares, the ferry port on the west side, is the natural place to spend the night. It tucks in well against the northerlies but stays open to the west, so keep an eye on any afternoon shift. Ashore, this is the island that taught Greece to cook. The inland villages of Apollonia and Artemonas are a short bus ride up, and the slow-baked meat in clay pots is worth the trip. We usually leave the boat by mid-morning to have the light for the walk out to Chrysopigi. Book nothing; just anchor and swim.
thingsToDo: Slow-baked lamb in clay pots at Artemonas
Walk to Chrysopigi monastery above Faros
Coffee in the Apollonia lanes at dusk
Swim the sandy bay at Platis Gialos
1.mooringTip: Sand holds well in 5-8 m across Kamares Bay; the small quay takes a stern-to line for roughly 20-30 euros a night. Should the breeze swing west and roll swell in, move round to Vathi.

2.shortDescription: A quick fourteen miles north drops you into Livadi, the port under Serifos Chora, one of the most theatrical hilltop towns in the group.
2.description: Serifos is a short jump, so there's time for a lazy morning on Sifnos before slipping the lines. Livadi Bay opens to the southeast and can accelerate the Meltemi straight down its throat, which makes the holding your main concern rather than the sail in. Set plenty of chain, then dive on the anchor before you relax; boats drag here more than most crews expect. The reward is the town overhead. Chora stacks white cubes up the ridge, and the old stone path climbing to it takes about half an hour on foot. Up top there are windmills, a couple of quiet kafeneia, and a view back down over the whole bay. Order the chickpea stew if the kitchen has it going that day.
thingsToDo: Climb the old stone path to Chora
Revithada chickpea stew at a kafeneio
Sunset by the windmills above town
Swim the fine sand at Psili Ammos
2.mooringTip: Anchor in Livadi Bay on sand in 5-8 m and lay out long scope; the town quay takes stern-to lines for 20-30 euros. Once it blows past the low twenties the bay gets nervous, so dive the anchor before heading ashore.

3.shortDescription: Fifteen miles north to Kythnos, where warm springs still run into the sea at Loutra and a sandbar links two bays at Kolona.
3.description: Break the leg north with a stop at Kolona, the thin ribbon of sand that joins Kythnos to the Agios Loukas islet. It shelves gently on both sides and gives cover from the north, so it makes a fine lunch anchorage and swim before you carry on. Loutra itself sits a few miles further up the coast. The thermal water that surfaces near the harbour has drawn people here since antiquity, and you can still feel the warm current where it meets the shallows. Stern-to in the harbour is straightforward with the laid lines. Inland, the linked villages of Chora and Dryopida reward an afternoon on a bike or in a hire car, with narrow lanes and a good taverna or two for the local cheese pie.
thingsToDo: Soak where the springs meet the sea at Loutra
Swim the double-bay sandbar at Kolona
Cycle the ridge between Chora and Dryopida
Sfougato cheese pie in a Dryopida taverna
3.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth in Loutra harbour on the laid lines, around 25-40 euros; it covers you from the north. When the wind has any south in it, Merichas on the west coast is the better bet.

4.shortDescription: Twenty-eight miles across to Syros and its grand capital Ermoupoli, a marble-and-neoclassical town that feels a world apart from the whitewashed islands.
4.description: The run over to Syros is the longest so far, usually a comfortable beam reach in the standard summer northerly. Ermoupoli comes as a surprise after the smaller islands. It was the busiest port in Greece in the nineteenth century, and the money built an opera house, a marble town square, and tiers of pastel mansions rising up two hills. The waterfront berth is one of the easier ones in the islands to lie against, with good protection whichever way the wind sits. Give yourself the evening ashore. Climb the stepped lanes into Ano Syros, the old Catholic quarter, for the view and a bite; back down at sea level, the confectioners still turn out loukoumi and nougat by hand. Miaouli Square fills up pleasantly once the heat drops.
thingsToDo: Climb into Ano Syros, the Catholic quarter
Taste hand-made loukoumi at an old confectioner
Evening stroll across Miaouli marble square
Swim off Foinikas on the south coast
4.mooringTip: Lie stern-to along the Ermoupoli waterfront on your own anchor at no charge, with shelter from every direction. If the quay is packed in high season, Manna marina nearby has berths for 60-80 euros.

5.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles east to Mykonos, with the option of dropping anchor off sacred Delos for a couple of hours on the way in.
5.description: Mykonos lies a straightforward twenty-two miles east. If the crew is up for it, break the passage at Delos, the uninhabited island that was one of the great sanctuaries of the ancient world; you can day-anchor off it and walk the ruins, but the site shuts around sunset and no one stays the night. Push on to Tourlos, the newer marina just north of the main town, and put a booking in a day ahead when July and August fill it. The town needs no introduction. Away from the crowded lanes there's a quieter side worth finding: the fishing houses of Little Venice at dusk, the row of windmills above them, and empty sand at Agios Sostis on the north shore, which has no road down to it.
thingsToDo: Day-anchor off Delos and walk the ruins
Lunch on the sand at Agios Sostis
Little Venice waterfront as the sun drops
Walk the loop to Armenistis lighthouse
5.mooringTip: Book a stern-to berth in Tourlos New Marina online, especially for July and August; reckon on 70-110 euros at peak. The old town quay only holds in light northerlies under about 18 knots.

6.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles south to Naxos, the biggest and greenest of the Cyclades, where a lone marble gateway frames the sunset at the harbour mouth.
6.description: Naxos is an easy reach south, and the town harbour is one of the more dependable stern-to berths on the route, tucked behind its mole and sheltered from most angles. The island earns a full day. It grows its own food, makes its own cheese and citron liqueur, and rises to Mount Zas, the highest peak in the Cyclades and, if you believe the old stories, the cave where Zeus grew up; the walk up takes the better part of four hours. Down in the old town, lanes wind up through the Venetian kastro. The set-piece is the Portara, a great marble doorframe standing alone on the islet at the harbour entrance, left over from a temple that was never finished. Walk out to it as the light goes.
thingsToDo: Sunset through the Portara marble doorway
Climb Mount Zas, the peak of the Cyclades
Kitron citron liqueur tasting at Halki
Swim the long sand at Agios Prokopios
6.mooringTip: Naxos town harbour is a reliable stern-to at 30-50 euros and holds up well whatever the wind does. There's usually room, so you rarely need to arrive early.

7.shortDescription: Twenty miles south to Ios, quiet coves and a Homer legend by day, and a hilltop Chora that only really wakes after midnight.
7.description: The reach down to Ios is short enough to build in a stop. Manganari, a string of sandy bays on the south coast, is the obvious one: sheltered from the north and made for an anchor lunch and a long swim. Round to Ormos afterwards for the night. Ios carries two reputations that rarely meet. By day it is farm tracks, empty beaches, and a windmill-topped Chora you can climb to on foot; up on the northern tip, tradition puts the grave of Homer at Plakotos, and there's a marked path out to it. After dark the same Chora turns into the loudest square in the Cyclades, and it doesn't get going until around eleven. Pick which version you want, or take both.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim off Manganari on the south coast
Walk to Homer's supposed grave at Plakotos
Late-night music in the Chora square
Sundowners by the windmills above town
7.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth in Ormos harbour, 25-40 euros; use the Mylopotas anchorage for daytime only. Manganari gives good cover from the north for the lunch stop.

8.shortDescription: Eighteen miles south into the flooded caldera of Santorini, where the water is too deep to anchor and Vlychada is the one marina that works.
8.description: Leave Ios at first light. Santorini is only eighteen miles off, but the caldera deserves to be entered in good daylight, and the marina you're aiming for is the sole practical overnight. The bay is a drowned volcanic crater, hundreds of metres deep almost to the cliff foot, so dropping the hook is out of the question; Vlychada, on the south coast, is where the boat stays. Book it well ahead in season. With the lines on, the island opens up above you. A taxi up to Oia gets you the famous cliff-edge sunset without fighting for a berth in the caldera, and the wineries inland pour a bone-dry Assyrtiko grown in low baskets against the wind. The volcanic soil shows up on the plate too, in the fava and the little tomato fritters.
thingsToDo: Taxi up to Oia for the cliff-top sunset
Assyrtiko tasting at Santo Wines or Sigalas
Fava and tomato fritters in Megalochori
Swim below the cliffs at Red Beach
8.mooringTip: Vlychada on the south coast is effectively the only overnight; take a stern-to berth for 60-100 euros and book it well ahead in high season. The caldera itself is too deep to anchor.

9.shortDescription: A full day off the water on Santorini, from a Bronze Age town frozen under ash to a slow caldera crossing by tender.
9.description: Keep the boat at Vlychada and give the island a day on its own. The obvious anchor for the morning is Akrotiri, the town that a volcanic eruption sealed under ash around 1600 BC and left almost intact, streets, drains and painted walls and all. It's the reason people call this the Cycladic Pompeii. In the afternoon, take the tender out into the caldera and cross to little Thirasia, the quiet island on the far rim, for lunch away from the crowds. Save the evening for the wine. The cliff-edge estates line up their Assyrtiko and sweet Vinsanto against a view that does half the work; Ammoudi Bay, down the steps below Oia, is the place for grilled fish once the sunset crowds thin out.
thingsToDo: Walk the ash-buried streets of Akrotiri
Tender across the caldera to Thirasia
Assyrtiko and Vinsanto at a cliff-edge estate
Grilled fish down at Ammoudi Bay
9.mooringTip: Stay put at Vlychada for a second night rather than chasing an anchorage. The cruise-boat buoys over at Thirasia aren't dependable for a charter yacht.

10.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles back northwest, usually hard on the wind, to Folegandros and its cliff-top Chora hanging two hundred metres above the sea.
10.description: Turning north again means putting the Meltemi on the nose, so this can be a wet, close-hauled slog of twenty-two miles; pick a lighter day for it if the forecast lets you. Karavostasi, the little port, gives fair shelter from the north but nothing from the south, so watch the wind direction before committing to the night. What you come for sits high above the harbour. Folegandros Chora runs along a cliff edge with a sheer drop to the water, and the church of the Panagia crowns the hill above it, reached by a zigzag of steps. The island stays gentler than its neighbours: a courtyard plate of matsata, the local hand-rolled pasta, under the plane trees, and a boat or a hard walk out to Katergo beach.
thingsToDo: Climb the steps to the Panagia church
Courtyard matsata pasta under the plane trees
Swim the pale pebbles at Katergo
Walk the old path out to Ano Meria
10.mooringTip: Karavostasi has only a handful of stern-to slots at 25-40 euros; if it's full, anchor on sand in 4-7 m in the bay just east. Steer clear of the whole place in any southerly.

11.shortDescription: Twenty more miles northwest to Milos, a volcanic island of white moon-rock and sea caves, wrapped around one of the safest bays in the Cyclades.
11.description: Another leg into the wind brings you to Milos, and the payoff is the anchorage. Adamas sits at the head of a huge bay that was once a volcano's crater, and it may be the most sheltered water anywhere in the western Cyclades; whatever the Meltemi is doing outside, it's usually calm in here. The island is the strange one of the group. Sarakiniko, on the north coast, is a field of wind-smoothed white rock that looks more like the moon than a beach, best visited by tender on a still day. Further round there are half-drowned sea caves at Papafragas and the pirate hideout of Kleftiko to the southwest. Ashore, climb to the castle at Plaka for the long view, and find the painted boat-garage doors down at Klima.
thingsToDo: Tender to the white rock at Sarakiniko
Snorkel the sea caves at Papafragas
Climb to Plaka castle for the panorama
Photograph the syrmata boat doors at Klima
11.mooringTip: Lie stern-to on the Adamas town quay for 30-50 euros, where the shelter holds from every direction. Pollonia on the northeast is cheaper but exposed to the north, so keep it for calm nights.
