12.shortDescription: Eighteen miles north back to Sifnos, this time to Vathi, a horseshoe bay with the calmest water and the island's old potters ashore.
12.description: Back to Sifnos, but a different corner of it. Vathi is a nearly enclosed bay on the southwest, with a sandy bottom that holds an anchor beautifully and almost no swell to speak of; after the exposed legs of the past few days, it's a relief to arrive somewhere this quiet. The village behind the beach has made pots for generations, and a couple of workshops still fire their kilns by the water, so there's usually something to watch and something to carry home. Spend the afternoon in the shallows and eat at one of the handful of tavernas along the sand. If the wind swings and the anchorage loses its charm, Kamares round the coast has a proper paid quay to fall back on.
thingsToDo: Watch a potter fire the kiln at Vathi
Swim the flat calm of Vathi Bay
Taverna lunch on the sand at Vathi
Taxi over to the Chrysopigi monastery
12.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in 4-6 m in Vathi, where you're shut in from just about everything. If you'd rather a quay, Kamares up the coast takes a stern-to line for 20-30 euros, though it opens to the west.

13.shortDescription: Twenty miles northeast back to Paros, an easier reach that ends at Naoussa's postcard fishing harbour for the last night aboard.
13.description: The last real sailing day is a kinder one, twenty miles northeast with the wind on the beam rather than the bow. Aim for Naoussa on the north coast, where a small fishing harbour with the stub of a Venetian fort in the water makes about as pretty a final berth as the Cyclades offers; Parikia, the capital, is the simpler option if you'd rather an easy tie-up. Naoussa is the place for a proper last supper. Fishermen still land the catch at the quay, the tavernas grill octopus that's been drying on a line all afternoon, and the lanes fill up slowly after dark. Earlier on, there's time for a swim among the sculpted granite of Kolymbithres, a short hop across the bay.
thingsToDo: Swim the sculpted granite at Kolymbithres
Grilled octopus on the Naoussa quay
Wine tasting at a Marpissa vineyard
Dusk walk on the Lefkes marble lanes
13.mooringTip: Naoussa's harbour takes stern-to lines for 30-50 euros but often fills by late afternoon, so aim to be in by five. Plastira Bay half a mile east anchors on sand in 5-7 m; Parikia is the roomier fallback.

14.shortDescription: A short last morning: hand the boat back at Parikia, settle the deposit, and slip the fifteen minutes to Paros airport.
14.description: Handover is usually before nine, so there's little to do but tidy the boat and be ready when the base manager comes aboard. The inspection runs through the hull, the engine hours and the inventory; assuming all is well, the security deposit comes back to your card within about a week. Paros airport sits down near Alyki in the south, only a quarter of an hour from Parikia, which makes the getaway painless. If the flights are later in the day, there's no rush. Take a slow breakfast on the Parikia waterfront while the ferries come and go, and if the timing works, drive across for one last swim on the long sandy stretch at Golden Beach before the transfer. Two weeks and the whole of the Cyclades behind you.
thingsToDo: Final walk-through with the base manager
Last swim on the sand at Golden Beach
Slow breakfast on the Parikia waterfront
Fifteen-minute transfer to Paros airport
14.mooringTip: Have the boat alongside and cleared before nine for the handover. Once the inspection is signed off, the deposit is released back to your card inside a week.

mykonos-kea-syros.metaDesc: Sail a week from Mykonos through Tinos, Andros, Kea, Kythnos and Syros: marble villages, thermal springs and the grand harbour of Ermoupoli.

1.shortDescription: A quick 8-mile hop across the windiest gap in the Cyclades to Tinos, marble country with a surf beach on its wild north coast.
1.description: Only eight miles separate Tourlos from Tinos, but the channel between the two islands squeezes the Meltemi into something fiercer than the open-water forecast suggests. Reckon on an extra five knots and a lively beam reach that has you tied up well before the afternoon builds. We usually aim to be secure by four. The main harbour takes you stern-to onto sand that holds only moderately, so lay out plenty of chain; when it blows past twenty-five, the inner basin is the calmer bet. Tinos rewards the effort ashore. Pyrgos, up in the hills, is the island's marble-carving heartland, and the villages are stitched together by hundreds of old Venetian dovecotes. Save an evening for artichokes cooked a la polita in a shaded courtyard, and if a northerly is running, the surf at Kolymbithra on the north coast is worth the drive over.
thingsToDo: Tour a marble workshop in Pyrgos
Follow the dovecote trail near Falatados
Eat artichokes a la polita in a courtyard
Surf the north-coast break at Kolymbithra
1.mooringTip: Go stern-to in the new harbour, roughly EUR 25-40 a night, and dig the anchor in well since the sand only holds moderately. If the Meltemi tops twenty-five knots, duck into the sheltered inner craft harbour instead.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles up to Andros, greenest of the group, where inland streams feed real waterfalls and Batsi's bay shelters you from the north.
2.description: The leg northwest runs about twenty miles, and the Andros-Tinos channel keeps the wind honest for most of it, though the passage is short enough that a stiff blow never becomes a chore. Batsi sits on the southwest coast, a compact port with room for a handful of charter boats; when it fills, Gavrio further north has far more space and shelter from any quarter. What sets Andros apart is water. Alone among the Cyclades, it runs with springs, streams and old stone bridges, and a morning inland takes you up to the Pithara waterfalls above Apikia. Chora deserves an afternoon too, its marble lanes leading down to a good contemporary art collection and the little Tourlitis lighthouse offshore. For lunch, look for froutalia, the island's potato-and-sausage omelette, at any proper kafeneio.
thingsToDo: Hike to the Pithara waterfalls above Apikia
Wander Chora's marble lanes and art museum
Try froutalia omelette at a village kafeneio
Swim at Achla, where a river meets the sea
2.mooringTip: Batsi's harbour is stern-to at about EUR 25-40 a night and well guarded from the north. If it is full or the forecast looks unsettled, motor up to Gavrio, which holds you snug from any direction.

3.shortDescription: A long thirty-two-mile downwind run southwest to Kea, home to an ancient rock-cut lion and a paddle-steamer wreck you can snorkel.
3.description: This is the longest single leg of the week, around thirty-two miles running with the Meltemi behind you, so the boat eats up the distance in comfort. Vourkari, on Kea's northwest side, makes the steadier overnight, with lazy lines and decent protection; Korissia's free town quay is fine when the wind stays under eighteen knots but chops up at the entrance once it pipes up. Get in by four and you will have the anchorage to yourself for a swim. Ashore, the walk up to the archaic Stone Lion above Ioulida is the island's set-piece, a smiling sixth-century BC beast cut straight from the bedrock. Divers and snorkellers head for the Patris, a steamship that went down off Koundouros in 1868. Book a table for octopus at one of the Vourkari ouzeris.
thingsToDo: Walk up to the archaic Stone Lion of Kea
Snorkel the Patris steamship wreck off Koundouros
Order octopus carpaccio at a Vourkari ouzeri
Stroll Ioulida's marble streets at dusk
3.mooringTip: Take lazy lines at Vourkari for EUR 25-35 a night and the calmest sleep. Korissia's quay costs nothing but sits exposed once a northerly Meltemi pushes above eighteen knots.

4.shortDescription: Twenty easy miles south to Kythnos, where hot springs pour straight into Loutra harbour and a double sandbar links the island to a bare islet.
4.description: South to Kythnos is a kind twenty miles with the wind on the quarter, an easy reach that leaves time to break the trip. Roughly halfway, the Kolona sandbar is one of the finest lunch stops in the northern Cyclades: a thin double beach joining Kythnos to the Agios Loukas islet, reachable only by boat and sheltered from the north. Drop the hook, swim across to the far side, then push on. Loutra is the night's target, a small harbour on the northeast coast where thermal water surfaces close to body temperature and runs down into the sea beside the quay. Stern-to with lazy lines here costs EUR 25-40. Inland, hire a bike or a scooter for the ridge road between Chora and Dryopida, and finish with sfougato, the local baked cheese pie, at a Dryopida taverna.
thingsToDo: Soak in the thermal springs at Loutra
Anchor and swim off the Kolona sandbar
Cycle the ridge road to Dryopida
Try sfougato cheese pie at a Dryopida taverna
4.mooringTip: Loutra takes you stern-to on lazy lines for EUR 25-40, well covered from the north. When it fills in high season, Merichas on the west coast is the reliable fallback.

5.shortDescription: A fast twenty-eight-mile reach east to Syros and Ermoupoli, the grand neoclassical capital of the Cyclades rising in pastel tiers above its quay.
5.description: Twenty-eight miles east, mostly a beam reach in the usual Meltemi, and Syros comes up fast. Ermoupoli's town quay sits deep inside a well-protected harbour and is about the most forgiving place to lie stern-to anywhere in the Cyclades, which is a genuine relief after a week of Meltemi mooring. There is no fee; you set your own anchor. The capital was once the busiest port in Greece, and it shows in the marble of Miaoulis Square and the toy-sized Apollon theatre modelled on La Scala. Two hills frame the town, Orthodox Vrontado on one and Catholic Ano Syros on the other, the latter a medieval warren worth climbing for the view and the quiet. Pick up loukoumi from one of the old confectioners before you leave. If you would rather a swim night, Foinikas on the southwest coast anchors well.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Catholic quarter of Ano Syros
Taste loukoumi at a 19th-century confectioner
Cross Miaoulis marble square after dark
Swim in sheltered Foinikas Bay
5.mooringTip: Lie stern-to on Ermoupoli's town quay on your own anchor, no charge, with good shelter from any wind. If the quay is packed, Manna marina charges EUR 60-80 a night.

6.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles back east past sacred Delos, where you can anchor for a few hours and walk the birthplace of Apollo before returning to Mykonos.
6.description: The run home is twenty-two miles east, and it passes close under Delos, so build the day around it. Anchor on the island's south side over sand in five to eight metres and you have bought three or four hours ashore on one of the great archaeological sites of the Mediterranean. Walk the Sanctuary of Apollo and the terrace of stone lions, then climb Mount Kynthos for the layout of the whole sacred island. The site closes at sundown and nobody stays overnight. Aim to have the boat back in Tourlos New Marina by five for the handover-prep evening, and reserve the berth online ahead of time. If you skip Delos, Ornos or Psarou on the Mykonos south coast make for an easy afternoon swim and a beach lunch instead.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Delos for the archaeological site
Walk the Apollo sanctuary and lion terrace
Climb Mount Kynthos for the island view
Beach lunch at Agios Sostis on Mykonos
6.mooringTip: Book the Tourlos New Marina berth online in advance; it runs EUR 70-110 a night at peak. Only lie on the Mykonos town quay if the northerly stays under eighteen knots.

7.shortDescription: An early handover at Tourlos before nine, a last swim at nearby Agios Stefanos, and a ten-minute road transfer to Mykonos airport.
7.description: The last day is straightforward. Have the boat alongside at Tourlos before nine for the handover, and stay aboard for the inspection so you can point out anything already logged and photograph any noted damage before you sign. Deposits are usually returned within a week once the base is satisfied. There is no need to rush off, though. Agios Stefanos beach is five minutes from the marina and makes a fitting last swim before the transfer, and there is time for a proper coffee in Mykonos town if your flight is later. The airport sits only about ten minutes by road from Tourlos, so onward connections are easy. Leave the marina tidy and the fuel topped as agreed, and the whole checkout runs quickly.
thingsToDo: Run the final inspection with the base manager
Take a last swim at Agios Stefanos beach
Grab a coffee in Mykonos town
Transfer to JMK airport, ten minutes away
7.mooringTip: Be alongside at Tourlos before nine and stay for the inspection. Photograph any damage the base notes before signing, and expect the deposit back within seven days.

mykonos-naxos-paros-kea-14-days.metaTitle: 14-Day Cyclades Grand Loop from Mykonos | Boat4You
mykonos-naxos-paros-kea-14-days.metaDesc: A two-week Cyclades circuit out of Mykonos, calling at Naxos, Paros, Milos, Sifnos, Serifos, Kythnos, Kea, Andros, Tinos and Syros.

1.shortDescription: A fast 22-mile reach to Naxos, greenest of the Cyclades, where the Kastro lanes and the marble Portara gate make an easy first evening.
1.description: The season's first passage runs 22 miles down from Mykonos, and if the Meltemi is up you'll cover it fast on a broad reach. Naxos is the big one, greenest and most fertile of the Cyclades, with the Tragea valley and Mount Zas climbing past the thousand-metre mark inland. The town harbour takes stern-to lines easily and stays calm in almost any blow, so there's no rush to grab a spot. Set the crew loose in the old Kastro lanes before dark. Everyone ends up at the Portara for sunset, a lone marble gateway left standing from an Apollo temple the ancients never finished, planted on the islet just off the harbour mouth. If you got an early start, Delos is worth a swing on the way past.
thingsToDo: Sunset behind the Portara marble gate
Hike Mount Zas from Filoti village
Kitron tasting at Vallindras distillery, Halki
Swim the long sand at Agios Prokopios
Graviera and potatoes in a Kastro taverna
1.mooringTip: Stern-to on the town quay, roughly €30-50 a night, with good holding in any wind direction. Slots rarely fill even in August, so you can arrive mid-afternoon without stress.

2.shortDescription: A lazy six-mile hop to Paros, with a turquoise swim in the Antiparos channel and the ancient Ekatontapiliani church waiting in Parikia.
2.description: Barely a passage, this one, six miles across the channel with the sails hardly warmed up. The turquoise shallows between Paros and Antiparos are too good to sail straight past, so most crews drop the hook off the Antiparos side for a long swim and a leisurely lunch first. Parikia is the practical overnight: a big, forgiving quay and lazy lines, far less of a scrum than pretty Naoussa up north. In town, the Ekatontapiliani draws you in, a low whitewashed Byzantine church behind courtyard walls, its stonework older than most of what you'll see all fortnight. Save an evening for Lefkes if you can rent a scooter; the old marble lanes catch the last light well, and the tavernas up there feed you away from the harbour crowd.
thingsToDo: Swim the turquoise Paros–Antiparos channel
Wander the Byzantine Ekatontapiliani church
Marble lanes of Lefkes at dusk
Wine tasting at Moraitis winery, Naoussa
Seafood on the old Naoussa quay
2.mooringTip: Take stern-to lines in Parikia, around €30-50 a night, bigger and calmer than Naoussa, which fills fast and turns tight in a blow. If you want Naoussa's postcard harbour, get in before early afternoon.

3.shortDescription: The long 38-mile leg southwest to Milos, a drowned volcano whose deep inlet flattens the sea at Adamas — pause at moonscape Sarakiniko if calm.
3.description: The long leg of the trip, and the one to start early, is 38 miles southwest, a full day on a beam reach when the Meltemi is filling in. The reward waits at the far end. Milos is a drowned volcanic crater, and its inlet cuts so deep that once you're tucked into Adamas the sea goes flat whatever's happening outside. Break the passage at Sarakiniko if the wind's kind: white rock scoured smooth into something closer to a moonscape, with deep clear water for jumping in. It sits open to the swell, though, so only stop when it's genuinely calm. Ashore, Adamas has the tavernas and the dive shops; up the hill, Plaka's castle rock gives you the whole gulf at sunset. Leave time for the syrmata at Klima.
thingsToDo: Swim the moonscape rocks at Sarakiniko
Snorkel the Papafragas sea caves
Sunset from Plaka's castle rock
Photograph the Klima syrmata boathouses
Farm-table dinner at O! Hamos taverna
3.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Adamas town quay, about €30-50, with first-class shelter from every direction once you're inside the inlet. Pollonia in the northeast is cheaper but lies open to a northerly Meltemi.

4.shortDescription: An easy 18 miles north to Sifnos, the best table in the Cyclades — moor at Kamares, or duck to Vathi if the wind clocks west.
4.description: An easy 18 miles north brings you to the island that eats better than any other in the Cyclades. Kamares, the ferry port on the west coast, is the natural overnight, well tucked from the north and northeast where the Meltemi usually sits. Watch the forecast, though. By late August the wind sometimes swings a touch west, and when it does the swell curls straight into Kamares and the night turns rolly; that's your cue to slip round to Vathi on the south coast, which stays glassy. Sifnos gave Greece its first celebrity cook, and it shows on every menu: chickpea stew slow-baked overnight in a clay mastelo, lamb braised with dill and red wine, honey-cheese pies. The whitewashed lanes of Apollonia and Artemonas climb the ridge above, and the pottery that made those cooking pots is still thrown here.
thingsToDo: Clay-baked chickpea revithada in Artemonas
Walk to Chrysopigi monastery by Faros
Throw a pot at a Sifnos workshop
Swim the sheltered south bay at Vathi
Sunset over medieval Kastro on its cliff
4.mooringTip: Anchor on sand at 5-8 metres in Kamares or take the little quay for €20-30. When the Meltemi picks up a westerly slant, move to Vathi on the south coast, which is fully closed off from that direction.

5.shortDescription: Fourteen miles north to Serifos, where Livadi funnels the Meltemi — dig the anchor in well before climbing the donkey path to that cube-stacked Chora.
5.description: Fourteen miles north, likely on the wind, and worth reading the anchorage carefully when you get there. Livadi is a long, sandy bay on the south coast that funnels the Meltemi down its length, so you can find yourself sitting in more breeze than you'd expect. The sand holds well once you're properly dug in, so lay plenty of scope, dive on the anchor to be sure, and hang around aboard for the first hour before anyone heads ashore. Dragging is the classic Serifos mistake. Once you trust the hook, the reward is one of the finest hilltop Choras in the Aegean, stacked in white cubes above the port and reached by a stepped donkey path that takes about half an hour to climb. Time it for the windmills at sunset.
thingsToDo: Climb the donkey path to Chora
Chickpea revithada at a village kafeneio
Swim the fine sand at Psili Ammos
Sunset among the Chora windmills
Old iron mines at Megalo Livadi
5.mooringTip: Anchor on sand at 5-8 metres in Livadi with long scope, or go stern-to on the town quay for €20-30. Above about 22 knots the drag risk is real, so dive the anchor before you leave the boat.

6.shortDescription: A lay-day on Serifos: reposition for a swim at Vagia or Psili Ammos, walk up to Chora, and poke around the old Megalo Livadi mines.
6.description: No passage today, and after Milos you'll have earned it. Serifos rewards a slow day. Depending on where the wind's sitting, lift the hook and reposition for a swim, Vagia on the north coast tucks in behind the land when it's blowing from the south, while Psili Ammos on the south side stays calm under a northerly. Back at anchor, put the crew ashore and walk the old donkey path up to Chora, maybe forty minutes of stone steps, and drop back down by the road. Lunch at a kafeneio table on the ridge, looking out over the whole south coast. If there's an afternoon to spare, the abandoned iron mines at Megalo Livadi make a strange, quiet ramble, with rusting machinery and an old loading pier from the island's mining century. Overnight back on the Livadi sand.
thingsToDo: Day-anchor swim at Vagia Beach
Stone donkey path up to Chora
Rusting mine ruins at Megalo Livadi
Kafeneio lunch on the Chora ridge
Windmill sunset above the port
6.mooringTip: Stay put on the Livadi sand or shift to a day-anchor at Vagia or Psili Ammos depending on the wind, then come back for the night. Long scope everywhere on this island.

7.shortDescription: Fifteen miles north to Kythnos, easy mooring at Merichas — but if it's calm, push on to the pale Kolona sandbar you reach only by sea.
7.description: Fifteen miles up to Kythnos, and the real prize sits a little further on. Merichas is the workaday ferry port on the west coast, an easy stern-to and the sensible choice if the wind's building. Break the run with lunch at anchor in Apokrousi cove, snug under a northerly. But if the day's calm and you've time in hand, push another six miles north to Kolona, a spit of pale sand joining the island to a low islet, water either side, reachable only from the sea. It's the anchorage everyone remembers. Kythnos is the one Cycladic island with genuine hot springs, and at Loutra the thermal water runs warm straight into the sea. Inland, the tile-roofed lanes of Dryopida are worth a wander, with a taverna or two doing sfougato, the local baked cheese.
thingsToDo: Anchor lunch in Apokrousi cove
Swim the Kolona sandbar detour
Soak in the Loutra thermal springs
Sfougato in a Dryopida taverna
Wander the tile-roofed lanes of Dryopida
7.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Merichas town quay, roughly €20-30, and reasonably tucked from a northerly Meltemi. Loutra on the north coast is the alternative if you want the hot springs on the doorstep.

8.shortDescription: Fifteen miles north to oak-clad Kea; take the lazy lines at Vourkari, tie up by four, and walk out to the smiling archaic Lion.
8.description: Another fifteen miles north, back toward the mainland's edge, to green and oak-clad Kea. Two harbours face you at the top of the island. Vourkari, the little yachting cove on the northwest, has lazy lines and better shelter for the night; Korissia next door is the bigger free quay, fine as long as the Meltemi stays under about 18 knots. Either way, aim to be tied up before four in the afternoon, since once it's blowing over twenty a swell works into the Korissia entrance and makes the approach awkward. Vourkari's waterfront is one of the better places to eat on the whole route, a row of ouzeris doing octopus and small plates. Up the hill, the old capital Ioulida rambles across two ridges, and a short walk beyond it stands the archaic Lion of Kea, carved smiling from the living rock.
thingsToDo: Walk out to the archaic Lion of Kea
Snorkel the Patris wreck off Koundouros
Octopus and ouzo on the Vourkari quay
Marble lanes of Ioulida at dusk
Hike the old stone paths inland
8.mooringTip: Stern-to with lazy lines in Vourkari, about €25-35, is the calmer bet overnight. Korissia's town quay is free but sits open to a northerly above 18 knots, and its entrance builds a swell by mid-afternoon.

9.shortDescription: A 32-mile beam reach northeast to Andros, the green one — springs, streams and stone bridges inland, with an easy port at Batsi.
9.description: A proper sailing day: 32 miles east-northeast, a long beam reach if the Meltemi cooperates, angling up toward the top of the island chain. Andros catches you off guard. Where the other Cyclades are bare and burnt, this one runs with water, springs bubbling in the villages, streams tumbling to the sea, old stone footbridges you'd expect in the mainland mountains. Batsi on the southwest coast is the small, friendly charter port and tucks in nicely from the north; if it's full, Gavrio further up is bigger and holds you well in anything. The island rewards a car. Drive inland to the Pithara falls above Apikia, wander the marble spine of Chora with its surprisingly good contemporary art museum, and find a kafeneio doing froutalia, the hearty herb-and-potato omelette that turns up nowhere else quite like this.
thingsToDo: Hike inland to the Pithara waterfalls
Chora's marble street and modern art museum
Froutalia omelette at a village kafeneio
Swim the river estuary at Achla
Cross an old stone footbridge inland
9.mooringTip: Stern-to in Batsi, about €25-40, with good shelter from the north. If the quay's crowded, Gavrio a few miles up is roomier and protected from any direction.

10.shortDescription: Twenty miles southeast to Tinos through a wind-funnelling channel — marble-carving heartland and Orthodox pilgrimage island, with dovecotes and boulder villages inland.
10.description: Twenty miles southeast, and the channel between Andros and Tinos has a reputation, since it squeezes and accelerates the Meltemi like a funnel, so expect the strongest wind of the day here even though the hop itself is short. Reef early and enjoy it. Tinos is two things at once: the marble-carving heartland of the Aegean and the holiest island in Greek Orthodoxy, its great pilgrimage church looming above the port. Inland, it's quieter and stranger than the coast suggests. Hundreds of ornate Venetian dovecotes stud the terraced valleys around Falatados, the boulder-strewn village of Volax looks half-invented, and up at Pyrgos the marble workshops still turn out reliefs by hand. Eat the artichokes if they're in season, braised the old Constantinople way, and don't leave without trying the local louza and kopanisti.
thingsToDo: Marble workshops at Pyrgos village
Dovecote trail above Falatados
Braised artichokes à la polita
Boulder-strewn village of Volax at sunset
Light a candle at Panagia Evangelistria
10.mooringTip: Stern-to in the new harbour, about €25-40, on moderate sand, so set the anchor with plenty of scope and check it holds. When the Meltemi tops 25 knots, the inner small-craft basin is noticeably calmer.

11.shortDescription: A car loop through inland Tinos: marble sculptors at Pyrgos, the boulder houses of Volax, a surf at Kolymbithra, sunset over Agios Fokas.
11.description: A day off the boat to get properly into the interior. Grab a rental car in town and make a loop of it. Pyrgos first, up in the marble north, where you can watch sculptors at the Museum of Marble Crafts and see the work that keeps the tradition alive; there's a good taverna in the square for lunch. Wind back down through Volax, where houses sit wedged among boulders the size of cars, then out to Kolymbithra on the north coast, two sandy bays that pick up enough swell for a surf when the Meltemi's running, or an easy swim when it isn't. Save the evening for Agios Fokas, just south of town, where the light drops behind the ridgeline over the water. Back aboard for a second night in the harbour.
thingsToDo: Museum of Marble Crafts at Pyrgos
Boulder houses of Volax village
Surf or swim the twin Kolymbithra bays
Taverna lunch in a Falatados square
Sunset over the water at Agios Fokas
11.mooringTip: Stay in the new harbour or the inner basin for a second night. If you fancy a swim break first, day-anchor at Kolymbithra when it's sheltered from the south, or at Agios Fokas, before heading back in to re-moor.

12.shortDescription: A lively fourteen-mile reach to Syros, where deep Ermoupoli offers the easiest stern-to of the trip below a neoclassical capital on two hills.
12.description: Fourteen miles west, quick and lively, since the Tinos-Syros channel does the same funnelling trick and you'll carry a good five knots more wind than the open sea, so it's a spirited beam reach across. Ermoupoli makes up for it. The harbour runs deep and the long town quay is about the most forgiving stern-to on the whole trip; you drop your own anchor and pay nothing. And what a town to arrive into: the 19th-century capital of the Cyclades, all pale neoclassical façades stepping up two hills, the marble expanse of Miaouli Square at its heart with a miniature opera house on one side. Climb the lanes to the medieval Catholic quarter of Ano Syros for the view back down. Buy loukoumi and halvadopita where they've been making it since the 1830s.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Catholic quarter of Ano Syros
Loukoumi from an 1830s confectioner
Marble Miaouli Square after dark
Swim the rock platforms at Asteria
Rebetiko night in an Ano Syros bar
12.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Ermoupoli town quay costs nothing and you use your own anchor, with fine shelter from any direction. If the quay's full, Manna marina takes you for €60-80 a night.

13.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles back east to Mykonos, breaking at sacred Delos for its marble lions and mosaics, then a farewell dinner in Little Venice.
13.description: The last real sailing day loops 22 miles back east to Mykonos, and it passes close by Delos, reason enough to break the passage. Drop anchor in the strait and take the tender across for a few hours ashore on the sacred island where Apollo was said to be born: the ranked marble lions on their terrace, the mosaic floors of merchant houses, a whole abandoned town in the sun. It closes at sundown and no one stays the night, so watch the clock. Aim to be into Tourlos marina by five, which leaves the evening free to sort gear for tomorrow's handover. Book the berth online ahead of time in July and August, since it fills. Then head into Chora for a farewell dinner along the waterfront at Little Venice, waves breaking under the balconies.
thingsToDo: Tender across to the ruins of Delos
Marble lion terrace and House of Cleopatra
Farewell dinner in Little Venice
Sunset drink at Scorpios beach club
Photograph the windmills above Chora
13.mooringTip: Stern-to in Tourlos New Marina, €70-110 a night at peak, and book the slot online well ahead, as it sells out in summer. The Mykonos town quay is only safe under about 18 knots of northerly.

14.shortDescription: Handover at Tourlos by nine — walk the boat with the manager, then a last swim at Agios Stefanos before the short run to the airport.
14.description: Handover morning. Bases like the boat back clean and empty by nine, so get the packing done the night before and give the cabins a last wipe-through early. Walk the boat with the base manager rather than leaving them to it, going over the hull, the tender and the inventory together, and photograph anything they flag before you sign so there's no argument later. The deposit comes back within a week once the inspection's done. If your flight's not until afternoon, Tourlos leaves you time for a last swim; Agios Stefanos is a short walk round the bay, an easy sandy beach to close the fortnight on. Mykonos airport is barely ten minutes up the road, so there's no need to rush off. One more frappé in town before the transfer.
thingsToDo: Final walkthrough with the base manager
Last swim at Agios Stefanos beach
Farewell frappé in a Chora café
Ten-minute transfer to Mykonos airport
14.mooringTip: Hand the boat over at Tourlos before nine. Photograph any damage the manager notes before you sign, and expect the deposit back within seven days of the inspection.

mykonos-naxos-paros.metaDesc: Sail a 7-day charter loop from Mykonos through Naxos, Paros, Sifnos, Serifos and Syros, a central Cyclades route with the Meltemi at your back.

1.shortDescription: A 22-mile run south with the Meltemi behind you, ending at Naxos town where the Portara frames the best sunset in the group.
1.description: Cast off from Tourlos by mid-morning and let the northerly do the work. The 22 miles down to Naxos are a proper reach in a standard Meltemi, boat settled on one tack, Delos and Rineia sliding past to starboard. We usually aim to be tied up before six so there's time ashore. Naxos town harbour takes you stern-to along the outer mole, and it holds against anything the Aegean throws at it, which is more than you can say for most Cycladic quays. Walk the causeway out to Palatia islet in the last hour of light. The Portara, a lone marble doorway left over from a temple the Naxians never finished, stands there catching the sun as it drops behind Paros. Bring a bottle. Half the crews on the island do the same.
thingsToDo: Sunset drinks at the Portara doorway
Climb Mount Zas from Filoti village
Taste kitron liqueur at Vallindras, Halki
Swim the sand at Agios Prokopios
1.mooringTip: Stern-to on the outer mole in Naxos town, around €30-50 a night, with genuine all-round shelter. There's usually room even in August, so no need to book, but arrive before the afternoon rush for a spot near the steps.

2.shortDescription: A lazy six-mile crossing to Paros with a long swim stop in the Antiparos channel before you settle into Parikia for the night.
2.description: There's no rush today. Six miles across the channel is barely two hours, so most crews drop the hook off Antiparos first and swim the morning away. The strait between the two islands runs shallow and clear over pale sand, and the holding is good enough to leave the boat and take the tender ashore for a coffee. When you're ready, it's a short reach up to Parikia, the working harbour on the west side. Tie stern-to here rather than fighting for space in prettier Naoussa; the room is real and the walk into town short. Behind the waterfront sits the Ekatontapiliani, a Byzantine church older than most of what you'll see this week. Come evening, the old-town lanes fill up, and the marble-paved village of Lefkes is worth a taxi for dinner.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim the Antiparos channel
See the Byzantine Ekatontapiliani in Parikia
Wander the marble lanes of Lefkes
Wine tasting at Moraitis winery, Naoussa
2.mooringTip: Parikia is stern-to on your own anchor, roughly €30-50, and easier to get into than Naoussa's tight fishing harbour. If you want the postcard, Naoussa is the alternative, but arrive early and expect to raft or wait.

3.shortDescription: Twenty-four miles west across open water to Sifnos, the eating island, dropping anchor in the sheltered sandy scoop of Vathi bay.
3.description: This is the longest open stretch of the western half, twenty-four miles with nothing to hide behind, so watch the forecast and leave by nine. In a fresh Meltemi you'll be hard on the wind for the last few miles into Sifnos. Vathi, on the south coast, is the anchorage I'd pick every time: a near-enclosed bay with a sand bottom that holds, salt pans behind the beach, and calm even when it's honking outside. Kamares, round on the west side, is the fallback quay if the wind swings westerly. Sifnos is where the Cyclades takes food seriously. Book a table inland at Artemonas for mastelo, lamb slow-cooked in a clay pot, or track down revithada, the chickpea stew locals bake overnight in a wood oven. Both taste like the island invented them.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in sheltered Vathi bay
Walk to Chrysopigi monastery near Faros
Mastelo lamb at a taverna in Artemonas
Watch a Vathi potter throw a pot
3.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in 4-6 metres inside Vathi and you're set for any northerly. Kamares is a stern-to quay at €20-30 but it's open to the west, so only use it when the Meltemi has no westerly bite.

4.shortDescription: A short fourteen-mile beat north to Serifos, where the whitewashed Chora rides the ridge above a bay that likes to test your anchor.
4.description: Fourteen miles, but they're into the teeth of the Meltemi, so it's a beat rather than a cruise. Livadi bay opens to the south and the wind funnels straight down the valley into it, which makes the anchoring the real work of the day. Set plenty of scope on the sand, back down hard, and dive the anchor to see it dug in before anyone goes ashore. Sitting the first hour aboard has saved more than one crew a midnight scramble here; drag is the classic Serifos story. Once you trust the hook, look up. Chora sits on the skyline in a tumble of white cubes, half an hour up the old donkey path or a quick taxi. The windmills at the top catch the last light, and the kafeneia in the square do a proper revithada.
thingsToDo: Hike the donkey path up to Chora
Sunset by the windmills above Chora
Swim at Psili Ammos beach
Chickpea revithada in a Chora kafeneio
4.mooringTip: Anchor in Livadi on sand at 5-8 metres with long scope, and dive it to confirm before leaving the boat. Once the wind tops 22 knots the bay gets bouncy; if you'd rather sleep easy, take the town quay stern-to at €20-30.

5.shortDescription: The big leg: thirty-two miles on a beam reach up to Syros and Ermoupoli, the grandest and most forgiving town quay in the Cyclades.
5.description: The longest day of the loop, thirty-two miles northeast, and in a standard Meltemi it's a fast beam reach that eats the distance. Aim to be off Syros by mid-afternoon; the wind tends to build through the day and you want the anchor set before it does. Ermoupoli sits at the head of a deep, well-sheltered harbour, and the town quay is about as easy as stern-to gets in these islands, forgiving even when you're tired. This was the richest port in Greece once, and it shows. Marble mansions climb the two hills above the water, Orthodox Vrontado on one and Catholic Ano Syros on the other, capped by the church of San Giorgio. Walk up Miaouli square at dusk, then hunt down a confectioner for loukoumi and halvadopita, the sweets the island has made for a century and a half.
thingsToDo: Climb the lanes to Catholic Ano Syros
Loukoumi and halvadopita at an old confectioner
Evening stroll across Miaouli marble square
Swim off the rock platforms at Asteria
5.mooringTip: Ermoupoli town quay is free and you lie to your own anchor with shelter from every direction. Get in by 17:00 when the wind's building and set long scope on the sand. If the quay's full, Manna marina takes you at €60-80.

6.shortDescription: An easy twenty-two miles back east to Mykonos, with the option to anchor off the sanctuary island of Delos on the way in.
6.description: The return leg is the gentlest of the week, twenty-two miles east with the wind now on the quarter. Break the trip at Delos if you can time it right. The whole island is a guarded archaeological reserve, so the stop is about walking the sanctuary of Apollo and the Terrace of the Lions rather than lingering, and the site shuts at sundown. Plan it so you're still into Tourlos by five, which leaves the evening free to sort the boat before handover. New Marina takes you stern-to, and in July or August you'll want the slot booked online in advance because it fills. Save the last night for a walk into town. Eat along the water in Little Venice as the sea slaps the house walls, then wander up to the windmills for the crowds and the light.
thingsToDo: Walk the Terrace of the Lions on Delos
See the Apollo sanctuary ruins
Dinner on the water in Little Venice
Sunset drinks below the Kato Mili windmills
6.mooringTip: Tourlos New Marina is stern-to at €70-110 a night in peak season, and you should reserve the berth online before you sail. The old town quay only works under about 18 knots of northerly, so don't count on it.

7.shortDescription: Handover at Tourlos before nine, a last swim at Agios Stefanos, and a ten-minute road transfer to Mykonos airport.
7.description: Nothing dramatic on the last morning, but a few habits pay off. Be alongside at Tourlos and ready to hand over before nine, and walk the boat with the base manager rather than leaving them to it. Photograph anything that gets noted before you sign, and the deposit comes back within a week without argument. If your flight is later, there's no reason to sit at the marina. Agios Stefanos beach is five minutes up the road for a final swim, and the town cafés do a decent coffee if you'd rather watch the harbour wake up. Mykonos airport is only about ten minutes away by taxi, so you can leave the marina fairly late and still make an afternoon departure in good time. A soft landing after a week of proper Meltemi sailing.
thingsToDo: Final walk-through with the base manager
Last swim at Agios Stefanos beach
Harbour-side coffee in Mykonos town
Ten-minute taxi to JMK airport
7.mooringTip: Hand back at Tourlos before nine so the base has time to inspect. Photograph any damage the manager flags before signing the report, and expect the deposit released within seven days.

paros-ios-santorini-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks under sail from Paros: Naxos, the Small Cyclades, Santorini, Milos, Sifnos, Kythnos and Syros before the run home. A full Cyclades circuit.

1.shortDescription: A gentle six-mile warm-up east to Naxos, where the marble Portara frames the sunset and the town quay forgives beginners.
1.description:
Six miles is barely a passage, and that is the point on day one. You clear Paros, settle the crew into the winches, and Naxos town opens up before anyone has found their sea legs. The harbour holds you steady whatever the wind does, so this is the night to relax rather than fuss over ground tackle.

Ashore, walk out to the Portara at dusk. It is a single marble doorway left standing from a temple to Apollo that was never finished, and the low sun behind it does the rest. With a spare morning, the climb up Mount Zas takes about four hours and lays the whole island out below you. Down in Halki, the old distilleries pour kitro, a citron liqueur you will not find bottled anywhere else.
thingsToDo: Watch sunset through the marble Portara
Climb Mount Zas, Zeus's birthplace (4h)
Taste kitro citron liqueur in Halki
Swim the sands at Agios Prokopios
1.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Naxos town quay, roughly EUR 30-50 a night, and it shelters from every direction. Even in August you will usually find a slot without racing for it.

2.shortDescription: A longer reach to Donousa, the loneliest of the Small Cyclades, where a hundred islanders share one harbour and Kedros Beach.
2.description:
Twenty-four miles east-southeast puts real distance between you and the crowds. Donousa has around a hundred permanent residents, and once the afternoon ferry leaves, the island belongs to whoever stayed. Aim for the little quay at Stavros, but keep the anchor ready; slots are few, and dropping the hook in the bay outside is no hardship at all.

Kedros Beach sits a short walk south of the village, the water shading from milk to deep blue over pale sand. Walkers can follow the track up to Kalotaritissa, a cluster of stone houses that looks unchanged in decades. For lunch, look for gouna, mackerel split and dried in the sun then grilled over coals at a beach shack. Simple, salty, exactly right after a passage.
thingsToDo: Long swim at Kedros Beach
Hike up to Kalotaritissa stone hamlet
Snorkel into the Cave of the Wall
Sun-dried gouna grilled at a beach shack
2.mooringTip: Berthing stern-to at Stavros costs EUR 15-25, but the few slots fill fast; if you miss out, the bay just outside holds well on sand in 5-7 m. Either choice keeps the northerlies off you.

3.shortDescription: South to Amorgos, where a white monastery is glued to a sheer cliff and the ridge villages hide the good tavernas.
3.description:
Amorgos earns its reputation the moment Hozoviotissa comes into view, an eight-storey monastery from 1088 pressed flat against a cliff that drops straight to the sea. Getting up there means around three hundred steps from the road, and the monks still hand visitors a shot of psimeni raki at the top. Wear something that covers knees and shoulders or you will be turned back.

Aegiali, up on the north coast, gives good shelter when the Meltemi blows; if it swings south, run round to Katapola instead. Divers can drop onto the Olympia wreck off Liveros, the ship from the Besson film that made this island famous. Evenings belong to the hill village of Tholaria, where slow-cooked goat comes to the table without ceremony.
thingsToDo: Climb the 300 steps to Hozoviotissa
Dive to the Olympia wreck at Liveros
Goat and psimeni raki in Tholaria
Follow the ridge path above Aegiali
3.mooringTip: Aegiali takes you stern-to for EUR 25-40 and shelters from the north. When the wind clocks into the south, shift to Katapola on the other side of the island.

4.shortDescription: West to Koufonissi, the pocket-sized island that draws the yacht crowd for Pori's lagoon and lobster spaghetti on the quay.
4.description:
Koufonissi is small and knows its worth, so plan to arrive early. The village and quay sit on Pano Koufonissi; the sister islet across the strait, Kato Koufonissi, is empty apart from a chapel and a taverna that opens in season. Between them lies Pori, a shallow lagoon where the water turns an almost unreal turquoise over white sand. Anchor off it for a long lunchtime swim.

The southeast coast is cut with low cliffs and sea caves worth exploring with a mask and fins. Back on the quay come evening, order astakomakaronada, lobster broken into a tangle of spaghetti, the dish every second table seems to be sharing. Walk it off through the whitewashed lanes before the night ferries reshuffle the harbour.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in Pori lagoon
Snorkel the southeast coast sea caves
Astakomakaronada lobster spaghetti for dinner
Wander Pano Koufonissi's whitewashed lanes
4.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Pano Koufonissi quay costs EUR 25-40, but the slots are usually gone by 17:00. If it is full, anchor on sand at 4-7 m just east of the village.

5.shortDescription: A five-mile hop to Schoinousa, two hundred souls, a harbour at the foot and a whitewashed Chora on the ridge above.
5.description:
Barely five miles separate Schoinousa from Koufonissi, but they feel like different decades. Two hundred people live here, most of them up in Chora, the single hilltop village reached by a short climb from the harbour at Mersini. There is no rush to anything.

Tsigouri Beach lies just around from the port, tucked in enough to stay calm when the north wind is up, and the swimming is easy over sand. In the village, find a taverna table under a tamarisk and order tomato keftedes, fritters of grated tomato, onion and herbs fried until the edges catch. Walkers with an afternoon to spare can follow the path out to the little mountain church of Panagia for a view that takes in half the archipelago.
thingsToDo: Swim sheltered Tsigouri Beach near the port
Climb to Chora on the ridge
Tomato keftedes at a village taverna
Walk out to the Panagia church
5.mooringTip: Mersini takes you stern-to for EUR 15-25, though the slots are limited. Failing that, anchor on sand at 4-6 m just east of the harbour, well sheltered from the north.

6.shortDescription: Seven miles on to Irakleia, one road, one taverna, and the clearest swimming water in the whole Small Cyclades.
6.description:
If you want to know what the Cyclades felt like fifty years ago, Irakleia is the answer. A hundred people, one road, one taverna doing dinner, and a pace that slows you down whether you planned it or not. Most crews drop the hook in Agios Georgios bay rather than fight for the short quay.

Livadi beach, a walk from the harbour, holds water so clear the anchor chain reads like it is hanging in air. Give yourself half a day for the hike to the Blue Cave, roughly thirty minutes from the village and worth the scramble when the light comes through the entrance. Up above, the ruined kastro from the pirate years is a quiet place to watch the sun go down.
thingsToDo: Hike thirty minutes to the Blue Cave
Swim the clear water at Livadi
Grilled gouna at the island's one taverna
Walk up to the pirate-era kastro
6.mooringTip: For the quietest night, drop the hook on sand in Agios Georgios bay at 5-7 m, where the northerlies can't reach. Quay berths are scarce, EUR 15-25 with no lazy lines, so pack a pair of good long stern warps.

7.shortDescription: A thirty-two-mile reach south to Santorini, with a swim off Ios on the way and the caldera saved for daylight.
7.description:
This is the biggest day of the fortnight, thirty-two miles south down the channel with the Meltemi usually giving you a fast, steady reach. Break it with a swim at Manganari on the south coast of Ios if the timing works. Aim to raise Santorini with plenty of light left, because the caldera is far too deep to anchor and the marina at Vlychada is the only sensible berth.

Get in before five and you have the evening for the island. A taxi up to Oia buys you the famous cliff-edge sunset, though the crowds are real; the wineries are the quieter pleasure. Santo Wines and Domaine Sigalas both pour the crisp, mineral Assyrtiko the volcanic soil produces. Order fava with capers somewhere in Megalochori.
thingsToDo: Sunset from the cliffs at Oia
Assyrtiko tasting at Santo Wines or Sigalas
Swim the volcanic Red Beach
Order fava with capers in Megalochori
7.mooringTip: Vlychada Marina is stern-to at EUR 60-100 a night and must be booked ahead in high season. The caldera runs over 300 m deep, so anchoring off the towns is simply not an option.

8.shortDescription: Upwind to Folegandros, the first real beat of the trip, rewarded by a cliff-top Chora and the boat-only cove at Katergo.
8.description:
Folegandros is where the wind turns honest. Twenty-two miles northwest with the Meltemi on the nose means a proper beat, so leave early and be ready to tack. The reward is one of the finest Choras in the islands, perched on a cliff edge two hundred metres above the port and reached by a stiff half-hour up the old kalderimi.

Karavostasi, the harbour, shelters well from the north but turns nasty in a southerly, so read the forecast before committing. Katergo, a strip of shingle reachable only by boat or a long walk, is the swim of the day. Come evening, look for matsata, hand-rolled pasta under a goat ragu, served in one of the vine-shaded courtyards up in Chora.
thingsToDo: Climb the kalderimi to cliff-top Chora
Swim the shingle at Katergo cove
Hand-rolled matsata with goat ragu
Walk the old path to Ano Meria
8.mooringTip: Karavostasi is stern-to at EUR 25-40 with limited slots, and it only works in northerlies; avoid it altogether when the wind is in the south. If the quay fills, anchor east of the port on sand at 4-7 m.

9.shortDescription: One more beat northwest to Milos, where the moon-white cliffs of Sarakiniko and a deep, safe bay at Adamas wait.
9.description:
Another twenty miles to windward, and then Milos rewards the effort. The long inlet leading to Adamas gives you the best-protected overnight anywhere in the western Cyclades, which matters when the Meltemi is running hard outside. Tuck up at the town quay and let the crew off to explore.

Sarakiniko is the sight everyone comes for, a headland of wind-smoothed white rock that could pass for the surface of the moon; swim there only when the wind lies down, as it is wide open. Snorkel the Papafragas caves, then climb to Plaka Castle late in the day for a view down the whole gulf. Down at Klima, the fishermen's syrmata, boat garages painted every colour, make the best photo on the island.
thingsToDo: Swim the white moonscape at Sarakiniko
Snorkel the Papafragas sea caves
Climb Plaka Castle for the panorama
Photograph the Klima syrmata boat garages
9.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Adamas town quay costs EUR 30-50 and holds you steady in any wind. Pollonia on the northeast coast is cheaper but lies exposed when the Meltemi blows from the north.

10.shortDescription: Eighteen miles north to Sifnos, the island Greeks come to for the cooking, from clay-pot lamb to the potteries of Vathi.
10.description:
Sifnos is the kitchen of the Cyclades, the home island of the cookbook writer Nikolaos Tselementes, and you eat well here without trying. Anchor decisions come down to wind: Vathi on the south coast is the calmest hole to swing in, while Kamares on the west gives you a bigger paid quay. If the breeze clocks round to the west, move to Vathi and sleep easy.

The signature dish is mastelo, lamb or goat slow-baked with wine and dill in a sealed clay pot, best eaten up in Artemonas. Walk out to Chrysopigi, the small monastery on its own rocky spit by Faros, at the golden hour. Vathi still keeps a couple of working potteries turning out the local clay.
thingsToDo: Mastelo clay-pot lamb in Artemonas
Sunset at Chrysopigi monastery, Faros
Visit a working pottery in Vathi
Swim the calm water of Vathi Bay
10.mooringTip: Vathi gives good holding on sand at 4-6 m and stays calm; alternatively the Kamares quay is stern-to at EUR 20-30. When the wind backs into the west, Vathi is the safer bet.

11.shortDescription: A long thirty-two-mile grind north past Serifos to Kythnos, where hot springs run straight into the harbour at Loutra.
11.description:
This is a working day, thirty-two miles north into the wind, so plan on two long port tacks and get away early. Break the leg at Serifos if you can, dropping anchor off Vagia for a lunch swim while the beach stays sheltered from the south. Then push on to Kythnos.

The prize at Loutra is unusual: natural thermal springs that surface right at the harbour and warm the shallows where they meet the sea. Soak after the day's sailing. With extra time, the Kolona sandbar on the northwest tip is a short detour to one of the loveliest anchorages in the group, a tombolo of sand joining two islands. Inland, the old capital Driopida hides tavernas doing sfougato, a baked cheese pie.
thingsToDo: Soak in the Loutra thermal springs
Lunch swim off Serifos at Vagia
Detour to the Kolona tombolo swim
Baked sfougato cheese pie in Driopida
11.mooringTip: Loutra takes you stern-to for EUR 25-40 with lazy lines and shelters from the north. If it is full, Merichas on the west coast is the fallback.

12.shortDescription: A long beam reach east to Syros, whose grand capital Ermoupoli climbs the hill in pastel tiers above the friendliest quay around.
12.description:
Syros comes as a surprise after two weeks of small islands. Ermoupoli was the great port of Greece in the nineteenth century, and its wealth still shows in the marble, the opera house and the neoclassical facades stacked up the two hills behind the harbour. The town quay is about the easiest stern-to berth you will find in the Cyclades, so even in a blow this is a relaxed arrival.

Climb to Ano Syros, the medieval Catholic quarter on the higher hill, for lanes barely wide enough for a donkey and a long view over the roofs. The island is famous for loukoumi, so buy a box from one of the old confectioners. End the day on Plateia Miaouli, the marble square, as the cafes fill.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Ano Syros Catholic quarter
Taste loukoumi at an old confectioner
Sit on Miaouli marble square at dusk
Swim the rock platforms at Asteria
12.mooringTip: The Ermoupoli town quay is free and needs your own anchor, and it shelters well from any direction. If it fills up, the Manna marina charges EUR 60-80 a night.

13.shortDescription: A last beam reach back to Paros, choosing the postcard fishing harbour of Naoussa for a final night on the pier.
13.description:
The last real sailing day brings you back to Paros on a comfortable beam reach, twenty-four miles southeast. You have a choice of ends: Naoussa, the small fishing harbour on the north coast that fills every camera on the island, or Parikia, easier to get into if the crew is tired. Most people pick Naoussa for the last night.

Save an hour for Kolymbithres, where wind and sea have carved the granite into smooth grey outcrops you can snorkel around. In the evening, take a table on the Naoussa pier where the boats still land octopus and hang it on a line to dry before it goes on the grill. If there is time, the marble lanes of Lefkes make a quiet inland detour.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the wind-carved rocks at Kolymbithres
Grilled octopus on the Naoussa pier
Wine tasting at a Marpissa vineyard
Walk the marble lanes of Lefkes
13.mooringTip: Naoussa is stern-to at EUR 30-50 but often full by 17:00, so plan to arrive by mid-afternoon. Plastira Bay half a mile east gives good holding on sand at 5-7 m, and Parikia is the larger fallback.

14.shortDescription: Hand the boat back before nine, settle up, and squeeze in a last swim at Golden Beach before the short hop to the airport.
14.description:
Handover is the unglamorous end to a fine fortnight. Bring the boat alongside at Parikia or Naoussa before nine, have the tanks full and the gear stowed, and walk the base manager through her while the crew packs. Do it cleanly and the deposit comes back within about a week.

There is usually still time for a proper breakfast on the Parikia waterfront before anyone thinks about transfers. If the flight is later in the day, a taxi out to Golden Beach on the east coast buys one last swim, the long sweep of sand that windsurfers favour for its steady afternoon breeze. Paros airport is barely fifteen minutes from Parikia, so nobody needs to rush.
thingsToDo: Walk the handover with the base manager
One final swim at Golden Beach
Breakfast on the Parikia waterfront
Fifteen-minute transfer to Paros airport
14.mooringTip: Be alongside at Parikia or Naoussa before 09:00 for the inspection. Leave the boat clean and fuelled and the deposit is usually released within seven days.

paros-ios-santorini.metaDesc: Sail seven days from Paros through Ios and Santorini, looping home via Folegandros and Sifnos. A compact southern Cyclades run for Meltemi-ready crews.

1.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles down to Ios on a fast Meltemi reach, the windiest run of the week, with Chora's bars waiting after dark.
1.description: We like to clear Parikia by mid-afternoon, once the ice and water are aboard, and point the bow south. The Meltemi builds through the Naxos–Sikinos channel, and this is where the day earns its reputation: 22 to 30 knots, steady, on a port reach. Tuck a reef in before you leave the lee of Paros rather than fighting it later. Ios runs to 22 miles, and Gialos harbour at the island's south end gives proper shelter with stern-to berths; the marina alongside is slowly growing new pontoons. Mylopotas, a mile down the coast, is a fine lunchtime swim but no place to spend a Meltemi night. Once you are tied up, the Chora windmills and its late-night squares are a short walk uphill.
thingsToDo: Walk up to Homer's reputed grave at Plakotos
Swim the pale sand at Manganari
Catch rembetika in a Chora backstreet
Windmill terrace at golden hour
1.mooringTip: Berth stern-to in Gialos, roughly €25–40 a night; if the new marina has a pontoon free, reserve it ahead since space is tight in July. Treat Mylopotas as a daytime stop only.

2.shortDescription: Eighteen quiet miles to Santorini, best sailed at dawn, tying up at Vlychada before the taxi ride up to Oia for sunset.
2.description: An early start pays off here. The caldera is too deep to anchor anywhere, so Vlychada on the south coast is your only real bed for the night, and the run down is short at 18 miles. Slip your lines at first light while the sea is flat, and you should be tied up by lunch with the afternoon free. Vlychada sits below wind-carved cliffs the colour of ash. Later, share a taxi up to Oia; the crush at the Ammoudi overlook starts around six, so aim to be settled with a drink before then. If you have a spare hour on the way, the wineries at Santo and Domaine Sigalas pour a proper Assyrtiko, bracing and mineral.
thingsToDo: Watch the sun drop from Oia's castle
Taste Assyrtiko at Santo Wines terrace
Snorkel the red cliffs off Red Beach
Order fava and capers in old Megalochori
2.mooringTip: Vlychada is the pricey one, around €60–100 stern-to, and it fills fast in July and August, so call the marina a day or two ahead. The Thirasia mooring buoys are a gamble I would not count on.

3.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles back into the wind to Folegandros, with a swim at boat-only Katergo before you tie up at little Karavostasi.
3.description: Now the loop turns for home, and the wind that pushed you south becomes work. This first leg west runs 22 miles with the Meltemi square on the nose, so expect a beat and a longer day than the mileage suggests. Break it with a stop at Katergo, a pale pebble cove reachable only from the water and well sheltered from the north; drop the hook, swim, and eat before the last few miles. Karavostasi is the island's only workable harbour, snug in northerlies but open to any southerly slop. Chora sits 200 metres above on its cliff, reached by taxi or a stiff half-hour up the old kalderimi. Go up in the evening when the heat drops.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at hard-to-reach Katergo
Climb to the cliff-edge Panagia church
Matsata pasta with rooster in a courtyard
Follow the old mule path to Ano Meria
3.mooringTip: Karavostasi has only a handful of stern-to slots at €25–40, so arrive early. If the quay is full, anchor on sand in 4–7 metres in the bay just east. Neither spot is comfortable when the wind swings south.

4.shortDescription: A day off the clock on Folegandros: a morning swim, an afternoon walk to Panagia, and cheese-and-honey pies in Chora.
4.description: No passage today, which everyone aboard tends to welcome by the fourth morning. Reposition to Katergo for an early swim, anchoring on sand in 4 to 7 metres while the cove is still empty, then head back and go up to Chora for the afternoon. The village hides behind its old gate, all whitewashed courtyards and bougainvillea, with the church of Panagia set on the ridge above. Walk the cliff path up to it for the long view over the water and the town below. Lunch is the reward: the courtyard tavernas bake soft cheese pies finished with local honey, worth ordering twice. If the northerly holds, Agali on the south coast gives a second calm swim before dinner.
thingsToDo: Walk the ridge from Chora to Panagia
Swim the sheltered sand at Agali
Order cheese pies with island honey
Sundown drink on the Ano Meria road
4.mooringTip: Hold your Karavostasi berth for a second night rather than moving the boat. Both Katergo and Agali make good north-wind lunch anchors. In high season, phone the port a day ahead to keep your slot.

5.shortDescription: The route's longest beat, thirty-two miles up to Sifnos, the Cyclades' kitchen island, ending with clay-pot lamb and slow-baked chickpeas.
5.description: This is the long one. Thirty-two miles northwest into the Meltemi means two big port tacks and a full day on the water, so leave early and pace the crew. When the wind sits in its usual north, Platys Gialos on the south coast is the safe overnight; the island's bulk throws a clean wind shadow over the anchorage. Should the breeze back into the east, shift to Kamares on the west side instead. Sifnos rewards the effort at the table. This is where Greeks argue about food: revithada, chickpeas left overnight in a wood oven and eaten Sunday, and mastelo, lamb or goat slow-cooked with wine in a sealed clay pot. Walk it off later along the coast at Faros.
thingsToDo: Walk out to Chrysopigi monastery near Faros
Eat mastelo lamb up in Artemonas
Visit a working pottery kiln in Vathy
Swim the flat water of Vathi Bay
5.mooringTip: Anchor off Platys Gialos on sand in 5 to 8 metres, which holds well in the northerlies. Kamares has a stern-to quay at €20–30 if you want a town berth. When the wind clocks east, Vathi on the south coast is the calmer bolt-hole.

6.shortDescription: An easy reach northeast back to Paros, aiming for Naoussa's fishing harbour and a last supper of rope-dried octopus on the pier.
6.description: After yesterday, this leg feels like a gift. Twenty miles northeast to Paros with the Meltemi on the beam is the sweetest sailing of the return, quick and steady. Naoussa on the north coast is the prize, an old fishing port that has filled up with yachts and tavernas without losing the little painted boats in its inner basin. Berths there are fought over, so plan to be alongside by four or drop anchor in Plastira Bay just east. Parikia on the west coast has more room if Naoussa is stuffed. For the last night, eat on the pier where the octopus hangs drying on a line, grilled to order. Earlier in the day, the granite boulders at Kolymbithres are worth a snorkel.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the sculpted granite at Kolymbithres
Taste wine at a Marpissa vineyard
Grilled rope-dried octopus on Naoussa's pier
Wander Lefkes' marble lanes at dusk
6.mooringTip: Naoussa's stern-to berths run €30–50 and are usually gone by five, so push to arrive mid-afternoon. Failing that, anchor half a mile east in Plastira Bay on sand in 5 to 7 metres, or fall back on roomier Parikia.

7.shortDescription: Handover before nine at Parikia, a portside breakfast, and if there's time a final dip at Golden Beach before the short airport run.
7.description: Charter mornings run to a rhythm, and this one is no different. The base wants the boat back at Parikia or Naoussa before nine, so sort the laundry the night before and settle any small damage notes with photos rather than argument. The handover walk-through with the manager is straightforward when the boat comes back clean and fuelled; deposits usually clear within a week. Paros airport is only a quarter of an hour from Parikia, which leaves room for a proper breakfast on the waterfront. If your flight is later, Golden Beach on the south coast is the spot for one more swim before you hand back the keys and think about the next loop.
thingsToDo: Walk the boat over with the base manager
One last swim at Golden Beach
Waterfront breakfast on the Parikia quay
Quarter-hour transfer to Paros airport
7.mooringTip: Bring her back to Parikia or Naoussa before nine for handover. Photograph anything you flag as damage so the deposit, normally returned inside a week, goes through cleanly.

paros-naxos-schoinousa.metaDesc: Sail a week from Paros through Naxos, Donousa, Koufonisia, Schoinousa and Irakleia, the quietest anchorages in the Small Cyclades.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 6-mile crossing east to Naxos, where the lone marble Portara frames the year's easiest sunset from the harbour.
1.description: Six miles is barely a passage, which suits day one. You clear the Naxos-Paros channel with the sails half-loaded, get a feel for the boat, and slot into the town quay by early afternoon. Naxos harbour is the most forgiving on the whole loop, protected whichever way the wind turns. Walk the causeway out to Palatia islet before dinner. The Portara, a single marble doorframe left standing from a temple to Apollo that was never finished, catches the last light and fills with crowds and cameras. Inland is worth a morning if you have it: the Tragea valley, the old citron distillery at Halki, kitro poured neat over ice. Provisioning is easy here, so stock the deep runs to the Small Cyclades now while shelves are full.
thingsToDo: Sunset at the Portara marble gate
Kitro tasting at Halki's Vallindras distillery
Hike Mount Zas, the Cyclades' high point
Swim the sands at Agios Prokopios
1.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Naxos town quay, roughly 30-50 euro a night with power and water. It holds against any wind and rarely fills, so there's no need to rush your arrival.

2.shortDescription: The long leg of the week: 24 miles east-southeast to Donousa, the most far-flung of the Small Cyclades, and its quiet Stavros bay.
2.description: This is the day to leave early. Twenty-four miles is the week's real passage, and with the meltemi in the north you want the wind behind you and time in hand. Donousa sits out on its own, roughly a hundred people through winter, a village strung around Stavros bay. The quay is tiny and lazy lines are hit or miss, so if it's taken, drop the hook on the sand just outside and dinghy in. Kedros beach is a ten-minute walk south, a curve of pale sand over turquoise, sheltered when it's blowing from the north. There's a wartime wreck a few metres down for anyone with a mask. Evenings are simple here: a couple of tavernas, grilled fish, the smell of oregano off the hillside.
thingsToDo: Swim Kedros beach south of the village
Snorkel the sunken wreck off Kedros
Hike the coast path to Kalotaritissa
Grilled gouna at a Stavros taverna
2.mooringTip: Stern-to on the little Stavros quay runs about 15-25 euro, but slots are few and lines unreliable. If it's full, anchor on sand in 5-7 m just outside the bay, well tucked from the northerly meltemi.

3.shortDescription: Fifteen miles southwest to Koufonisia, the busiest of the small islands, with a lagoon swim off uninhabited Kato and lobster pasta on the quay.
3.description: Koufonisia is where the loop gets a little livelier, though by Cycladic standards that still means small. Two islands face each other across a shallow channel: Pano, where everyone lives, and Kato, empty but for a summer taverna and goats. Tie up on Pano and the day sorts itself out. Walk the low cliff path east from the village and you pass a string of natural rock pools cut into the limestone, the water so clear it barely reads as water at all. Pori, further round, is the big sandy bay everyone photographs. For dinner, astakomakaronada is the thing to order, lobster broken through long pasta, cooked all over the island. Get the boat in by late afternoon; the quay tightens up as the day-trippers' ferries come and go.
thingsToDo: Swim the Pori beach lagoon
Follow the rock-pool path east of Chora
Day-anchor off uninhabited Kato Koufonissi
Astakomakaronada lobster pasta at the port
3.mooringTip: The Pano harbour quay is stern-to at 25-40 euro and typically full by around five, so aim to arrive mid-afternoon. If there's no room, anchor on sand in 4-7 m just east of the village.

4.shortDescription: A brief westward hop of five miles reaches Schoinousa, the most peaceful of the inhabited islets, its Chora perched high above the harbour at Mersini.
4.description: Five miles is a lazy morning, so there's no reason to hurry off Koufonisia. Schoinousa keeps maybe two hundred people and does very little to court visitors, which is its whole appeal. The port at Mersini is a single row of buildings at the water; the real village, Chora, sits on the ridge above, a fifteen-minute walk up to windmill views and a handful of tavernas. Tsigouri is the swim, sandy and shallow, a few minutes from the harbour and protected from the north. The island is laced with footpaths to beaches you'll often have to yourself. We usually eat up in Chora, tomato keftedes and whatever the kitchen fried that afternoon, under a tamarisk with the whole channel laid out below.
thingsToDo: Swim sheltered Tsigouri beach near the port
Walk up to Chora's windmill ridge
Tomato keftedes at a Chora taverna
Hike the footpaths to Psili Ammos
4.mooringTip: Stern-to at Mersini costs 15-25 euro but the quay is short on space, so don't dawdle. Failing that, anchor on sand in 4-6 m east of the harbour, snug against a northerly.

5.shortDescription: Seven miles west to Irakleia, the sleepiest stop of all, with one harbour taverna, a cave hike, and the loop's clearest swimming.
5.description: Irakleia is the smallest inhabited stop on the run, a hundred residents give or take, and it wears its quiet lightly. Agios Georgios is the port, one bay with a taverna or two and a road that doesn't go far. Anchor off in the bay rather than fight for the little quay, which has no lazy lines and precious few slots. Livadi, a walk over the hill, holds the best swimming water of the whole week, pale sand shelving into green. The cave inland is the island's set-piece, a walk up from the village to a chamber of stalactites; take a torch and decent shoes. If a panigiri, a saint's-day feast, happens to land while you're in, go. The food, the fiddle and the raki all come out and nobody's watching the clock.
thingsToDo: Hike to the stalactite cave inland
Swim the clear water at Livadi
Grilled fish at the Agios Georgios taverna
Walk up to the old kastro ruins
5.mooringTip: Drop the hook in Agios Georgios bay on sand in 5-7 m, well protected from the northerly meltemi. The small quay does take boats at 15-25 euro, but there are no lazy lines, so anchoring off is the easier call.

6.shortDescription: Twenty miles west back to Paros, with an optional cave-snorkel off Antiparos and a last night on Naoussa's little fishing harbour.
6.description: The run home is twenty miles and mostly downwind on a northerly, an easy last passage. If time allows, swing past the south of Antiparos and drop anchor off the cave for a snorkel before the final leg. On Paros you've a choice for the night. Naoussa is the prettier call, a Venetian-era fishing harbour where the boats are small and the octopus hangs to dry on the lines; the quay is tight and books up, so radio ahead or take the anchorage at Plastira just east. Parikia, the ferry town, is plainer but easier to get into. Either way, this is the night for a proper dinner ashore, a bottle of Paros white, and a slow walk through marble lanes still warm from the day.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the sea cave off Antiparos
Dinner on the Naoussa harbour pier
Wine tasting at a Marpissa vineyard
Evening walk through marble-paved Lefkes
6.mooringTip: Naoussa is stern-to at 30-50 euro and often full by five in high season, so call ahead or plan on Plastira Bay half a mile east, anchoring on sand in 5-7 m. Parikia is the roomier fallback.

7.shortDescription: Handover morning at Parikia or Naoussa before nine, with the airport fifteen minutes away and time for one last swim.
7.description: Checkout is straightforward if you plan the morning. Bases here want the boat back before nine, so bring her in the night before if you can and hand over fuelled and tidy. Walk the deck with the base manager, note anything the sea threw at you, and photograph any mark before you sign; the deposit usually clears within a week once the inspection is done. Parikia sits a quarter-hour from Paros airport, so the transfer to a flight or ferry is painless. If your onward leg is later, there's still time for a coffee on the Parikia waterfront and a last dip. Golden Beach on the south coast is the pick, a long stretch of sand and the same wind that's carried you all week.
thingsToDo: Final walk-through with the base manager
Last swim at Golden Beach, south coast
Coffee on the Parikia waterfront
Fifteen-minute transfer to Paros airport
7.mooringTip: Have the boat back and handed over before nine, ideally moored the evening before. Photograph any noted damage before signing; deposits normally release within seven days of a clean inspection.

paros-syros-mykanos.metaDesc: A week-long sailing loop from Paros through Sifnos, Syros, Tinos and Mykonos: sheltered swim bays, marble villages and easy town quays in the Cyclades.

1.shortDescription: A 24-mile reach west to open the week, dropping the hook in Vathi's near-landlocked bay where the pottery kilns still smoke.
1.description: The first leg runs about 24 miles west, enough to shake down the boat and let the crew find their sea legs before anything demanding. Vathi sits behind a narrow entrance on Sifnos's south coast, and once you're inside it feels closer to a lake than an Aegean bay. The bottom is clean sand, holding is reliable, and the surrounding hills knock the wind flat by evening. Sifnos has fired clay for centuries; you can still see kilns on the slopes above the water. We usually anchor off, swim before the light goes, then row in for dinner at the taverna by the quay, where the seafood comes straight off the day boats. If your legs want stretching, the chapel of Chrysopigi is a manageable walk or a short taxi across the island.
thingsToDo: Watch the potters at Vathi's kilns
Swim the sheltered head of Vathi bay
Taxi across to Chrysopigi's islet chapel
Order baked revithada chickpeas at a kafeneio
1.mooringTip: Drop on sand in 4-6 m anywhere inside the bay; it holds well and shelter is all-round. The short quay takes a few stern-to boats at roughly EUR 15-25 a night, but the anchorage is honestly the nicer option.

2.shortDescription: An easy 8-mile hop up the coast to Kamares, the island's working port, with the bus to Apollonia and the best kitchens inland.
2.description: Barely two hours' sailing, so there's no rush to leave Vathi. Kamares is where the ferries land and where most charter crews reprovision, a broad bay on the west side backed by a sand beach and a line of tavernas. It takes the northerlies well but stays open to the west, so keep an eye on the forecast if a westerly is due. This is worth an afternoon ashore. Sifnos earned its reputation as the Cyclades' kitchen here, and the mastelo, lamb slow-cooked with wine and dill in a clay pot, is the dish to order up in Artemonas. The bus climbs to Apollonia in fifteen minutes if you'd rather wander the whitewashed lanes. Back on the quay, the sunset over the harbour mouth earns its own drink.
thingsToDo: Ride the bus up to old Apollonia
Order clay-pot mastelo lamb in Artemonas
Swim the sheltered sand at Platys Gialos
Sundowner on the Kamares waterfront
2.mooringTip: Anchor off the beach in 5-8 m of sand, or go stern-to on the little quay for EUR 20-30. If the wind backs into the west and the bay starts to roll, slip round to Platys Gialos or back to Vathi for the night.

3.shortDescription: The week's longest leg, a 32-mile beam reach into the Cyclades capital, where Ermoupoli's grand quay forgives even a clumsy stern-to.
3.description: Give this one a proper start. It's around 32 miles north-northeast, a long, satisfying beam reach when the Meltemi is in its usual mood, so reef early and let the boat do the work. Ermoupoli surprises people. It was the busiest port in Greece a century and a half ago, and the wealth left behind a marble town of neoclassical mansions, a miniature opera house and the vast Miaouli square. The town quay sits deep in the harbour and holds in almost any wind, which is why so many crews time the week to overnight here. Climb the stepped lanes to Ano Syros, the older Catholic quarter under the hill, for the view back down over the roofs. Before you leave, buy a box of the soft loukoumi the island has made since the 1830s.
thingsToDo: Climb to San Giorgio in Ano Syros
Buy loukoumi from an old confectioner
Cross the marble of Miaouli Square
Detour to Finikas bay for a swim
3.mooringTip: Go stern-to on the town quay using your own anchor; there's no charge and the shelter is genuinely all-round. If it's full in high season, Manna marina next door runs EUR 60-80 a night with lazy lines.

4.shortDescription: Only 14 miles, but the Syros-Tinos gap accelerates the Meltemi, so be tied up before the afternoon swell rolls into the harbour mouth.
4.description: The channel between Syros and Tinos does something predictable in summer: it squeezes the north wind and hands you an extra five knots, so what reads as a gentle forecast can feel lively on the water. It's a quick passage, though, and the trick is timing. Aim to have the lines ashore by four, before the swell starts wrapping around the breakwater. Tinos rewards a car more than most islands. Inland you'll find hundreds of ornate dovecotes, the marble-carving village of Pyrgos where sculptors still work the local stone, and Volax, a strange plain of round granite boulders. The local artichokes turn up braised with lemon and dill in the village tavernas. Save the evening for the light on the boulder fields.
thingsToDo: Tour the marble workshops of Pyrgos
Follow the dovecote path near Falatados
Braised Tinos artichokes in a village yard
Sunset among the Volax boulders
