4.mooringTip: Berth stern-to in the new harbour for EUR 25-40; the sand is only moderate holding, so lay plenty of scope and dig the anchor in hard. When the Meltemi tops 25 knots the inner fishing harbour is the calmer, safer bet.

5.shortDescription: A short eight miles into the busiest waters of the group, with a swim off Ornos before you slot into the marina at Tourlos.
5.description: The hop across to Mykonos is barely worth reefing for, though the same channel effect applies and the last mile past the harbour can be gusty. Book your berth at Tourlos ahead of time in July and August, ideally the day before, because the new marina fills fast and the old town quay only stays comfortable in a light northerly. There's no reason to hurry in. Drop anchor at Ornos or Psarou first for a long lunch and a swim while the day-trippers thin out. The island's real treasure lies a short sail west: Delos, the sacred island the ancients held as Apollo's birthplace, its marble lions and ruined streets worth a full morning. Come evening, the crowd drifts to Little Venice, where the houses stand with their feet in the sea and the sun drops behind the windmills.
thingsToDo: Spend a morning in ancient Delos
Lunch at road-free Agios Sostis beach
Sundown drinks in Little Venice
Walk out to Armenistis lighthouse
5.mooringTip: Tourlos New Marina is the sensible overnight, stern-to at a steep EUR 70-110 in peak weeks; reserve online at least a day ahead in July and August. Only take the old town quay if the northerly is under 18 knots, otherwise it becomes untenable overnight.

6.shortDescription: A glorious 22-mile downwind run back to Paros, easing into Naoussa's little fishing harbour with its half-drowned Venetian fort.
6.description: This is the leg crews remember. Twenty-two miles southwest with the Meltemi over your quarter, sails goose-winged or poled out, the boat surfing down the fronts of the swells. It's one of the more dependable downwind runs in the summer Cyclades, and it goes quickly. Naoussa waits at the end, a working fishing village on the north coast wrapped around a tiny harbour, the ruins of a Venetian fort half-sunk at its entrance. Boats still land octopus here, and you'll see it drying on lines outside the tavernas that ring the water. Take the tender across to Kolymbithres to snorkel around the wind-sculpted granite, or hire a car up to Lefkes, the old marble-paved capital in the hills, and taste the wine at one of the Marpissa vineyards on the way.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the granite rocks at Kolymbithres
Taste wine at a Marpissa vineyard
Grilled octopus on the Naoussa quay
Wander the marble lanes of Lefkes
6.mooringTip: Naoussa's harbour is small and popular; stern-to runs EUR 30-50 and it's often full by five, so arrive early. If you miss a spot, anchor in Plastira Bay just to the east on sand in 5-7 m, or drop down to the bigger, easier quay at Parikia.

7.shortDescription: A quiet last morning: hand the boat back by nine, squeeze in one more swim at Golden Beach, then a short transfer to Paros airport.
7.description: Checkout mornings are calmer if you prepare the night before: settle the bill, bag the rubbish, and leave the boat tidy so the handover is quick. Bases here want the yacht back by nine, when the manager walks the deck and cabins with you and checks the hull and gear. Assuming nothing's amiss, the security deposit is usually released within about a week. If your flight is later in the day, there's time for a proper breakfast on the Parikia waterfront and a final swim at Golden Beach on the south coast, where the long stretch of sand catches the morning sun. Parikia airport, PAS, is only a quarter of an hour from town, so transfers are painless. It's a gentle way to end a week that asked a fair bit of the crew.
thingsToDo: Walk the handover with the base manager
One last swim at Golden Beach
Breakfast along the Parikia waterfront
Fifteen-minute transfer to Paros airport
7.mooringTip: Have the boat back on the quay at Parikia or Naoussa before nine so the base manager can inspect on schedule; the deposit typically clears within a week of a clean check. Fill the fuel tank the evening before to avoid the morning queue at the pump.

preveza-ithaka-kastos.metaDesc: Sail a 7-day yacht charter from Preveza through Meganisi, Ithaka, Kefalonia, Kastos and Kalamos — quiet quays, sea caves and Ionian tavernas.

1.shortDescription: The longest leg of the week gets you out of Cleopatra Marina, through the Lefkada canal, and into Meganisi's sheltered eastern bays.
1.description: Give yourself a proper start and slip the lines by nine. Preveza to Meganisi is the biggest single hop of the trip, and the Lefkada canal sets the pace: the floating bridge at the north end swings open on the hour, so time your arrival rather than idling in the channel. Once through, the dredged cut runs straight and shallow, so stay between the marks. Meganisi opens up to the east, and the two bays worth aiming for are Porto Atheni and Abelike, both deeply indented and calm even when the afternoon maestro fills in. Spartochori sits up on the cliffs above Port Spilia if you want a village night instead. We usually take a restaurant mooring in one of the smaller coves, drop the hook off the bow, and run a line ashore. Swim before dinner while the light's still low.
thingsToDo: Time the Lefkada floating bridge opening
Anchor stern-to in Abelike Bay
Dinghy into the Papanikolis sea cave
Walk up to Spartochori village
Swim off a Porto Atheni cove
1.mooringTip: Porto Atheni and Abelike are anchor-plus-longline bays; go in bow-first to a taverna pontoon and the buoy comes with dinner. Vathy has a small town quay if you'd rather lie stern-to for the night.

2.shortDescription: A short, easy run southeast brings you to Ithaka, where Vathy's deep horseshoe harbour and the quieter northern villages of Kioni and Frikes wait.
2.description: With only twelve miles to cover, there's no rush. Most crews potter down the west coast of Meganisi, then cross the strait toward Ithaka's steep, green shoulders. Vathy is the obvious target, a near-landlocked bay ringed by the town, with a couple of pins on the map worth your morning: the Cave of the Nymphs above the road, where Homer's story has Odysseus stashing the Phaeacian gifts, and Dexa Bay for a swim on the way back. If Vathy's quay looks packed, keep going north. Kioni and Frikes are smaller, prettier, and slower, and the three ruined windmills at the mouth of Kioni make the approach one of the best in the Ionian. Eat ashore either way. A plate of sofrito and a cold bottle of local white finishes the day well.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Cave of the Nymphs
Swim in shallow Dexa Bay
Anchor off Kioni's three windmills
Eat sofrito at a Vathy taverna
Wander the lanes in Frikes
2.mooringTip: Vathy's town quay is stern-to with your own anchor and fills fast in July; get in before the breeze or take the north bays. Kioni and Frikes both have short quays plus room to anchor off in the bay.

3.shortDescription: Coast across to Kefalonia and tuck into Argostoli, the island's capital, where loggerhead turtles cruise the harbour and Robola vineyards sit just inland.
3.description: Kefalonia is the big one, and Argostoli sits deep inside a long, sheltered bay behind the Lassi peninsula. The approach past the Fanari lighthouse is straightforward, and the town quay puts you a two-minute walk from the morning market. Argostoli has a party trick worth catching early: loggerhead turtles work the harbour front at dawn, hanging around the fishing boats for scraps, and you'll often see them from the deck with a coffee in hand. Inland, the Robola cooperative up near Fragata pours the island's crisp white in the shadow of Mount Ainos, an easy taxi run if the crew fancies a tasting. Back on the water, Fanari Bay just outside the harbour makes a calmer overnight if the quay feels too lively. Try the kreatopita, Kefalonia's rich meat pie, before you leave.
thingsToDo: Spot loggerhead turtles at Argostoli harbour
Taste Robola white near Fragata
Walk the De Bosset stone bridge
Try kreatopita at a quayside taverna
Anchor overnight in quiet Fanari Bay
3.mooringTip: Argostoli's town quay is stern-to and busy; arrive by early afternoon to claim a spot with water and power. For a still night away from the traffic, drop the hook on sand in Fanari Bay just inside the headland.

4.shortDescription: A longer beat back across the inner Ionian lands you at Kastos, a thin, near-empty island with one small quay and total quiet.
4.description: This is the day you earn the peace at the far end. Twenty-odd miles back across the inner sea, usually close-hauled or on the beam once the maestro fills in around midday, so a nine o'clock start pays off. Kastos is long, thin, and about as sleepy as the Ionian gets, with a resident population you could count on two hands out of season. The single village sits on the west coast, its little harbour backed by olive terraces and a ruined windmill on the ridge above. Nights here are genuinely dark and genuinely silent. There's a taverna or two by the quay doing whatever came off the boat that morning, and not much else, which is the whole point. Swim off the south bays in the afternoon and let the day wind down slowly.
thingsToDo: Hike the ridge to the old windmill
Swim off the quiet southern bays
Eat the day's catch by the quay
Walk the island's stone donkey paths
4.mooringTip: The village quay takes a handful of boats stern-to with your own anchor, and there are restaurant moorings off the harbour where dinner ashore covers the buoy. Space is tight, so get in mid-afternoon rather than at dusk.

5.shortDescription: A short hop to pine-covered Kalamos, where George catches your lines at Port Kalamos and the ghost village of Port Leone waits round the coast.
5.description: Barely an hour under sail separates Kastos from Kalamos, so there's time to explore before you settle. Kalamos rises steep and green, wrapped in pine forest that runs almost to the waterline, and it hides one of the Ionian's strangest sights. Round the west coast to Port Leone, a village abandoned after the 1953 earthquake dropped its wells below the water table; the houses stand roofless and empty, though the little church is still kept and still used. Anchor off and row in for a look. Port Kalamos itself is the night stop, and mooring here is half the fun: George and his family have run the quayside taverna for decades and will wave you in, take your lines, and hand you a cold drink before the sun's off the hill. Book nothing, just arrive early.
thingsToDo: Row ashore at the Port Leone ghost village
Let George take your lines at the quay
Snorkel the clear water at Agrapidia
Climb up to Episkopi village
Walk the pine trails above the port
5.mooringTip: At Port Kalamos, George's taverna hands you a lazy line and pontoon space stern-to, no booking needed but arrive by mid-afternoon in season. For a night at anchor, Asprogiali Bay drops to five to seven metres over good holding sand.

6.shortDescription: The long ride home to Preveza back through the Lefkada canal, with a last swim stop off sandy Mytikas on the mainland shore.
6.description: Twenty-five miles closes the loop, so this is another early one, especially if you want the sails up before the wind builds. The run north hugs the mainland coast, and Mytikas makes a fair lunch stop: a soft, golden-sand shore that's a rarity in a region built mostly on white pebble and rock. Drop the hook, swim, eat on board, then push on. The Lefkada canal comes round again on the way back, floating bridge and all, so factor the hourly opening into your timing rather than arriving at the wrong minute. Aim to be alongside at Cleopatra Marina with light to spare; the last evening is easier when the boat's already settled. Book your fuel slot ahead so the morning handover isn't a scramble.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim off sandy Mytikas
Time the Lefkada bridge opening again
Fuel up before the Cleopatra Marina berth
Walk the Preveza waterfront in the evening
6.mooringTip: Cleopatra Marina is the base and lays you alongside or stern-to in a made berth. Radio ahead for a berthing slot and book the fuel dock 24 hours out, since the pontoon backs up on changeover days.

7.shortDescription: A slow last morning at Cleopatra Marina before handover, with time for the Venetian lanes of Preveza's old town or the Roman ruins at Nikopolis.
7.description: Handover usually runs mid-morning, so there's room for one last wander before the boat changes hands. Preveza's old town rewards a slow lap: narrow lanes under Venetian arches, a fish market that's been trading in the same spot for generations, and cafes where a Greek coffee comes with a glass of cold water and no hurry. If the crew has an hour and a hire car, the Roman city of Nikopolis sits a short drive north, built by Octavian to mark his victory at nearby Actium and still spread across the hillside in walls and mosaics. Sort the cleaning and packing the evening before so the morning stays unhurried. Then it's coffee on the paralia, one more look at the water, and the drive to the airport.
thingsToDo: Coffee under the Venetian arches
Browse the old-town fish market
Drive to the Nikopolis Roman ruins
Final walk along the Preveza paralia
Clean and pack for handover
7.mooringTip: Cleopatra Marina handles the check-out at your booked berth; clean the boat and settle the fuel the evening before so the morning inspection is quick.

preveza-kefalonia-zakynthos-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks under sail from Preveza through Meganisi, Ithaka, Kefalonia and Zakynthos, taking in castles, caves, turtle bays and quiet island quays.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 22-mile shakedown south from Cleopatra Marina, through the Lefkas swing-bridge on the hour, to a pick of three quiet Meganisi bays.
1.description: Week one starts at Cleopatra Marina, the big service base on the Preveza side of the gulf, and the first job is the Lefkas Canal an hour south. The swing-bridge lifts on the hour, so we usually aim for the nine o'clock opening and let the fleet sort itself out in the waiting basin. Once you're through, the afternoon breeze fills in down the east side of Lefkada and pushes you across to Meganisi by early afternoon. It's a small island, one you could drive around in an hour, but it packs in more good anchorages than places twice its size. Atheni in the west is the quiet choice, with Niagas taverna on the water and a stiff climb up to Spartochori on the clifftop. Vathy keeps the small marina and the livelier quay. Save the Papanikolis submarine cave for a dinghy poke tomorrow morning.
thingsToDo: Dinghy into the Papanikolis submarine cave
Climb the steps to Spartochori village
Long swim off Atherinos Bay
Grilled octopus on the Vathy quay
Sundowner above the harbour at Errikos
1.mooringTip: Most bays here run restaurant moorings — you take the buoy free and settle up over dinner. Vathy's little marina has lazy lines and a fee if you'd rather book a berth; holding is good on sand in 4–8 m, and it's well tucked from the north-west.

2.shortDescription: Fourteen miles south-east and round Meganisi's tail to Vathy on Ithaka, a cypress-ringed horseshoe with the tightest harbour entrance in the inner Ionian.
2.description: Ithaka is where the Homer story really takes hold, and most two-week crews make Vathy their first stop on the island. The harbour is a tight horseshoe, cypress and pale houses stacked up the slopes, Mt Nirito standing over the whole thing and catching the last of the light. The entrance channel is narrow and the cliffs rise steeply on both sides, so take it slow on the way in. Ashore, the walk up to the Cave of the Nymphs, where legend has Odysseus stashing his gifts from the Phaeacians, is a warm half-hour on a dirt track above town. Dexa, the pebble cove just west, is the swim on the way home. For dinner, To Trehantiri or Sirenes on the front both do a proper sofrito, the Ionian's veal-in-white-wine dish. Bag the town quay early in August.
thingsToDo: Walk up to the Cave of the Nymphs
Swim off pebbly Dexa Bay
Sofrito veal at To Trehantiri
Wander Vathy's old-town lanes
Evening stroll to the harbour battery
2.mooringTip: Go stern-to on the Vathy town quay — small fee, water and power, and good holding in 5–8 m of mud, but set the hook hard because the basin is tight. If you arrive after four in high summer and it's packed, drop back to Kioni six miles north for a calmer night.

3.shortDescription: Sixteen miles across the strait to Assos, a pastel Kefalonia village pinned under a Venetian fortress, with Myrtos Beach a short drive over the hill.
3.description: Cross the channel south-west and Kefalonia opens up, all pine and limestone. Assos sits on a little isthmus below its Venetian fortress, the one the Venetians threw up in the 1590s to watch the strait for pirates. The circuit of the walls is an easy hour on foot, with the old cisterns and magazine still standing inside the gate and the view worth the climb on its own. Down below, the bay is the postcard everyone knows — terracotta roofs, dark pines, green water. Myrtos, arguably the island's most famous beach, is a mile away by road; the shot from the cliff on the descent beats standing on the pebbles. Order a glass of Robola, the crisp local white grown on the slopes of Mount Ainos, at whichever taverna has a table in the shade.
thingsToDo: Walk the Venetian fortress walls
Drive to the Myrtos Beach overlook
Kayak the coves north of the bay
Robola tasting at a hillside taverna
Coastal path through the wild oregano
3.mooringTip: Anchor off the village in 5–8 m of clean sand, or grab a second-line stern-to slot on the little quay if one's open. The bay is wide open to the west, so if a westerly shows in the forecast, shift round to Fiskardo or Foulis a few miles north for the night.

4.shortDescription: A layover at anchor under the castle: swim the marble pebbles at Myrtos, or head inland to the Drogarati cave and the Melissani lake.
4.description: No passage today, which is rather the point of booking two weeks rather than one. You can spend the whole day in and out of the water below the castle, or make an excursion of it. Myrtos is the obvious swim, and the crescent under the cliffs does live up to the pictures, though bring reef shoes: the 'sand' is ground-down marble and the beach shelves off fast. Inland, Drogarati is worth the drive, a deep stalactite chamber with acoustics so good they hold the odd concert down there. Melissani sits nearby on the same loop — a sinkhole lake where the roof has fallen in and, around midday, the sun drops a column of light straight onto the water. Lunch is kreatopita, the Kefalonian meat-and-rice pie, at any village kafeneio. No fuel here; save that for Sami.
thingsToDo: Swim the marble crescent at Myrtos
Tour the Drogarati stalactite chambers
Catch the noon light at Melissani
Kayak across to Foulis Bay
Kreatopita at a village kafeneio
4.mooringTip: Stay put at anchor off Assos in 5–8 m of sand. Should the wind back into the west, motor round to Foulis or Fiskardo, three to five miles north, where you'll find better shelter.

5.shortDescription: Twenty-five miles down the sheltered west coast to Lixouri, Kefalonia's easygoing second town, with a swim under the cliffs of Petani on the way.
5.description: Twenty-five miles sounds like a haul, but you spend all of it tucked in the island's lee, so on a calm afternoon it's mostly motoring on flat water. Break the run at Petani, where gold cliffs frame a deep-water anchorage over sand in 5–7 m; it's the best swim on this stretch. Lixouri sits at the foot of the Paliki peninsula, the quieter counterpart to busy Argostoli over the water. The quay is long and easy, with power and water, and a shady seafront promenade running under tamarisks. Hire a bike at the harbour and roll south to Xi, where the sand is a strange rust-red from the clay cliffs behind and the shallows run out forever. The Calliga winery pours Robola in the hills above town. On a weekend evening you might catch the kantadores, the old mandolin-and-guitar trios, playing in the square.
thingsToDo: Swim below the cliffs at Petani
Cycle to the red sand at Xi
Robola tasting at Calliga winery
Stroll the Lixouri seafront promenade
Catch the kantadores in the square
5.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth on Lixouri's main quay — small fee, water and power, and plenty of room. If a strong northerly is forecast, the Argostoli quay across the gulf is more sheltered, though you'll share it with more commercial traffic.

6.shortDescription: Thirty open miles south to Zakynthos, pausing to anchor off the Navagio shipwreck cliffs before rounding to the rebuilt Venetian arcades of Zante Town.
6.description: Thirty miles of open water carry you off Kefalonia and onto Zakynthos, the last and southernmost of the Ionians. Navagio, the shipwreck beach that turns up on every Greece poster, is the waypoint no one skips: a strip of white sand at the foot of sheer chalk cliffs, with the rusting hull of the Panagiotis half-buried where it ran aground back in 1980. You can't sail in — anchor in the deep water outside the cliffs and take the tender the last stretch. It's strictly a daytime stop, wide open to the north-west, and the cliffs shed rock, so don't linger and mind the buoyed exclusion zone. Round Cape Skinari and run down to Zante Town for the night. The arcaded seafront was rebuilt almost stone for stone after the 1953 quake, and Solomos Square fills up for the evening stroll. Carry some mandolato nougat back aboard.
thingsToDo: Anchor under the Navagio shipwreck cliffs
Mandolato and ouzo on a Zante terrace
Join the evening volta on Solomos Square
Clifftop view from Cape Skinari
Buy nougat at a harbour bakery
6.mooringTip: Go stern-to on the Zante Town quay; it's basic, with water but no power, and no proper marina exists on the island. It's open to the south and south-west, so if a southerly threatens, run eight miles down to Keri Bay and anchor in 5–8 m of sand.

7.shortDescription: A free day of short hops on Zakynthos: the Blue Caves off Cape Skinari north, the loggerhead turtle lagoons of Keri to the south.
7.description: Think of a day off on Zakynthos as two easy runs, one north and one south of the town, with the afternoon thermal to nudge you home. Up at Cape Skinari the Blue Caves are the morning trip — a run of low sea-arches where the light bounces off the pale rock below and the water inside glows an improbable blue. Local boats run tours from Skinari, but with a snorkel and a torch you can do it off your own tender. To the south lies the marine park around Keri and the little islet of Marathonisi, the main Mediterranean nesting ground for loggerhead turtles. Anchor clear of the buoyed zone off the islet's south end and keep an eye on the surface; through the summer the turtles come up to breathe every quarter-hour or so. Finish with saganaki, the flamed cheese, at a Keri clifftop taverna.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Blue Caves at Skinari
Anchor in Keri's warm shallow bay
Watch for loggerheads off Marathonisi
Saganaki at a Keri clifftop taverna
Clifftop walk above Vromi cove
7.mooringTip: For a quiet night, anchor in Keri Bay in 5–8 m of sand, holding is reliable; otherwise it's back to the town quay. Around Marathonisi you're inside a national marine park, so anchor only outside the buoyed nesting zone at the islet's south end.

8.shortDescription: The week's long leg: thirty-eight miles north back across open water to Sami, so slip the lines by eight before the afternoon breeze fills in.
8.description: This is the long one — thirty-eight miles back across open water, and the reason the alarm goes early. Slip out of the anchorage by eight, ride the lee of Zakynthos for the first ten miles, then let the north-westerly settle on the beam for the crossing. Sami is a working town on Kefalonia's east side, best known for standing in as the set of the film Captain Corelli's Mandolin; the waterfront survives much as it was shot, and a few tavernas have leaned hard into the connection. Practically, it's the easiest fuel stop in the inner Ionian, with the diesel pump a few metres off the bow. Save an hour for Melissani, the sunlit cave lake up the road, reached by little boats from the visitor centre. If there's time left, Antisamos beach is a five-minute drive north.
thingsToDo: Boat into the sunlit Melissani lake
Lounge on cinematic Antisamos Beach
Walk the Captain Corelli waterfront
Drive up to the Drogarati cave
Top up diesel at the fuel dock
8.mooringTip: Berth stern-to on the Sami quay — small fee, water and power, and the fuel dock right alongside, which makes this the simplest place to refuel all week. It's long and easy to come onto, and well sheltered from the north-west.

9.shortDescription: An easy twelve miles up the coast to Fiskardo, the one village that rode out the 1953 quake, its pastel waterfront lined with cypress.
9.description: A short twelve miles up the inside of the coast, often under engine in the flat of the afternoon, with the headland opening on Fiskardo from a few miles out. This is the one corner of Kefalonia the 1953 earthquake spared, and the reward is a run of pastel neoclassical houses along the water that has been under heritage protection for decades. It's no secret, so the quay fills by early afternoon in August; a call to the Port Police on Channel 12 ahead of time doesn't hurt. Just east at Emplisi, the ruins of a Roman villa lie in three to five metres of clear water, walls and pottery shards you can pick out without a mask on a still day. For dinner, Tselenti up the hill or Lagoudera on the quay; finish on bakaliaros, salt cod with garlicky skordalia.
thingsToDo: Stroll the cypress-lined Fiskardo quay
Snorkel the Roman ruins at Emplisi
Bakaliaros at Lagoudera on the water
Sundowner above the harbour at Tselenti
Dinghy to the wreck off the entrance
9.mooringTip: The town quay is free but goes early — call the Port Police on Channel 12 as you approach, and try to be alongside by two in high season. If it's full, Foulis Bay a mile east is a good overflow anchorage, 4–6 m over sand with solid holding.

10.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles east to the mainland at Astakos, a working fishing harbour of caiques and quayside ouzeria that most charter brochures forget.
10.description: Twenty-two miles east puts you back on the mainland, on a stretch of the Acarnanian coast that almost no charter itinerary bothers with — which is exactly its charm after the camera-crowds of Fiskardo. Astakos is a fishing town first and a yacht stop a distant second: the quay is lined with caiques, the berthing is free, and the little one-table ouzeria along the front haven't yet swapped their menus for something international. The local plate is revithada, chickpeas cooked slow overnight in a clay pot with lemon and oregano; here they'll often put the morning's fish alongside it. Walk a couple of kilometres north to Kounina, a quiet pebble beach reached on a path past olive groves and tamarisk. Pick up a jar of thyme honey from the village shop; the hives sit on the wild hillsides above the town.
thingsToDo: Revithada with the fishermen on the quay
Walk past olive farms to Kounina Beach
Watch the day's catch come in
Buy thyme honey from the village shop
Sundowner under the tamarisk trees
10.mooringTip: Berth stern-to on the free town quay. Holding is only fair over mud and weed in 4–6 m, so back down hard to make sure the anchor has bitten. It's calm at night, but expect a few katabatic gusts off the mountains first thing.

11.shortDescription: A short eight miles across to Kastos, a slender island of fewer than fifty souls, one quay, one taverna, a ridge of dead windmills.
11.description: Kastos lies eight miles south-west, the long thin island off Ithaka's shoulder, home to fewer than fifty year-round residents and a stillness the rest of the Ionian mostly lost decades ago. One village around the harbour, one free quay, one fig-shaded taverna at its head. The morning walk is up the donkey path to the old stone windmills on the ridge, half an hour with the view back to Ithaka opening as you climb; the mills are roofless now but the masonry holds. On the south coast, Sarakiniko is the swim anchorage, sand in 4–6 m with no road down to it, so you'll often have the place to yourselves unless another crew had the same idea. The kafeneio squeezes its lemonade from the tree on the dock, and dinner is whatever came in that morning. Remember your last fuel and stores were Sami.
thingsToDo: Swim off quiet Sarakiniko Bay
Climb to the abandoned windmills
Homemade lemonade in the fig shade
Walk the donkey path to Kastos Village
Snorkel the Blue Cave on the south side
11.mooringTip: There's a single free quay with no services — space for eight or ten boats at most. Go stern-to, drop the anchor well clear over sand in 5–7 m, and back in gently. It's snug in any wind, but there's no fuel and nothing to provision, so arrive stocked.

12.shortDescription: Barely four miles east to pine-clad Kalamos, with its clifftop village of Episkopi and the haunting shell of Port Leone, emptied by the 1953 earthquake.
12.description: Four miles east is barely a passage — you'll likely motor it across on flat afternoon water and hardly bother with the main. Kalamos is Kastos's bigger neighbour, still small and still unfussy, but with two villages: Port Kalamos down by the harbour and Episkopi higher on the ridge, pines running right to the shore. The set-piece is Port Leone on the north coast, a whole stone fishing village left to fall quiet after the 1953 quake wrecked the spring that watered it. Nobody came back, and seventy years on the houses are folding slowly into the bay. Reach it on the ninety-minute coast walk or, easier, by tender. Asprogiali just south of the harbour is the swim, a strand of pale moonstone pebbles under grey-blue water. Order the savoro, small fish fried and steeped in vinegar and rosemary, and watch the goats wander down at dusk.
thingsToDo: Walk or dinghy to the Port Leone ghost village
Swim the moonstone pebbles at Asprogiali
Hike up to Episkopi village
Savoro fish at the harbour taverna
Watch the goats come down at dusk
12.mooringTip: Take a stern-to slot on the single Port Kalamos quay — small fee, water but no power, and room for about a dozen boats. Holding is excellent in 5–8 m of sand, well sheltered from the north-west, though the channel from Kastos can gust in the afternoon.

13.shortDescription: Twenty-eight miles north up the inner channel to Cleopatra Marina, with a last swim off Mytikas and the Lefkas swing-bridge to clear before you berth.
13.description: Twenty-eight miles north closes the loop back to Cleopatra Marina, mostly gentle motoring up the inner channel with the Lefkas swing-bridge to time on the way. On hand-back day the four o'clock lift is the usual target; the waiting basin fills through the afternoon in high season, so don't leave it late. Break the run for one last swim at Mytikas, on the mainland side, where golden sand backs a shallow bay with easy anchoring off it. Once you're berthed, it's worth the twenty-minute walk into Preveza. The old town runs in a tangle of Venetian arches behind the seafront, the market street cuts back from the harbour, and the tavernas along Pantokratoras haven't been priced up for tourists. Take on fuel at the marina before evening, put a load of washing through, and go into town for moussaka and a jug of retsina.
thingsToDo: Last swim off golden Mytikas Beach
Restock provisions in Preveza Town
Moussaka and retsina on Pantokratoras Street
Wander Preveza's Venetian old town
Refuel at the Cleopatra Marina dock
13.mooringTip: You'll come back onto lazy lines at Cleopatra Marina, full services and a fee, usually pre-booked through your charter agent. Leave time for the swing-bridge, which lifts on the hour and queues up early in August, and refuel before the technical hand-back.

14.shortDescription: A final morning at Cleopatra Marina before the short hop to Aktion airport, with time for coffee in Preveza or a last dip at Monolithi.
14.description: The hand-back itself is painless: papers signed at the desk, the fuel receipt logged, the cleaning time settled the night before. That usually leaves the morning free before your transfer, and it's well spent on the walk into Preveza — the arched old town along the seafront, the café strip on the Pantokratoras front for the first coffee, the small market a street back that sells local thyme honey and Kalamata olives to carry home. If you've time and a car, Monolithi to the south is the longest beach in Epirus, a twenty-two-kilometre run of fine sand behind the pines, with the deep, clean swimming you don't get inside the Ambracian Gulf. Then it's the easiest getaway of any Ionian base: Aktion airport shares the peninsula with the marina, five minutes by taxi across the channel.
thingsToDo: Morning coffee on Pantokratoras Street
Wander Preveza's cobbled old town
Buy thyme honey at the market
Last swim down at Monolithi Beach
Five-minute taxi to Aktion airport
14.mooringTip: Aim to be back at Cleopatra Marina by six on the Friday for the technical hand-back, then sleep aboard and step off by nine on Saturday. The airport is a five-minute taxi or a short shuttle away on the same peninsula, so no early alarm needed.

rhodes-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks from Rhodes through Symi, Nisyros, Kos and Patmos to the quiet far islands of the Dodecanese, then back via Astypalaia, Tilos and Lindos.

1.shortDescription: A short 24-mile hop northwest to Symi, whose stacked neoclassical houses make the most photographed harbour front in the Dodecanese.
1.description: We usually leave Mandraki by mid-morning and point the bow north-northwest, a gentle 24 miles to open the trip. Symi comes into view all at once: tiers of ochre and pale-blue mansions climbing the hillsides above Gialos, the working harbour. The stern-to berths on the inner quay are the prize, and they go early, so aim to be tied up by four in the afternoon in high season. If the quay is full, Pedi Bay sits two miles east with room to anchor. Ashore, the sponge stalls still trade along the front, a leftover from the island's old diving trade. Order the small sweet Symi shrimp at a quayside table, eaten shell and all. A taxi boat runs out to Nanou, a shingle cove you can only reach from the water.
thingsToDo: Visit Panormitis Monastery, Symi's south tip
Buy natural sponges on the Gialos front
Try sweet Symi shrimp at a quay taverna
Swim at boat-only Nanou cove
1.mooringTip: Stern-to on the inner Gialos quay runs roughly €30-50 a night and fills by late afternoon in July and August. If you miss it, drop the hook on sand at 5-7 m in Pedi Bay, two miles east.

2.shortDescription: Thirty-two miles west to Nisyros, an island built on a live volcano, with a caldera floor you can walk across.
2.description: A longer leg today, thirty-two miles west across open water, so an early start pays off before the afternoon breeze builds. Nisyros is still an active volcano, and the real payoff is inland. From Pali on the north coast you can arrange a lift up to the caldera, then walk down onto the flat floor of the Stefanos crater, where the ground hisses and smells of sulphur underfoot. Back at sea level, the old harbour town of Mandraki rewards a slow wander through its cobbled lanes. The thermal baths at Loutra sit on the road between the two. Local capers grow in the volcanic soil and turn up in most kitchens here. Pali itself is small and quiet, well sheltered from the north, which makes for an easy night after the crossing.
thingsToDo: Walk the Stefanos crater floor
Wander Mandraki's cobbled back lanes
Soak at the Loutra thermal baths
Buy volcanic-soil capers from a shop
2.mooringTip: Tie up stern-to in Pali on the north coast, about €25-35 a night and sheltered from the prevailing north wind. Mandraki on the west side is smaller and less protected.

3.shortDescription: A short fifteen miles north to Kos, the region's main charter base, home to the ancient Asklepion and a 2,400-year-old plane tree.
3.description: Only fifteen miles today, a straightforward run north, which leaves the afternoon free for town. Kos Marina is the largest base in the Dodecanese and has everything you might need to restock or fix a fault. Refuel at the fuel berth by the entrance on the way in. In town, the Asklepion spreads across a hillside just outside the walls, a fourth-century BC healing sanctuary tied to Hippocrates. Down in the old centre, a vast plane tree shades Platia Platanou, said to be well over two thousand years old. If time allows, the shallow hot springs at Therma warm a corner of the sea a short ride down the coast. Order kleftiko, lamb slow-cooked until it falls off the bone, at a taverna in the old quarter and watch the evening fill the square.
thingsToDo: Explore the Asklepion healing sanctuary
Sit under the ancient Platanou tree
Bathe in the Therma sea hot springs
Eat kleftiko lamb in the old town
3.mooringTip: Kos Marina berths you stern-to on lazy lines for around €60-90 a night with full services. Take on fuel at the entrance berth before you moor up.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles north to Leros and Lakki, one of the deepest natural harbours in the Mediterranean, ringed by 1930s Art Deco.
4.description: Leros sits twenty miles up the chain, and the approach into Lakki is unlike anywhere else in the Aegean. The bay is enormous and almost landlocked, one of the largest natural harbours in the Mediterranean, and its quay is lined with curved Art Deco buildings the Italians put up in the 1930s. It reads as a film set more than a Greek port. The marina stays calm even when it blows hard outside. If the Meltemi is up, avoid the town quay at Agia Marina on the north side, which catches the wind badly above about twenty knots. Climb to Panteli Castle for the long view over both coasts, then swim off Xerokambos on the quieter south shore. Ask for pitaroudia, the island's chickpea fritters, at a quay taverna before you turn in.
thingsToDo: Walk the 1930s Art Deco quay
Climb up to Panteli Castle
Swim at Xerokambos on the south coast
Try pitaroudia chickpea fritters quayside
4.mooringTip: Lakki Marina takes you stern-to on lazy lines at €40-60 a night, fully protected in any wind. The pretty town quay at Agia Marina is cheaper but exposed once the north Meltemi passes twenty knots.

5.shortDescription: A short twelve miles to Patmos, the island where Saint John wrote Revelation, its monastery and cave both UNESCO-listed.
5.description: Twelve easy miles bring you into Skala, the port, where the quay is long and holds well against the north wind. Patmos carries a lot of weight for a small island. Halfway up the hill to the old capital of Chora sits the Cave of the Apocalypse, where tradition says John received his visions, and above it the fortress-like Monastery of St John, founded in the eleventh century. Both are UNESCO-listed and worth the climb. Chora itself is a maze of whitewashed lanes that stay cool once the sun drops. For a beach day, Psili Ammos on the south coast is a proper stretch of golden sand, reachable by path or dinghy. Finish the night with a mastiha cocktail somewhere up in the old town, above the harbour lights.
thingsToDo: Visit the Cave of the Apocalypse
Tour the hilltop Monastery of St John
Swim the golden sand at Psili Ammos
Sip mastiha cocktails in Chora's lanes
5.mooringTip: Stern-to on Skala's main quay costs about €30-50 a night and stays sheltered from the north. The quay is long, so there is usually space even if you arrive later in the day.

6.shortDescription: Twelve miles southeast to little Lipsi, home to just 700 people, a single bakery and a string of clear snorkelling coves.
6.description: After the crowds of Patmos, Lipsi feels like exhaling. Around seven hundred people live here, spread across one small town and a scatter of outer islets. The pace is slow and the food is honest family cooking. Tie up on the town quay, which is cheap and sheltered from the north, then take the tender two miles east to Aspronissi for the best snorkelling in the group, the water pale over white sand. A walk out to the tiny chapel of Panagia, perched on a single rock, fills a lazy hour. Try the lipsiako, a soft local cheese, drizzled with the island's thyme honey. In the morning, queue at the one village bakery for warm koulouria before you slip the lines. Nights here are quiet in the best possible way.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the pale water at Aspronissi
Walk to the Panagia rock chapel
Eat lipsiako cheese with thyme honey
Buy warm koulouria from the village bakery
6.mooringTip: The town quay runs a bargain €15-25 a night and is well sheltered from the north. For a swim stop instead, anchor on sand at 4-6 m in Aspronissi Bay, two miles east.

7.shortDescription: Twenty miles out to Levitha, a speck of rock with six residents, no ferry, no ATM and the darkest night sky around.
7.description: Today you leave the ferry map behind. Levitha lies twenty miles southwest in open water, a bare island farmed by a single family who keep goats and run the only taverna. There is no port, no shop and no cash machine, so bring what you need. Pick up a mooring or anchor on sand in Ammoudi Bay, well tucked from the north wind, and the afternoon is yours to swim off the boat in water that barely sees another hull. The family will cook if you ask ahead; astakomakaronada, lobster with pasta, is the thing to order. What people remember, though, is the sky. With no light for miles, the stars come right down to the horizon and the Milky Way throws a shadow. Sit on deck and let it get properly dark.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in Ammoudi Bay
Eat astakomakaronada at the family taverna
Lend a hand at the goat farm
Stargaze on deck with zero light
7.mooringTip: Anchoring in Ammoudi Bay is free, on sand at 5-7 m and fully sheltered from the north. There are no services at all, so top up water, fuel and cash before you leave Lipsi.

8.shortDescription: Thirty-two miles east to Kalymnos, sponge-diving heart of the Aegean and a magnet for climbers on its west-coast limestone.
8.description: A solid thirty-two miles east returns you to a busier island. Kalymnos made its name from sponges, and the town of Pothia still carries that history in its museum and in the grand captains' houses along the front. Overnight stern-to in the harbour there. The other Kalymnos belongs to climbers: the west coast around Massouri and Armeos is laced with world-class routes on grey and orange limestone, and the Grande Grotta cave looms straight above the road. Even non-climbers should go and watch. For a swim, Vathy on the east side is a narrow fjord-like inlet, green with citrus groves and startling blue at the head. Order mououri, squid stuffed and slow-cooked, at an ouzeri in Pothia. The town has a working, unpolished feel that some crews come to prefer.
thingsToDo: Watch climbers at the Grande Grotta cave
Snorkel the fjord-like inlet at Vathy
Visit Pothia's Sponge Diving Museum
Try stuffed squid mououri at an ouzeri
8.mooringTip: Stern-to in Pothia harbour costs around €30-50 a night. If you would rather anchor, the sheltered inlet at Vathy on the east coast makes a fine alternative.

9.shortDescription: A long thirty-eight-mile beam reach southwest to butterfly-shaped Astypalaia, the westernmost Dodecanese island, crowned by a Venetian castle.
9.description: This is one of the big legs, thirty-eight miles southwest, but with the Meltemi on the beam it can be a fast and glorious sail rather than a slog. Astypalaia sits apart from the rest of the group, shaped like a butterfly with two wings pinched at a narrow waist. Pera Gialos, the port below the Chora, is the usual berth, sheltered from the north. Above it the Venetian castle of Querini caps the hill, its whitewashed chapels built right into the walls, with a line of windmills on the ridge. Walk up in the late afternoon when the light softens. Vatses, on the south coast, is a broad sand beach worth the rough track down to it. Book a table at Kaminakia and order the ladenia, a Cycladic-style tomato flatbread baked in olive oil.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Querini castle in Chora
Swim the sand beach at Vatses
Eat ladenia tomato pie at Kaminakia
Hunt for Roman mosaics near Mesa Nisi
9.mooringTip: Berth stern-to in Pera Gialos below the Chora, about €25-40 a night and sheltered from the north. Maltezana, further along the south coast, gives a quieter anchorage if the port is full.

10.shortDescription: A further thirty-eight miles, this time on a southeast heading to Tilos — Greece's pioneer zero-waste island, whose ghost village wakes up once night falls.
10.description: Back the other way now, thirty-eight miles southeast, so watch the forecast and leave early. Tilos is quietly proud of being the first island in Greece to go zero-waste, and it has a gentle, unhurried air. Livadia, the port, holds well and shelters from the north. The island's odd delight is Mikro Chorio, a mountain village abandoned decades ago and left in ruins, except for one bar that opens only at night among the empty houses. Go up after dinner. By day, hike to the monastery of Agios Panteleimon, tucked in the hills under old cypresses. At a hilltop kafeneio, order mizithra cheese with thyme honey. Tilos even grows its own wine, and a small tasting is easy to arrange. Few boats stop here, which is rather the point.
thingsToDo: Drink at Mikro Chorio's after-dark bar
Hike to Agios Panteleimon monastery
Eat mizithra cheese with thyme honey
Taste the island's own Tilos wine
10.mooringTip: Expect roughly €20-30 a night lying stern-to inside Livadia harbour, which keeps the north off you. Want a swim instead? Eristos Bay down the south coast anchors nicely.

11.shortDescription: Fifteen miles south to car-free Chalki, 300 residents, a bright snorkelling cove and a Knights' castle on the ridge above town.
11.description: A short fifteen miles south drops you at Chalki, where the pace slows once more. The single village, Emporio, has around three hundred residents and almost no cars, its mansions painted in faded pastels around the bay. Tie up stern-to on the harbour quay, sheltered from the north. Just east lies Pontamos, a shallow cove where the water glows an improbable blue over pale sand, ideal for an afternoon in the tender. Above the village a track climbs to the old Knights' Castle on the ridge, built over an ancient acropolis, with the whole channel laid out below. Sit down at Taverna Giorgos and try the pougia, octopus dried in the sun. On the way back to the boat, pick up a bottle of souma, the local fig spirit, from the Yiannis distillery.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the electric-blue Pontamos cove
Hike to the ridge-top Knights' Castle
Try sun-dried octopus at Taverna Giorgos
Buy souma fig spirit from Yiannis
11.mooringTip: Stern-to in Emporio harbour is about €20-30 a night and sheltered from the north. For a swim stop, anchor on sand at 4-6 m off Pontamos, one mile east.

12.shortDescription: Thirty-two miles east to Lindos on Rhodes, its ancient acropolis high on the cliff and no town quay, so you anchor in St Paul's Bay.
12.description: Thirty-two miles east brings you back to Rhodes, landing at Lindos near its southern end. There is no quay here, so plan to anchor: St Paul's Bay, just south of the village, is a near-enclosed circle of turquoise ringed by rock, with sand at four to six metres and shelter from the north. Above it all stands the acropolis, a fifth-century BC temple to Athena set on a sheer headland, reached by a stepped path through the old town. Go early or late to dodge the day-trippers and the worst of the heat. The village below is a warren of whitewashed lanes and old captains' houses. Find a rooftop taverna for melekouni, the local sesame-and-honey bar, and stay for the sunset spreading over the white cubes.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Lindos acropolis at dawn
Swim the rock-ringed St Paul's Bay
Eat melekouni sesame bars on a rooftop
Wander the whitewashed lanes at sunset
12.mooringTip: Lindos has no town quay, so anchor on sand at 4-6 m in St Paul's Bay, sheltered from the north. Lardos Bay, two miles south, is a calmer fallback if it fills up.

13.shortDescription: A final twenty miles up to Rhodes Town, closing the loop at Mandraki beneath the largest inhabited medieval old town in Europe.
13.description: The last real sail, twenty miles north up the east coast, returns you to where it began at Mandraki Marina in Rhodes Town. Refuel at the entrance berth before you tie up for the night. Then give the evening to the Old Town, a walled medieval city that people still live in, the largest of its kind in Europe. Walk the cobbled Street of the Knights, lined with the inns of the old crusader orders, and step into the Palace of the Grand Master at the top. If there is time before you arrive, swim in Anthony Quinn Bay south of the marina, a rocky cove named for the film shot there. For a last drink, find the leafy courtyard at Socratous Garden, tucked off one of the busier lanes.
thingsToDo: Walk the medieval Street of the Knights
Tour the Palace of the Grand Master
Swim at rocky Anthony Quinn Bay
Drink in the Socratous Garden courtyard
13.mooringTip: Mandraki Marina berths you stern-to on lazy lines for €60-90 a night. Take on fuel at the entrance berth before mooring so you are ready for the morning handover.

14.shortDescription: Handover at Mandraki before nine, then a last morning in the Old Town before the short run to the airport.
14.description: Disembarkation morning. The base wants the boat back at Mandraki by nine, so pack the night before and strip the beds early. The handover is a walkthrough with the base manager to check the boat over; once that is done, the deposit is usually released within a week. Crews with a late flight can still squeeze in a last wander through the Old Town, a coffee in the sun, one more look at the harbour mouth where the Colossus is said to have stood. Marco Polo Cafe, in a quiet corner of the medieval quarter, is a fine spot for a farewell breakfast. Rhodes airport is only about thirty minutes away by road, so there is no great rush. Two weeks and a full circuit of the Dodecanese now behind you.
thingsToDo: Final walkthrough with the base manager
Coffee at Marco Polo Cafe
Last walk down the Street of the Knights
Thirty-minute transfer to Rhodes airport
14.mooringTip: Hand the boat back at Mandraki Marina before 09:00 for the check-out inspection. The deposit is normally returned within seven days once the boat is signed off.

rhodes-route.metaDesc: Round trip from Rhodes: sail to Symi, Nisyros, Kos, Tilos and Chalki in a week — volcanic craters, Knights-era harbours and quiet snorkel coves.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 24-mile run north to Symi, where tiers of pastel merchant houses climb straight up from the fishing quay.
1.description: We usually treat the first leg as a shake-down, and this one obliges: 24 miles NNW out of Mandraki, sails eventually filling once you clear the Rhodes headland. Symi's Gialos harbour comes into view like a painted amphitheatre, ochre and faded rose façades stacked up the hillside behind the quay. Get stern-to on the inner wall by mid-afternoon; the good slots go early in July and August. If the quay is packed, motor two miles round to Pedi Bay and drop the hook on sand. The town rewards a wander uphill once the day-trippers' boats leave — the light on those houses at seven in the evening is the reason people photograph this place. Eat the tiny Symi shrimp shell and all, and buy a natural sponge from the stalls before you cast off.
thingsToDo: Hike to Panormitis Monastery at the south end
Swim at boat-only Nanou Beach
Buy natural sponges from the Gialos stalls
Try Symi shrimp at a quayside taverna
1.mooringTip: Aim for the inner quay before four in the afternoon; stern-to costs about €30-50 and berths vanish fast in peak season. Pedi Bay, two miles east, gives good sand-holding in 5-7 m if you'd rather anchor.

2.shortDescription: Thirty-two miles west to a living volcano — overnight in little Pali, then descend into a steaming crater on the caldera floor.
2.description: The longest hop of the week, so we like to leave by nine and get the westerly behind us. Nisyros is one of the few Aegean islands where you can walk down into an active caldera; the Stefanos crater floor hisses with sulphur and the ground is warm through your soles. Pali, on the north coast, is the sensible overnight — a small, tidy harbour tucked in behind a mole and sheltered from the prevailing northerlies. Hire a scooter or catch the island bus up to Mandraki, the whitewashed capital, and lose an hour in its cobbled lanes above the sea. The volcanic soil grows famously intense capers, sold by the jar. Time your return for a soak at the Loutra thermal baths if the day runs long.
thingsToDo: Walk down into the Stefanos crater
Wander Mandraki's cobbled clifftop lanes
Soak at the Loutra thermal baths
Buy volcanic-soil capers by the jar
2.mooringTip: Pali is the reliable overnight: stern-to behind the mole at €25-35, well covered from the north. Mandraki on the west coast is smaller and turns uncomfortable in any northwesterly, so treat it as a fallback.

3.shortDescription: A short 15-mile leg north to Kos, the fleet's home base — big marina, hot springs on the beach and the birthplace of Hippocrates.
3.description: An easy morning's sail sets up a comfortable stop, and Kos Marina takes the strain off — proper lazy lines, a fuel berth at the entrance, showers that work, and shelter whatever the wind does. Top up diesel before you swing into your berth. The town is walkable and layered with history: the Asklepion, the healing sanctuary where Hippocrates is said to have taught, sits on a terraced hillside a couple of miles out. In the old town, a plane tree reckoned to be over two millennia old shades the square by the castle. If the crew wants a beach with a twist, Therma lies a few miles south, where a hot spring bubbles straight into a walled pool at the shoreline. Order kleftiko, lamb slow-baked until it falls off the bone.
thingsToDo: Tour the hillside Asklepion of Hippocrates
Sit under the ancient plane tree by the castle
Bathe in the Therma shoreline hot spring
Order slow-baked kleftiko lamb in town
3.mooringTip: Kos Marina moors stern-to on lazy lines at €60-90 a night and is the best-protected berth on the route. Take on fuel at the entrance berth before you turn in. Lambi, on the north coast, is a lower-key alternative.

4.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles south to Tilos, Greece's greenest island, where a whole abandoned village comes back to life as a night-time taverna.
4.description: Tilos runs itself on sun and wind and banned single-use plastic bags years before it was fashionable, and the island feels calmer for it. We drop into Livadia, the port on the east coast, which holds the northerly swell out nicely. The set-piece here is Mikro Chorio: a stone village on the hillside, emptied out generations ago, now revived after dark when a single bar reopens among the ruins and the walls are lit up. Go late. By day, the cliff path up to Agios Panteleimon monastery is a good stretch of the legs, with the whole channel laid out below. Sit in a hilltop kafeneio over mizithra cheese drizzled with thyme honey, and if there's time, taste the wine from the solar-run vineyards.
thingsToDo: Drink at Mikro Chorio's ruined village bar after dark
Climb the cliff path to Agios Panteleimon
Taste mizithra cheese with thyme honey
Sample wine from the solar-run vineyards
4.mooringTip: Livadia takes you stern-to at €20-30 and shrugs off the northerlies. If you want a sand bottom and a swim off the boat instead, Eristos Bay on the south coast anchors well.

5.shortDescription: Fifteen easy miles to car-free Chalki — one quay, three hundred residents, and a cove of electric-blue water a mile from your berth.
5.description: Chalki is tiny and all the better for it — no cars to speak of, one village, one quay. Emporio's waterfront is a neat row of restored houses in soft colours, and the pace drops the moment you tie up. A short walk or a very short hop east brings you to Pontamos, a shallow cove where the water glows an improbable blue and the snorkelling over the sandy bottom is easy and clear. Above the village, the ruined Knights' Castle repays the climb with a view back across to Rhodes. Book nothing fancy for dinner: order the sun-dried octopus at Taverna Giorgos, and before you leave, pick up a bottle of souma, the local fig spirit, from Yiannis' little distillery. It's a slow, restorative sort of day.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the electric-blue shallows of Pontamos Cove
Climb to the Knights' Castle above Emporio
Eat sun-dried octopus at Taverna Giorgos
Buy souma fig spirit from Yiannis' distillery
5.mooringTip: Emporio's quay is the only game on the island, so arrive by early afternoon for a stern-to berth at €20-30, sheltered from the north. Pontamos anchors on sand in 4-6 m if you'd rather swim than tie up.

6.shortDescription: A 32-mile run back east to Lindos, its floodlit acropolis riding the cliff above one of the Aegean's prettiest anchorages.
6.description: The last full day is also the longest sail back east, so we weigh anchor early and make the most of the breeze. Lindos announces itself from miles off: a fortified acropolis on a headland, the Temple of Athena up top, and a knot of sugar-cube houses spilling down beneath it. There's no town quay here, so the drill is to anchor in St Paul's Bay, the near-enclosed lagoon just south of the village, and go ashore by tender. Swim first; the water in that rocky crescent is a proper turquoise. Then climb the donkey path to the acropolis before the afternoon heat sets in. Back down in the lanes, look for melekouni, the sesame-and-honey bars that turn up at village weddings, and take your evening drink on a rooftop as the walls go gold.
thingsToDo: Climb the donkey path to Lindos acropolis
Swim the turquoise lagoon at St Paul's Bay
Try melekouni sesame-honey bars in the lanes
Take sunset drinks on a village rooftop
6.mooringTip: Lindos has no quay, so plan to anchor in St Paul's Bay on sand in 4-6 m, tucked in from the north. It fills up, so if you arrive late, Lardos Bay a couple of miles south is the fallback.

7.shortDescription: A gentle 20-mile hop back up the coast to Mandraki for handover, then time to walk the medieval Street of the Knights.
7.description: An easy final passage: twenty miles up the east coast to Mandraki, timed so you're back in good order. Most bases want the boat handed over by nine the next morning, so aim to berth the afternoon before, settle the log and let the crew pack at leisure. The manager will walk the deck and hull with you, and the security deposit usually clears within a week once the inspection's done. Rhodes airport is only half an hour away by road, which takes the pressure off departure day. Don't rush straight to the gate, though. The medieval Old Town is right there: pace the cobbled Street of the Knights, duck into the courtyards, and take a last Greek coffee at the Marco Polo Café before you hand back the keys.
thingsToDo: Walk the cobbled Street of the Knights
Take Greek coffee at the Marco Polo Café
Wander the Old Town's medieval courtyards
Run the final inspection with the base manager
7.mooringTip: Berth back at Mandraki the afternoon before and hand over by nine the next morning. The deposit is typically released within seven days of a clean inspection, so photograph the boat's condition as you leave her.

santorini-milos-paros.metaDesc: Sail a week from Santorini through Ios, Paros, Serifos, Sifnos and Milos, then home. A southern-Cyclades loop of caldera cliffs and volcanic bays.

1.shortDescription: An easy 18-mile warm-up north from Vlychada to Ios, where Ormos gives calm stern-to mooring and the whitewashed Chora rewards a climb at dusk.
1.description: Your first leg is a gentle one, eighteen miles of open water while the crew finds its feet and someone remembers where the winch handles live. Clear Vlychada, point the bow north, and Ios rises within a few hours. We usually aim for Ormos, the harbour tucked into the island's southwest corner, where stern-to holding is dependable and the ferry wash settles by evening. Mylopotas, a mile south, is the swim stop everyone photographs, though it turns rolly the moment the Meltemi fills in, so treat it as a lunchtime call rather than a berth. Ashore, the port stays sleepy until the switchbacks up to Chora deliver you into a maze of chapels and bars. Climb before sunset. The light off the windmills is worth the sweat, and the square only wakes later.
thingsToDo: Hike to Homer's tomb above Plakotos
Swim the sandy coves at Manganari
Climb to Chora's windmills at sunset
Lunch anchor off Mylopotas beach
1.mooringTip: Berth stern-to at Ormos, roughly 25-40 euros a night, with good holding once the ferry traffic thins after dark. Use Mylopotas only by day; it becomes untenable overnight once the Meltemi sets in.

2.shortDescription: A 28-mile reach north to Paros, breeziest through the Naxos-Sikinos gap, ending at Parikia where mooring is kinder than Naoussa and marble lanes wait ashore.
2.description: This is the leg that reminds you the Meltemi has teeth. The channel between Naxos and Sikinos accelerates the northerly, and most days you will see the low twenties building to thirty as you cross it, so shorten sail before you commit rather than wrestling it later. Once past, the wind eases under Paros and Parikia opens up ahead. We prefer it to Naoussa for an overnight; the harbour has been dredged, berthing is calmer, and you are a short walk from the old town. Wander the marble lanes to the Ekatontapiliani, the fourth-century church at the heart of Parikia, then find a table for grilled fish as the ferries come and go. Save Naoussa for a lunchtime run if the crew wants the fishing-harbour postcard.
thingsToDo: Tour the 4th-century Ekatontapiliani church
Walk Parikia's marble old-town lanes
Anchor off Kolymbithres granite rocks
Wine tasting at a Naoussa cellar
2.mooringTip: Go stern-to in Parikia, around 30-50 euros a night; the dredged basin makes it an easier berth than Naoussa in a fresh Meltemi. If you want Naoussa's harbour instead, arrive early, since its handful of quay spots fill fast in July and August.

3.shortDescription: The longest reach of the week, 32 miles west to Serifos, where Livadi holds on sand and the cliff-top Chora glows white above the bay.
3.description: Thirty-two miles of beam reach in a settled Meltemi make this the big mileage day, so leave the berth early and let the boat do the work. Serifos sits low and dry on the horizon until Livadi opens on the south coast. The bay accelerates the wind coming over the ridge, but the sand underneath holds well if you give it scope. This is the leg where crews most often drag, usually from laying too little chain, so set the hook properly and swim down to check it before anyone heads ashore. Above the port, the Chora clings to its rock like something from a film set. Walk the old mule steps up rather than taking the bus; the view back over the anchored fleet at dusk is the reward. Down at sea level, Psili Ammos is a fine afternoon swim.
thingsToDo: Climb the mule steps to Chora
Swim Psili Ammos on the east coast
Chickpea revithada in a Livadi kafeneio
Sunset by the ruined Chora windmills
3.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in Livadi Bay at 5-8 metres and lay plenty of scope; the wind funnels down the valley and holding turns marginal above 20 knots, so dive the anchor before leaving the boat. The town quay takes a few stern-to boats at 20-30 euros a night if you would rather tie up.

4.shortDescription: A short 14-mile hop south to Sifnos, food capital of the Cyclades, mooring at Kamares or slipping round to Vathi when the wind swings west.
4.description: After yesterday's slog, fourteen miles feels like a rest day. Roll south under a steady northerly and you will be anchored in time for a long lunch. Kamares, the ferry port on the west side, is the usual overnight and sits well protected from the north and northeast, though it opens up if the breeze backs into the west. When that happens, up anchor and run round to Vathi on the south coast, a near-landlocked sandy bay that stays calm in almost anything. Sifnos is where you eat properly. This is the island of clay-pot cooking: revithada baked overnight, chickpea soup, slow lamb in the mastelo pot, and honey-almond sweets. Book a table up in Artemonas or Apollonia, hire a scooter if you have the afternoon, and don't skip the walk out to the Chrysopigi monastery on its rock at Faros.
thingsToDo: Clay-pot revithada in Artemonas
Walk to Chrysopigi monastery at Faros
Anchor overnight in sheltered Vathi bay
Browse the potters' studios in Vathi
4.mooringTip: Drop the hook on sand at 5-8 metres in Kamares, or take one of the few stern-to spots on the small quay for 20-30 euros a night. If the wind clocks into the west and Kamares starts to roll, move to Vathi on the south coast, which holds calm through most conditions.

5.shortDescription: Just 18 miles south to Milos, whose deep Adamas inlet gives the best all-weather shelter in the western Cyclades, past lunar Sarakiniko.
5.description: Milos earns its place on this loop for two reasons: the swimming and the shelter. The run down from Sifnos is short and usually kind, which leaves the afternoon free. Weather permitting, ghost past Sarakiniko on the north coast for a swim, the white pumice folded into shapes that look poured rather than eroded. It is fully exposed, so only stop when the sea is flat. From there, the long inlet reaches deep into the island toward Adamas, one of the most protected harbours in these waters, calm whatever the Meltemi is doing outside. Tie up, then get ashore. Take a taxi or scooter up to Plaka for the castle view, and wander down to Klima, where the fishermen's boat garages line the shore in blocks of painted colour beneath the cliff.
thingsToDo: Swim the white cliffs of Sarakiniko
Snorkel the Papafragas sea caves
Climb to Plaka Castle for the view
Photograph the painted syrmata at Klima
5.mooringTip: Berth stern-to on the Adamas town quay for around 30-50 euros a night; shelter here is excellent from every direction. Pollonia in the northeast is quieter and a little cheaper, but it sits open to a northerly Meltemi, so leave it for settled spells.

6.shortDescription: The long 38-mile beam reach back to Santorini, timed to reach Vlychada before evening so the crew has one last night for the Oia sunset.
6.description: The final passage is the second-biggest of the week, thirty-eight miles southeast across open water, usually a comfortable beam reach in the prevailing northerly. Point at Santorini early and keep an eye on the clock. You want to be inside Vlychada before five, both to leave the evening free and because the marina berth is worth arriving to in daylight. Call ahead in peak season; the slots go quickly and you don't want to be circling at dusk. With the boat secure, hand the lines to someone and take a taxi north to Oia. The sunset there draws a crowd for good reason, and the walk down to Ammoudi afterwards puts you at a fish taverna by the water. If you would rather dodge the crush, the vineyards at Santo Wines or Domaine Sigalas pour a fine Assyrtiko.
thingsToDo: Taxi to Oia for the caldera sunset
Assyrtiko tasting at Santo Wines
Fish dinner down at Ammoudi Bay
Swim off the Red Beach cliffs
6.mooringTip: Reserve a Vlychada berth well ahead in July and August; stern-to here runs 60-100 euros a night and the marina fills in peak weeks. Aim to be alongside by mid-afternoon, and confirm your handover slot with the base 24 hours out.

7.shortDescription: Handover morning at Vlychada: boat settled by nine, walk the inspection with the base, then a last caldera swim before the airport run.
7.description: Last morning, and it's the tidy one. Have the boat back at Vlychada and cleared out before nine so the handover runs to schedule. Walk the inspection with the base manager rather than leaving them to it, and photograph anything already marked on the sheet before you sign; deposits usually clear within a week of a clean check-out. Once the paperwork is done, you have a little time before the transfer. The airport at Santorini sits about twenty-five minutes up the road, so there's room for a slow breakfast on the caldera rim or a final swim at Ammoudi Bay beneath Oia, where the water is deep and cool off the rocks. Then it's bags on the dock and the drive to JTR. A tight, satisfying loop of the southern and western Cyclades, done.
thingsToDo: Walk the check-out with the base manager
Final swim at Ammoudi Bay below Oia
Breakfast on the caldera rim
Transfer to Santorini airport (JTR)
7.mooringTip: Have the boat alongside at Vlychada before nine for the handover; the deposit is typically released within seven days once the inspection is clean. Photograph any pre-noted damage before you sign, so nothing is disputed later.

santorini-mykonos-milos-14-days.metaDesc: Round-trip yacht charter from Santorini: Milos, Sifnos, Syros, Tinos, Mykonos, Amorgos, the Small Cyclades and Naxos across two weeks of Aegean sailing.

1.shortDescription: A 22-mile beat northwest into the Meltemi, landing at Folegandros where the Chora perches high above the little port of Karavostasi.
1.description: Stock the boat at Vlychada first thing, because once you clear the breakwater the wind is on the nose and it stays there. Twenty-two miles of close-hauled sailing sets the tone: this itinerary front-loads the hard work so the second week runs downhill. Karavostasi is the only real harbour on Folegandros, a modest curve of quay tucked under the southeast cliffs. It holds up well when the wind sits in the north and turns nasty when it swings south, so read the forecast before you commit to the night. The village worth seeing is Chora, two hundred metres overhead, either a taxi ride or a sweaty half-hour up the old stone kalderimi. Break the passage with a swim at Katergo, a pale-pebble cove you can only reach from the water.
thingsToDo: Swim into Katergo cove by dinghy
Climb the kalderimi to Chora's clifftop
Try matsata pasta with goat in a courtyard
Walk out to Ano Meria's farmsteads
1.mooringTip: Stern-to on Karavostasi quay runs €25-40 and fills fast; if there's no room, drop anchor east of the harbour on sand in 4-7 m. Clear out if a southerly is coming, the port has no protection from that quarter.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles more upwind to Milos, where the long gulf swallows the swell and Adamas gives the crew its first calm night.
2.description: Another day hard on the wind, but the reward comes at the finish. Milos is really a drowned volcanic crater, and the entrance funnels you into a huge inner gulf where the sea flattens and the breeze fades to nothing. Adamas sits right at the top of that inlet and stays quiet whichever way the wind blows, which is why crews exhale here. Time the approach to pass Sarakiniko on the north coast: chalk-white rock scoured into ridges and hollows, more moonscape than beach. It only works as a swim stop in settled air, so grab it while the sea is flat. Ashore, the painted boathouses of Klima and the castle lookout at Plaka fill an easy afternoon once you have tied up.
thingsToDo: Swim the white ridges of Sarakiniko
Snorkel the Papafragas sea caves
Climb to Plaka Kastro for the view
Photograph the syrmata boathouses at Klima
2.mooringTip: Tie stern-to along the Adamas town quay for roughly €30-50, with shelter from any direction you could ask for. Pollonia in the northeast costs less but takes the full force of a northerly Meltemi.

3.shortDescription: A long thirty-five-mile run north via Sifnos, breaking for lunch in sheltered Vathi before the easy quay at Parikia on Paros.
3.description: The longest single day of the fortnight, so leave early. Vathi on the southwest corner of Sifnos makes the natural halfway halt, a near-landlocked bay with a little monastery on the sand and holding good enough for a lazy lunch swim. From there it is another eighteen miles up to Parikia. Naoussa gets all the postcards, but Parikia is the sensible overnight: the harbour is dredged, the stern-to is honest, and you are not fighting a dozen other boats for a slip at dusk. Wander into the old town after you have secured the boat and you will find the Ekatontapiliani, a squat Byzantine church going back to the fourth century, its courtyard cool even in August. The marble lanes behind it are worth an unhurried hour.
thingsToDo: Lunch swim in Sifnos's Vathi bay
Step inside the fourth-century Ekatontapiliani
Wander Parikia's marble old-town lanes
Dinner on the Parikia harbourfront
3.mooringTip: Parikia's harbour takes stern-to at €30-50 and is far kinder than Naoussa when it comes to finding a berth. If you want the classic fishing-port scene, Naoussa is the trade-off, expect to arrive early and queue for space.

4.shortDescription: A day off the passage plan: anchor under Antiparos, swim the Kolymbithres rocks, and pedal up to the marble village of Lefkes.
4.description: No miles to log today, which frees the morning for the dull-but-necessary jobs: laundry, water, a proper provisioning run in Parikia. Once that is behind you, motor across the channel and drop the hook off the east side of Antiparos, a calm cove facing the strait with sand at four to seven metres. The swim to remember, though, is Kolymbithres, round the headland from Naoussa, where wind and salt have carved the granite into smooth grey lobes you can clamber over. Landlubbers in the crew can hire bikes and grind up to Lefkes, an old capital set back in the hills, its paved lanes some of the prettiest on the island. Come evening, swap the anchor for a berth in Parikia or Naoussa.
thingsToDo: Anchor for lunch off east Antiparos
Clamber the granite lobes at Kolymbithres
Cycle inland to Lefkes village
Taste Moraitis wine in Naoussa
4.mooringTip: Anchoring off Antiparos is free, on sand in four to seven metres; hold there only for the day. For the night, take a stern-to slot in Parikia or Naoussa and switch berths before the light goes.

5.shortDescription: Twenty-four miles on a beam reach to Syros, the finest sail of the trip, ending at the grand neoclassical quay of Ermoupoli.
5.description: If the Meltemi behaves, this is the leg you will talk about afterwards. Twenty-four miles with the wind on the beam, the boat heeled and settled, Syros lifting out of the haze ahead. Ermoupoli's quay sits deep inside the harbour and makes an easy stern-to, a relief after a spirited crossing. The town itself is unlike anywhere else in the group. Once the shipping capital of Greece, it climbs the hillsides in tiers of faded marble and pastel plaster, with the Orthodox quarter on one crest and the Catholic town of Ano Syros on the other. Walk up Miaouli Square as the cafés fill, buy a box of the local loukoumi, and you understand why islanders here never quite left for Athens.
thingsToDo: Climb to Catholic Ano Syros
Buy loukoumi from an old confectioner
Take an evening coffee on Miaouli Square
Swim off the Asteria rock platforms
5.mooringTip: The Ermoupoli town quay is free and you lie to your own anchor, with shelter that holds in almost any blow. If it is packed, the Manna marina nearby has serviced berths at €60-80.

6.shortDescription: A brisk fourteen-mile beam reach east to Tinos, marble-carvers' island, where the channel funnels the Meltemi harder than the forecast admits.
6.description: Short does not mean gentle. The gap between Syros and Tinos squeezes the northerly and adds a good five knots to whatever the forecast promised, so a twenty-five-knot afternoon in high summer is routine rather than remarkable. Sail it fast on a beam reach, but plan to have lines ashore by four, before the swell stacks up at the harbour mouth. Tinos new port takes a stern-to on middling sand holding, so lay plenty of chain; when it really pipes up, the inner basin is calmer. Beyond the pilgrimage church, this is a quiet, workaday island of dovecotes and stonemasons. Drive up to Pyrgos, where marble has been carved for generations, then loop back through the boulder fields around Volax.
thingsToDo: Watch marble carving in Pyrgos
Wander the boulder field at Volax
Follow the dovecote trail near Tarabados
Order Tinos artichokes in a village taverna
6.mooringTip: Stern-to in the new harbour costs €25-40, but the sand is only fair, so set the anchor hard and give it scope. When the Meltemi tops twenty-five knots, move into the inner small-craft basin.

7.shortDescription: Just eight miles east to Mykonos, but the channel bites; book Tourlos marina ahead and duck into Ornos for an afternoon swim.
7.description: A hop of eight miles, and half of it spent bracing. The Tinos-Mykonos channel runs a reliable few knots above the regional wind and kicks up a short, hard chop by early afternoon, so it pays to be moving early. In July and August, reserve a slot at Tourlos New Marina the day before; the old town quay is free but lies wide open to swell and only makes sense when the northerly stays under eighteen knots. With the boat settled, the day is yours. Anchor off Ornos or Psarou for a swim, then leave the boat and go find whatever version of Mykonos suits the crew, the Little Venice waterfront at sunset for some, the Armenistis lighthouse walk for others.
thingsToDo: Anchor-swim at Ornos or Psarou
Stroll Little Venice at sunset
Walk out to Armenistis lighthouse
Beach lunch at road-free Agios Sostis
7.mooringTip: Tourlos New Marina runs €70-110 a night in season and needs booking online a day ahead. The free town quay is only safe below about eighteen knots of northerly, otherwise the swell makes it miserable.

8.shortDescription: Stay put on Mykonos and sail three miles to Delos, the uninhabited sanctuary of Apollo, before an afternoon on the southern beaches.
8.description: Give the engine a rest and let the day belong to Delos. The little island three miles west is one of the great archaeological sites of the Mediterranean, an entire sacred city left to the lizards, and you can sail across, anchor off the south side in five to eight metres, and be walking among the marble lions inside the hour. Three or four hours ashore covers the Apollo sanctuary, the mosaic floors and the theatre. Wardens clear everyone off by sundown, so there is no lingering. Back aboard, point the bow at the south coast and work along Paradise, Elia and Agrari as the mood takes you, winding up at Scorpios for a drink as the light drops.
thingsToDo: Day-anchor and tour ancient Delos
Find the Terrace of the Lions
Beach-hop Paradise, Elia and Agrari
Sunset drink at Scorpios
8.mooringTip: Spend the night back at Tourlos as before. At Delos, anchor off the south shore in five to eight metres of sand, keep it a daytime stop only, and be off before the wardens close the site at dusk.

9.shortDescription: A big thirty-five-mile reach southeast to Amorgos, leaving the crowds behind for the cliff-hung monastery of Hozoviotissa.
9.description: The longest reach of the second week, and the one that changes the mood of the trip. You leave the marquee islands astern and cross open water toward Amorgos, a long spine of rock at the eastern edge of the Cyclades where charter boats thin out and the anchorages go still. Aegiali on the north coast is the safer berth when the Meltemi blows from the north; Katapola, round on the south, is the fallback.
