The reason everyone comes is Hozoviotissa, a white monastery glued to a sheer ochre cliff a few miles south, reached by three hundred steps up from the road. Climb it in the cool of the evening. The divers among you will know Amorgos as the backdrop to a certain film about the deep.
thingsToDo: Climb the 300 steps to Hozoviotissa
Dive the Olympia wreck off Liveros
Hike the ridge to Tholaria village
Try psimeni raki in a Langada taverna
9.mooringTip: Aegiali gives stern-to at €25-40 with cover from the north; if the wind swings south, cross to Katapola instead. Amorgos has few charter berths, so call ahead in July and August to be sure of a spot.

10.shortDescription: Eighteen quiet miles west into the Small Cyclades and Irakleia, an island of a hundred souls, one taverna and a cave to walk to.
10.description: This is the emptiest corner of the whole route. Irakleia is the smallest of the four inhabited Small Cyclades, and it wears its size plainly: a hundred or so residents, a single road, a scatter of tavernas around the harbour at Agios Georgios. The big-island crowds simply never make it out here. Anchor in the bay on good sand and let the pace slow to match the place. The one set outing is the Cave of Agios Ioannis, half an hour on foot above the village of Panagia, a proper limestone chamber you scramble down into with a torch. Otherwise the day is for swimming off Livadi, the best strip of sand in the group, and eating grilled fish where the boats come in.
thingsToDo: Hike to the Cave of Agios Ioannis
Swim off Livadi beach
Eat grilled fish at the harbour
Walk up to the old kastro
10.mooringTip: Drop anchor in Agios Georgios bay on sand in five to seven metres, tucked out of the northerly. The few quay slots go for €15-25 but have no lazy lines, so be ready to lay your own.

11.shortDescription: Twenty miles northwest to Naxos, biggest and greenest of the Cyclades, where the marble Portara frames the harbour sunset.
11.description: Naxos is the outlier in this crowd of arid rocks: big, fertile, mountainous, with peaks over a thousand metres and valleys full of olive and citrus. The town harbour is well sheltered and takes a stern-to without drama, and there is usually room even at the height of summer. On the islet guarding the entrance stands the Portara, a lone marble doorway left over from a temple to Apollo that was never finished, and it is the spot the whole town walks out to at sunset. If you have the legs for it, Mount Zas behind Filoti is the highest point in the Cyclades and a four-hour climb. If you would rather taste than trudge, the Tragaea villages pour a citron liqueur found nowhere else.
thingsToDo: Watch sunset through the Portara
Hike Mount Zas above Filoti
Taste kitron liqueur at Halki
Swim the sands of Agios Prokopios
11.mooringTip: Naxos town harbour takes stern-to at €30-50 and holds well in any wind, with more spare capacity than most stops on the route. No need to book, but arriving mid-afternoon secures the better berths.

12.shortDescription: Twenty miles south to Ios, swimming at Manganari before rounding to Ormos and the late-night din of the hilltop Chora.
12.description: Twenty miles south on a Meltemi reach, threading the channel below Naxos. Break the run at Manganari, a set of sandy bays on the south coast of Ios that stay sheltered when the north wind is up, then work back round to Ormos for the night. Ios has one reputation, and it earns it after dark: the Chora, strung along the ridge above the port, empties into its own alleys around eleven and turns into an open-air party that runs till dawn through the summer. It is not for everyone, and you can sit it out at anchor in the bay. But climb up for the sunset by the windmills at least once, when the crowds are still warming up and the light is on the water.
thingsToDo: Swim the bays at Manganari
Watch sunset by the Chora windmills
Climb to the reputed tomb of Homer
Wander the Chora's whitewashed lanes
12.mooringTip: Take a stern-to slot in Ormos harbour at €25-40; the big Mylopotas beach is fine for a daytime anchor but untenable overnight in the Meltemi. Manganari gives good north-wind shelter for a lunch stop.

13.shortDescription: The last leg, eighteen miles south to Santorini, with a caldera-edge swim off Thirasia before berthing at Vlychada for the handover.
13.description: Eighteen miles closes the loop back to where it started. Break the passage at Thirasia, the small inhabited island on the western rim of the caldera, for a last proper swim in deep blue water. The caldera itself is a sight but not a berth: it drops away to more than three hundred metres almost at the cliff foot, so anchoring is out and the mooring buoys off Thirasia are not to be trusted with a charter boat overnight. Vlychada on the south coast is the only sensible place to lie, and it is where you hand back the boat in the morning. Book a taxi up to Oia for the cliff-edge sunset, and toast a fortnight that ran the full arc of the Cyclades.
thingsToDo: Swim the caldera edge off Thirasia
Taxi to Oia for the sunset
Taste Assyrtiko at Santo Wines
Crew dinner in cliff-top Megalochori
13.mooringTip: Vlychada Marina takes stern-to at €60-100 and should be booked well ahead in peak season. Confirm your handover time with the base a day out, and get in before the afternoon swell sets into the entrance.

14.shortDescription: Handover at Vlychada before nine, a last swim beneath Oia at Ammoudi, then the short transfer to Santorini airport.
14.description: Last morning, and it runs to a familiar script. Have the boat cleared and ready by nine for the handover, walk the inspection with the base manager aboard, and expect the deposit back within a week once everything checks out. Photograph anything that gets noted before you sign, just so there is no argument later. The airport at Santorini is a twenty-five-minute drive from Vlychada, which leaves time for one last ritual if your flight is not too early: a taxi over to Ammoudi, the little fishing cove below Oia, for a cold-water swim off the rocks and a plate of something grilled at a waterside table before you point yourselves home.
thingsToDo: Walk the handover inspection with the base
Photograph any noted damage before signing
Last swim at Ammoudi below Oia
Grilled lunch at an Ammoudi taverna
14.mooringTip: Hand the boat back at Vlychada before nine, cleared and fuelled to the terms of your contract. Photograph any damage the manager records before you sign, and the deposit should clear within seven days.

skiathos-route.metaDesc: Sail seven days from Skiathos through Skopelos, Skyros and the Alonissos Marine Park, with monk-seal anchorages, cliff villages and golden Koukounaries.

1.shortDescription: An easy 15-mile warm-up east to Skopelos, with a swim at Stafylos and a stern-to berth under the pine-clad old town.
1.description: Fifteen miles is a gentle first leg, and a good one for shaking down the boat before the longer hops ahead. The meltémi here blows from the north at fifteen to twenty knots through July and August, softer than anything you'd meet in the Cyclades, so most crews carry a comfortable beam reach across to Skopelos. Drop the hook at Stafylos, four miles short of town, for a lunch swim over sand. Skopelos harbour takes you stern-to along the quay, well tucked from the northerly. In the evening, climb the stone lanes above the waterfront as the light goes gold. The whitewashed chapel from the Mamma Mia films, Agios Ioannis Kastri, sits out on the east coast and is easiest reached by road. Book a taxi if you want it before the day-trip crowds.
thingsToDo: Swim over sand at Stafylos Bay
Climb Skopelos old town's stone lanes
Taxi to Agios Ioannis Kastri chapel
Order yemista at Ouzeri Anatoli
1.mooringTip: Berth stern-to along Skopelos town quay, roughly €25-40 a night and sheltered from the northerly. If it fills up, Loutraki over on the Glossa side is a quieter fallback.

2.shortDescription: A long 38-mile beam reach southeast to Skyros, the biggest and most far-flung of the Sporades, tying up at little Linaria.
2.description: This is the longest day of the route, close to forty miles, so we usually slip the lines by nine to reach Linaria in daylight. With the meltémi behind the beam it's a fast, rolling sail; keep an eye on the swell building on the quarter as you clear the shelter of Skopelos. Skyros sits apart from the rest of the group, and it feels it. Linaria, a small working harbour on the southwest coast, gives good protection from the north. From there it's a short taxi or a stiff walk up to Chora, the old town spilling down beneath a Byzantine castle. Wander the lanes and you'll pass workshops carving the island's famous little wooden horses. Save an appetite for goat stew in a courtyard taverna once the heat drops.
thingsToDo: Hike up to Chora's Byzantine castle
Watch a craftsman carve a Skyrian horse
Swim at Pefkos Beach, south coast
Eat goat stew in a Chora courtyard
2.mooringTip: Go stern-to in Linaria, about €25-40 a night with solid cover from the north. For a quiet night on the hook instead, Atsitsa Bay on the west coast is well protected.

3.shortDescription: Thirty-two miles northwest into the marine park to an empty island, anchoring alone in the near-landlocked pool of Planitis Bay.
3.description: Kyra Panagia has no village, no road and nowhere to tie up, which is exactly the appeal. You come here to anchor and to have the place more or less to yourself. Planitis, on the north coast, is an almost enclosed lagoon; motor in through the narrow entrance and drop onto sand in five to seven metres, sheltered whatever the meltémi is doing. This is the heart of the Alonissos marine park, home to Europe's monk seals, so keep the swimming quiet and the spearguns stowed. Ashore, a half-hour climb leads to a working twelfth-century monastery still tended by a monk or two. Come evening there's little to do but eat in the cockpit under the anchor light, listen to the cicadas in the scrub, and count stars.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in Planitis Bay
Hike to the 12th-century Panagia Monastery
Snorkel quietly for monk seals
Read on deck as the cicadas start
3.mooringTip: This is a free anchorage on sand in five to seven metres, protected from the north with no services ashore. Park rules apply: no spearfishing and no fires on the beach.

4.shortDescription: A relaxed 15 miles south to Alonissos, the only lived-in island in the park, berthing at the ferry town of Patitiri.
4.description: After the solitude of Kyra Panagia, fifteen easy miles bring you back to signs of life. Alonissos is the only island people actually live on inside the reserve, the largest protected stretch of sea in Europe, and it wears that lightly. Patitiri, the main harbour on the south coast, is where the charter boats go stern-to among the ferries and fishing caiques. Leave the waterfront and climb, on foot or by taxi, to Old Alonissos, the original village high on the ridge, car-free and quiet after the day-trippers leave. Divers can visit the Peristera wreck, a fifth-century BC cargo ship of amphorae, but only with a licensed operator. Back down at the port, the local kitchens do a good lobster pasta, the astakomakaronada that Alonissos is known for. Steni Vala, a few miles east, is the calmer overnight if the harbour feels busy.
thingsToDo: Walk car-free Old Alonissos on the ridge
Dive the 5th-century BC Peristera wreck
Try astakomakaronada lobster pasta at the port
Join a marine-park snorkel trip
4.mooringTip: Patitiri takes you stern-to at roughly €25-40 a night, sheltered from the north among the ferry berths. Steni Vala on the east coast is smaller and usually calmer if you'd rather not fight for space.

5.shortDescription: Twenty miles west back toward Skiathos to anchor off Koukounaries, a curve of golden sand backed by umbrella pines.
5.description: Twenty miles carries you back into Skiathos waters and to Koukounaries, the beach that fronts half the island's postcards. It's a long arc of pale gold sand with a pine forest pressing right down to the shore, and from the water it earns the reputation. Anchor off the beach in four to six metres over good sand, tucked in from the northerly. Behind the dunes lies a small wetland worth an hour's wander, where herons stalk the shallows and, some springs, flamingos stop over. This is a livelier corner of the trip after the quiet islands, so make the most of it. We often stay on the hook for sunset, then dinghy across to Achladies for a drink. If the anchorage is crowded or the wind swings, run the last couple of miles into Skiathos Marina and take a paid berth on the town quay.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Koukounaries' golden sand
Walk the wetland behind the beach
Sundowner at Achladies Beach Bar
Catch rembetika near Bourtzi in Skiathos Town
5.mooringTip: Anchoring off Koukounaries is free, on sand in four to six metres and sheltered from the north. When it blows up or fills in, the town quay at Skiathos Marina is the paid stern-to fallback nearby.

6.shortDescription: Just eight miles home, with time to detour to boat-only Lalaria before settling onto the Skiathos town quay for the last night.
6.description: Only eight miles separate Koukounaries from the marina, so there's no rush and plenty of reason to take the long way round the top of the island. Lalaria is the stop to make: a beach of smooth white pebbles under pale chalk cliffs, reachable only from the sea, with a natural rock arch at one end. Anchor off, swim in, and mind your footing on the stones. The water there is startlingly clear over the pale bottom. From Lalaria it's a short run down to Skiathos town, where you tie up stern-to on the quay beneath the little Bourtzi fort guarding the harbour mouth. Spend the last evening ashore over grilled octopus at one of the waterfront tavernas, then square the boat away for morning. Top up fuel at the entrance berth before you take your spot.
thingsToDo: Swim at boat-only Lalaria Beach
Photograph Lalaria's white rock arch
Walk out to the Bourtzi fort
Grilled octopus at Taverna Alektor
6.mooringTip: Skiathos Marina is a lazy-line stern-to berth at around €40-60 a night. Refuel at the fuel berth by the entrance before you back in, and confirm your handover slot with the base a day ahead.

7.shortDescription: Handover at Skiathos Marina before nine, a last spanakopita in town, then a five-minute hop to the airport.
7.description: The boat is usually due back before nine, so it pays to have bags packed and lockers cleared the night before. The base manager walks the deck and hull with you, checks the inventory, and signs off; with nothing amiss the deposit comes back to your card within a week or so. If there's an hour to spare, wander into Skiathos town for a last frappé and a warm spanakopita from the bakery before the taxi. Skiathos airport sits barely five minutes up the road from the marina, one of the shortest transfers in the Aegean, which makes the final morning refreshingly unhurried. A week in the Sporades tends to send people home already planning the next boat.
thingsToDo: Final walk-through with the base manager
Grab a spanakopita from Skiathos Bakery
Last frappé at a town café
Five-minute transfer to Skiathos airport
7.mooringTip: Have the boat back on the Skiathos Marina quay before 09:00 for the handover inspection. The deposit is typically released within seven days once the checks are done.

volos-skopelos.metaDesc: Sail seven days from Volos through Trikeri, Skiathos, Alonissos, Skopelos, the Sporades Marine Park and quiet Pelion anchorages.

1.shortDescription: An easy twenty miles south through the Pagasetic Gulf to the island of Trikeri, the Pelion cliffs standing over your starboard side all afternoon.
1.description: This first leg eases everyone in. Twenty miles of flat gulf water gives the crew time to sort out lines and lunch before anything demanding. The Pelion peninsula runs the whole way along to starboard, its slopes dropping steeply into the sea, and the wind inside the gulf stays kinder than out in the open Sporades. We usually reach Trikeri by mid-afternoon. The little island sits right at the gulf's mouth, and its harbour holds well on sand in four to six metres, sheltered whichever way the breeze turns overnight. Once the anchor is down, walk up the ridge to Panagia Evangelistria, a whitewashed monastery from 1734 that looks back over the water you just crossed. Back at the quay, order the saganaki shrimp and let the first evening go slow.
thingsToDo: Climb to Panagia Evangelistria monastery (1734)
Snorkel the rocky shore off the harbour
Order saganaki shrimp at the quay
Wander Trikeri's stone-paved village lanes
1.mooringTip: Anchoring in Trikeri harbour is free and holds on sand at four to six metres, with all-round shelter. If you would rather tie up, the village quay has a handful of slots at roughly EUR 15-25 a night.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles east to Skiathos, dropping the hook off Koukounaries where a pine forest backs one of the Aegean's best golden beaches.
2.description: Leaving the gulf, you feel the open Sporades for the first time. The meltemi can fill in from the north by early afternoon, so we like to be off Koukounaries before it does. The bay sits on Skiathos's southwest corner, its long arc of soft sand held in by pine woods that come almost to the waterline. Anchor on sand in four to six metres; the holding is good and the headland cuts the northerly. Row ashore and walk the Strofilia wetland trail behind the beach, where herons and the odd flamingo work the shallows. If the crew wants livelier water, paddle round to Banana Beach, which has no road in. Later, the beach bars at Achladies do a decent sundowner without the town crowds.
thingsToDo: Swim off Koukounaries' golden sand
Walk the Strofilia wetland flamingo trail
Paddle to road-free Banana Beach
Sundowners at Achladies beach bars
2.mooringTip: Koukounaries is a free anchorage on sand at four to six metres, protected from the north. If the meltemi swings or you want shore power, Skiathos town quay takes you stern-to for a fee.

3.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles east into the Alonissos Marine Park, anchoring at Tzortzi Bay a short hop from the ancient Peristera shipwreck.
3.description: Today you cross into the National Marine Park of the Northern Sporades, the largest protected sea area in Europe and the last stronghold of the Mediterranean monk seal. Keep an eye out on the passage; sightings are rare but not unheard of. Tzortzi Bay cuts into Alonissos's west coast, a calm notch that holds on sand in five to seven metres with good cover from north and east. Just off the entrance lies the Peristera wreck, a merchant ship that went down in the fifth century BC with its full cargo of amphorae still in place. You can only visit it with a licensed dive operator, but knowing it's down there adds something to the swim. Ashore later, the hilltop lanes of Old Alonissos are worth the taxi up.
thingsToDo: Swim the sheltered sand of Tzortzi Bay
Dive the fifth-century BC Peristera wreck
Astakomakaronada (lobster pasta) in Patitiri
Wander car-free Old Alonissos village
3.mooringTip: Tzortzi Bay anchors free on sand at five to seven metres, sheltered from north and east. For a secure overnight with services, Patitiri harbour is the stern-to alternative. Note the Park rules: no spearfishing anywhere inside.

4.shortDescription: A short eight-mile hop north to uninhabited Kyra Panagia, anchoring in the near-landlocked Planitis Bay below a twelfth-century monastery.
4.description: This is the day the trip goes quiet. Kyra Panagia has no village, no road and no quay, just an island the Marine Park keeps for the birds and the seals. Planitis Bay on the north coast is why boats come; a narrow entrance opens into an almost enclosed lagoon that shrugs off any northerly, with the anchor biting sand in five to seven metres. There is nothing to buy here, so provision well before you leave Alonissos. Once you're settled, hike the half-hour up to the Panagia Monastery, a Byzantine foundation tied to Mount Athos that still watches over the bay. The snorkelling along the rocky edges is some of the clearest on the route, and dusk over an empty anchorage is the reason people sail the Sporades.
thingsToDo: Anchor in the enclosed Planitis lagoon
Hike to the Byzantine Panagia Monastery
Snorkel the clear rocky shallows
Watch dusk over an empty anchorage
4.mooringTip: Planitis is anchor-only and free, holding on sand at five to seven metres with near total shelter. The island has no water, fuel or shops, so top up at Alonissos first. Park rules ban beach fires and spearfishing.

5.shortDescription: Twenty miles west to green Skopelos, tying stern-to in the amphitheatre harbour beneath the town's pine-covered slopes and tiled roofs.
5.description: Skopelos is the greenest of the Sporades, its hills shaggy with pine right down to the shoreline. The run west is open water, so watch the afternoon northerly and reef early if it builds. Skopelos town wraps around its harbour like a theatre; you tie stern-to on the quay, well covered from the north, with cafes and bakeries a few steps from the boat. Try the local plum products and the island's cheese pie while you're ashore. The headline excursion is Agios Ioannis Kastri, the tiny chapel on its rock stack that stood in for the wedding scene in Mamma Mia, a short drive up the east coast. Below it, Kastani Beach is where much of the film was shot, and it swims as well as it photographs.
thingsToDo: Visit the Mamma Mia chapel, Agios Ioannis Kastri
Swim at Kastani Beach film location
Taste Skopelos plum pie and cheese pie
Wander Glossa's terracotta-roofed hillside lanes
5.mooringTip: Skopelos town quay is stern-to at roughly EUR 25-40 a night, sheltered from the north but exposed to a rare southerly. Loutraki below Glossa is the quieter alternative on the northwest end.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen miles southwest back to the Pelion mainland and Agia Kiriaki, a working boatyard village of two quayside tavernas and olive groves.
6.description: After the islands, Agia Kiriaki feels like stepping back a few decades. It's a small fishing settlement on the southern tip of Pelion, where a traditional shipyard still lays down wooden hulls the old way and only a couple of tavernas front the quay. The approach is 15 miles of usually easy sailing, and the cove takes an anchor on sand in four to six metres with cover from the north. The slopes above are thick with olive groves, some of the most fragrant on the mainland, and a footpath climbs through them if the crew has energy left. Order the spanakopita and finish with honey and thick yogurt. There's little to do here beyond eat, swim and watch the boatbuilders work, which after a week of sailing is exactly the point.
thingsToDo: Watch caiques built at the working shipyard
Spanakopita and honey yogurt at the quay
Snorkel the rocks north of the cove
Walk the olive-grove path above the village
6.mooringTip: Agia Kiriaki anchors free on sand at four to six metres, sheltered from the north. A few village quay slots run EUR 15-20 a night, but there are no lazy lines, so carry a long line ashore.

7.shortDescription: Fifteen miles north up the gulf back to Volos, with a last swim at Agios Ioannis before refuelling and handing back the boat.
7.description: The last leg retraces the gulf, an easy 15 miles north with the Pelion again on the beam. There's no rush, so break the passage with a swim; Agios Ioannis, on Pelion's east side, has clean sand and usually calm water in the morning. Volos Marina sits at the very head of the Pagasetic Gulf, big and well protected, with full services and a fuel berth at the entrance. Refuel before you tie up, then take the stern-to berth on the lazy lines. Once the boat is squared away, the Volos waterfront is made for a last evening: pull up a chair at a seafront ouzeri, order grilled octopus and a carafe of tsipouro, and toast a week that ran from mountain gulf to marine park and back.
thingsToDo: Morning swim at Agios Ioannis beach
Refuel at the Volos Marina fuel berth
Grilled octopus and tsipouro at a seafront ouzeri
Stroll the Volos waterfront promenade
7.mooringTip: Volos Marina is stern-to with lazy lines at around EUR 40-60 a night, with full shelter and services. Refuel at the entrance berth before mooring, and confirm your handover slot a day ahead.

zakynthos.metaDesc: Sail a week around Zakynthos: Keri caves, the Navagio shipwreck, the Blue Caves at Cape Skinari and Marathonisi turtle bay, starting from Zakynthos Town.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 12-mile run south under the island's lee to Keri Bay, timed for an afternoon dinghy tour of the sea caves.
1.description: We usually cast off from Zakynthos Town mid-morning and point the bow south. The first leg keeps the island to starboard, so the water stays flat and the breeze rarely climbs past a comfortable twelve knots on the beam. By early afternoon the cliffs at Cape Keri come up on the bow, rising a sheer eighty metres from a sea that darkens to ink beneath them. Keri Bay is where you drop the hook. Ashore, the natural tar springs at the head of the bay have been seeping pitch since antiquity, and the lighthouse walk up the cape opens a long view across the strait to the Peloponnese. Save the dinghy for golden hour: run it into the caves on the point, where the light bends through the limestone arches and floods the pool an unreal blue. Order saganaki at a cliff-edge taverna and watch the sun go.
thingsToDo: Dinghy into the Keri sea caves
Walk up to Keri lighthouse viewpoint
Swim beneath the limestone arches
See the natural tar springs
Flame-lit saganaki at a cliff taverna
1.mooringTip: Anchor free on sand in 5 to 8 metres; holding is excellent, but back down hard to check the set, as weed patches lurk along the eastern edge. In August the bay packs with day-boats by early evening, so drop the hook before four if you want room.

2.shortDescription: A lazy 6-mile hop east across Laganas Bay to the pine-backed quay at Porto Koukla, best reached before the afternoon breeze fills.
2.description: Barely a passage at all, this one. Six miles of easy reaching east brings you to Porto Koukla, a quiet corner of the south coast that the charter fleet mostly sails past. The draw here is underwater. Thick meadows of posidonia carpet the shallows in three to five metres, and a mask and fins will usually turn up octopus, bream and the odd shy moray tucked into the grass. The pine-shaded coast road runs flat in both directions, which makes for a good dawn jog before the heat settles in. This stretch of coast sits well away from any resort glare, so the night sky here is genuinely dark; stay up past midnight and the Milky Way arcs right down to the masthead. Eat simply and eat local, and let the boat swing quietly on her lines.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the posidonia meadows for octopus
Paddleboard to a quiet pebble cove
Order bianco fish stew on the quay
Midnight stargazing over Laganas Bay
Dawn run along the pine coast road
2.mooringTip: Take a lazy line on the small quay for a modest fee, but slots are few and go first-come. The berth faces south, so if a southwest swell gets up overnight, fall back four miles west to anchor on sand in Keri Bay. Expect nothing here beyond fresh water.

3.shortDescription: Eighteen miles round Cape Keri to Vromi and the Navagio shipwreck, a fair-weather-only leg with no bail-out along the cliffs.
3.description: Read the forecast twice before you commit to this one. Rounding Cape Keri puts you on the exposed west coast, where chalk walls climb two hundred metres for the best part of twenty kilometres and there is nowhere to duck in if the weather turns. Rockfall is common enough that stretches of the shoreline are now buoyed off. Vromi is the single sheltered notch on the whole side, a north-facing inlet with deep sand under the keel. Tender round the corner to Navagio itself, the crescent of pale sand where the freighter Panagiotis has lain rusting since it ran aground in 1980. Land by dinghy for the photograph, but keep clear of the cliff base, which sheds stone without warning. Falcons wheel along the rock faces overhead. Toast the day on deck with a glass of Verdea as the light goes amber.
thingsToDo: Tender into Navagio Shipwreck Cove
Photograph the rusted Panagiotis wreck
Watch Eleonora's falcons on the cliffs
Swim below the two-hundred-metre chalk walls
Sunset glass of Verdea on deck
3.mooringTip: There is no quay at Vromi, so anchor free on sand in 8 to 12 metres tight against the south wall for shelter from the north. The bay is wide open to the west and southwest; leave at the first hint of a southerly, and if the wind backs overnight run back east round Cape Keri to Porto Koukla.

4.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles round Cape Skinari to the little port of Agios Nikolaos, gateway to the Blue Caves and a calm night on the east coast.
4.description: The long haul north round Cape Skinari is the last of the open-water work, and it eases the moment you clear the point. Under the lee of the headland the wind drops away and the run down to Agios Nikolaos settles into a lazy beam reach. The morning's excursion is the Blue Caves on the cape, a run of sea grottoes where sunlight bounces off the white rock underwater and turns the whole pool a hard cobalt. The little glass-bottom boats work the circuit out of Skinari, but you can do it yourself with a dinghy, a torch and a snorkel. Agios Nikolaos is a working fishing harbour, its blue-domed church the one landmark on the front. Walk the half-hour up to the Cape Skinari lighthouse if the legs need stretching, for the long view north across the strait to Kefalonia. Rabbit stifado, slow-cooked with onions and cinnamon, is the plate to order under the vines.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the cobalt Blue Caves
See the old Cape Skinari windmills
Rabbit stifado in a vine-shaded courtyard
Coffee under the blue church dome
Sunset photo from Cape Skinari
4.mooringTip: Berth stern-to or on a lazy line at the Agios Nikolaos quay for a small fee; slots are limited, so phone ahead in August. The harbour shelters well from the prevailing north but chops up in a southeast blow; if it fills, anchor a mile south at Makris Gialos on sand in 5 to 7 metres.

5.shortDescription: An easy 12-mile downwind run to Tsilivi's wide sand bay, the handiest base for a trip inland to Bochali castle and the Volimes wine villages.
5.description: Nothing taxing today. Twelve miles down the sheltered east coast with the thermal on the beam, and the wide bay at Tsilivi opens up by mid-afternoon. The anchoring is as simple as it gets: clean sand in four to six metres, good holding, shelter from everything bar a southerly. The point of stopping short of town is what lies behind the beach. Tsilivi is the closest hop to the Venetian walls at Bochali on the ridge above Zante Town, where the whole island lays itself out below you in one sweep. Push further inland and the mountain villages around Volimes make the local cheese and ceramics; carry a wheel of ladotyri, matured in olive oil, back down to the boat. Come evening, the promenade is a quiet stroll, and the tavernas at the south end put out the better plates.
thingsToDo: Swim the long Tsilivi sand crescent
Drive up to Bochali for the panorama
Tour the Volimes mountain wine villages
Buy ladotyri cheese at the market
Sundowner on the seafront promenade
5.mooringTip: Anchor on clean sand in 4 to 6 metres with good holding, well tucked from the north and northwest. Nothing here breaks a southerly, though; if one is forecast, shift five miles down to the Zakynthos Town quay for the night. Expect no shore services in the bay.

6.shortDescription: Ten miles round the southeast corner into the Laganas marine park and Marathonisi, the loggerhead turtles' nesting bay, anchored clear of the buoys.
6.description: Ten miles takes you round the southeast tip and into Laganas Bay, the single most important loggerhead nesting ground in the Mediterranean. Marathonisi, the little uninhabited islet the tour boats call Turtle Island, is the anchorage, but this is a national marine park and the rules bite. Drop the hook only on the south side, well outside the buoyed exclusion line; the north end is off-limits to protect the nests. No jet skis, no wakeboards, and no amplified music after ten at night through the May-to-October season. Between June and August the turtles come up to breathe every ten or fifteen minutes, close enough to the boat to watch from the cockpit. The sand crescent on the islet's south end is the backdrop everyone photographs. Save dinner for the tavernas under the cliffs at Limni Keriou across the water.
thingsToDo: Watch loggerheads surface off the cockpit
Swim the south-end Marathonisi crescent
Snorkel the limestone caves nearby
Photograph the islet at golden hour
Dinner ashore at Limni Keriou
6.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in 4 to 6 metres off the south end of the islet, always outside the buoyed nesting zone; the protected north end is closed to boats. It holds against the north and northwest, but shift two miles west to Limni Keriou if a southeast swell builds. Park rules ban jet skis and cut music after 22:00.

7.shortDescription: A short 8-mile hop round Cape Gerakas back to Zakynthos Town, with a last swim off Pelouzo Islet before the evening hand-back.
7.description: The last leg is a gentle eight miles round Cape Gerakas and back up the coast to where the week began. Pelouzo Islet, a lone rock a mile offshore, makes the final swim stop, with deep water over pale sand and the kind of clarity that earns a last photograph. Zakynthos Town was flattened by the 1953 earthquake and rebuilt close to its old form, so the arcaded seafront looks Venetian even though the stone is barely seventy years old. Take the evening volta through Solomos Square at the north end, then find a bar under the bougainvillea for a farewell drink. Order souzoukakia, the cumin-scented meatballs that came over with the refugees from Smyrna, at a taverna up on the Bochali hill. Berth on the town quay by six, and let tomorrow's flight wait.
thingsToDo: Last swim off Pelouzo Islet
Evening volta through Solomos Square
Souzoukakia at a Bochali hill taverna
Honeyed baklava from a harbour bakery
Hand-back briefing at the town quay
7.mooringTip: The town quay is free and takes you stern-to, though it sits exposed to the south and southwest, so double your stern lines should a southerly get up overnight. Be alongside by 18:00 Friday so the technical check-out is finished, then sleep aboard and clear the boat by 09:00 Saturday. It's a quarter-hour ride south to the airport.

sporades-grand-tour-14-days.metaDesc: Two weeks from Skiathos Marina through Skopelos, Alonissos, the marine park, Skyros and the Pagasetic Gulf, with anchorages and taverna moorings.

1.shortDescription: Slip out of Skiathos and cross to Skopelos town, where whitewashed houses climb the hillside above a busy little harbour.
1.description: Most crews collect the boat at Skiathos and want to be moving by late morning, once provisioning is done. The hop to Skopelos town runs around 15 miles, an easy first leg with the afternoon breeze usually filling in from the north. Skopelos Chora rises straight out of the water in tiers of white houses and blue shutters, the kind of skyline that has people reaching for a camera before you have even tied up. The town quay gets tight in July, and the ferries need room to swing, so we tuck into the southeast corner of the port and set the anchor there. Ashore, the lanes climb past small churches and shaded courtyards. Dinner is easy: grilled fish and a cold bottle of local white a few steps from the water.
thingsToDo: Climb the lanes to Panagitsa tou Pyrgou chapel
Eat grilled fish on the Chora waterfront
Browse the plane-tree platia at dusk
Watch sunset over the harbour rooftops
1.mooringTip: The town quay fills by mid-afternoon in summer and ferries need swinging room, so either arrive early for a stern-to berth or anchor off in the southeast corner clear of the ferry lane.

2.shortDescription: A short leg east to Alonissos, ending at Patitiri or the fishing hamlet of Steni Vala with its lobster tavernas.
2.description: Alonissos sits a comfortable morning's sail from Skopelos, roughly ten to fourteen miles depending on where you drop the hook. Patitiri, the island's main port, has everything you need to top up: water, fuel, ice and a decent run of shops behind the front. If you would rather trade services for atmosphere, carry on to Steni Vala, a working fishing village further up the east coast facing Peristera. The taverns there put out whatever the boats brought in that morning, and the lobster is worth the detour. We often take on provisions at Patitiri in the afternoon, then move up to Steni Vala for a quieter night on the anchor. Either way you are now inside the marine park, so it pays to read up on the zones before pushing further north tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Provision and fuel up at Patitiri port
Eat fresh lobster at a Steni Vala taverna
Ride up to Old Alonissos hilltop Chora
Swim off Peristera's sheltered Xero Bay
2.mooringTip: Patitiri is the more sheltered, better-serviced overnight; Steni Vala's small quay fills fast, so grab a taverna berth early and expect to raft in high season.

3.shortDescription: Push north into the marine park to Kyra Panagia, an almost landlocked bay watched over by a Byzantine monastery.
3.description: This is the day the trip goes properly wild. Kyra Panagia lies deep inside the National Marine Park, ten miles or so north of Alonissos, and there is nothing on it but goats, a monastery and one of the safest anchorages in the Sporades. Planitis bay almost closes over on itself, so the water stays flat even when it is blowing outside. Holding is good over sand and weed, though we usually back up with a long line ashore to sit clear of other boats and the gusts that tumble off the ridge. There are no shops and no tavernas here, so cook aboard and enjoy the silence. A rough track leads up to the whitewashed monastery of Kyra Panagia, a dependency of Mount Athos, worth the walk for the view alone.
thingsToDo: Anchor with a line ashore in Planitis bay
Walk up to the hilltop Kyra Panagia monastery
Snorkel the clear water near the entrance
Cook the day's catch on board at anchor
3.mooringTip: Planitis is nearly enclosed and very secure; set the anchor in sand and run a long line to the rocks, ideally in daylight, since there are no facilities and no room to sort it out after dark.

4.shortDescription: A longer open passage south to Skyros, the Sporades outlier of wild ponies, and its tucked-away harbour at Linaria.
4.description: Skyros stands apart from the rest of the group, and getting there means the longest passage of the fortnight, close to thirty miles of open water. Leave early. The wind tends to build through the afternoon, so a nine o'clock start gives you a fair chance of an easy sail rather than a slog. Linaria, on the west coast, is the island's only real harbour, and it is a snug one. Anchoring inside is awkward because the bottom is laid with private moorings and the holding is patchy, so most yachts go stern-to along the west quay, rafting up when it is busy. Skyros feels different once you are ashore: a Cycladic-white hilltop town, Byzantine roots, and the small wild ponies the island is known for. Book ahead in high season if you can, as space is limited.
thingsToDo: Bus up to the whitewashed Skyros Chora
See the endemic Skyrian wild ponies
Visit Rupert Brooke's grave at Tris Boukes
Eat at a Linaria harbourfront taverna
4.mooringTip: Linaria's seabed is cluttered with private moorings and holds poorly, so plan on going stern-to at the west quay and rafting in July; call ahead or arrive by early afternoon to be sure of a spot.

5.shortDescription: Turn back north across open water to Kyra Panagia, trading Skyros's harbour bustle for a second night at anchor.
5.description: The route retraces the long leg north today, back over the channel that separates Skyros from the marine park. It is the same water as day four but rarely the same sail, and with the wind often on a kinder angle the miles can pass quickly. Aim to be off the Skyros quay by mid-morning so you reach Kyra Panagia with the light still high. If Planitis felt too enclosed on the way down, the alternative is Agios Petros on the southeast side, a string of coves where a line ashore keeps you off your neighbours and steadies the boat against the katabatic gusts. Once the hook is set, the day belongs to swimming and doing very little. No engines, no shops, just the marine park doing what it does best after dark: complete quiet.
thingsToDo: Anchor in an Agios Petros cove, line ashore
Swim off the empty southeast shore
Keep watch for Mediterranean monk seals
Stargaze with no shore lights around
5.mooringTip: If the wind has veered easterly, Planitis stays flat while Agios Petros gets gusty; pick the corner sheltered from the day's breeze and always run a line ashore in these narrow coves.

6.shortDescription: Drop back south to Tzortzi Bay on Alonissos, a wide, sheltered anchorage ringed by pine with no crowds and no shops.
6.description: Coming out of the marine park, the sensible next stop is Tzortzi Bay, back on the east coast of Alonissos and about ten miles south. It is a big, open bay with room to spread out, backed by pine and scrub, and it stays calm in almost anything short of a hard easterly. There is nothing ashore, which is rather the point after a busy week. You get green water, the smell of the pines off the hills, and not much else to bother you. We tend to arrive by early afternoon, get the anchor well dug into the sand, and let the crew swim off the back of the boat until the light goes soft. If the forecast leans east, keep a bay or two further south in mind, because Tzortzi opens straight to that quarter.
thingsToDo: Anchor over sand and swim all afternoon
Paddle the pine-backed shoreline by dinghy
Snorkel the rocky edges of the bay
Grill dinner aboard at anchor
6.mooringTip: Tzortzi holds well in sand and shelters from north and west, but it is wide open to the east, so check the forecast before committing to the night and move on if a fresh easterly is due.

7.shortDescription: A gentle coastal hop to Patitiri, time to reprovision, then climb to the old hilltop Chora above Alonissos's port.
7.description: Today is short and mostly about resetting. Patitiri is only a handful of miles down the coast from Tzortzi, so there is no rush to leave, and the run along the cliffs of eastern Alonissos is a pretty one. Patitiri is the island's proper harbour, with water and fuel on the quay and shops close behind for the second-week provisioning. Once the boat is squared away, take the old mule path or a quick taxi up to Alonissos Chora, the original hilltop town abandoned after the 1965 earthquake and slowly brought back to life. The views over the Sporades from up there are the best on the island. Back down at sea level, the tavernas along the front are unfussy and good, and it is an easy evening before turning back west tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Provision and refuel on Patitiri quay
Walk the mule path up to Alonissos Chora
Sip a drink at Hayiati with the view
Browse the old town's folklore museum
7.mooringTip: Patitiri is stern-to on the quay with laid lines and good shelter; the inner corners fill first in season, so if you want easy fuel and water access, aim to be alongside before the afternoon ferry rush.

8.shortDescription: Sail back west to Skopelos, this time swapping the town quay for the pine-fringed anchorage at Panormos or Agnontas.
8.description: Heading back toward home waters, the leg to Skopelos runs a dozen miles or so across open channel, usually a fast reach when the meltemi is up. Rather than repeat the town, this is a good night to try one of the island's south-coast bays. Panormos is the pick for many: a deep, sandy inlet with room to anchor and clear water for swimming, though the evening wind funnels down off the hills, so leave a bit of scope. Agnontas, a little further east, is the fallback when it blows hard from the north, with a long breakwater you can lie stern-to. Both are quiet, both have a taverna or two at the water's edge, and neither has the ferry traffic of Chora. A calm night before the run into the Pagasetic Gulf.
thingsToDo: Anchor in Panormos and swim the sandy shallows
Take a taverna dinner at the Agnontas quay
Dinghy across to the islet off Panormos
Watch the katabatic evening breeze set in
8.mooringTip: Panormos is anchor-only over sand with gusty evening downdrafts, so dig in well and allow extra chain; if the north wind is strong, Agnontas offers a sheltered stern-to berth along its breakwater.

9.shortDescription: Cross to the mouth of the Pagasetic Gulf and Palio Trikeri, a car-free islet whose only village is a row of fish tavernas.
9.description: The character of the trip changes here as you leave the open Aegean and slip into the Pagasetic Gulf, the near-landlocked bay behind the Pelion peninsula. It is around twenty miles from Skopelos to Palio Trikeri, the only inhabited island in the gulf and a genuinely peaceful spot. There are no cars and barely a road, only a monastery, a couple of beaches and the little anchorage of Agios Ioannis where a handful of fish tavernas line the shore. Anchoring is straightforward over sand and weed, and several of the taverns will hand you a line off their jetty if you are eating in, which you probably should. The gulf is far better sheltered than the islands, so nights here are calm and warm. A good place to slow the pace before Volos.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Agios Ioannis for a taverna dinner
Walk to the monastery on Palio Trikeri
Swim off the car-free island's quiet beaches
Dinghy across to the Kottes fishing cove
9.mooringTip: Anchor over sand off Agios Ioannis, or take a line from one of the fish tavernas if you are dining ashore; the gulf is well sheltered, so holding matters more than swell protection here.

10.shortDescription: A hop across the strait to Agia Kiriaki, a working fishing port and boatyard where the tavernas hand you a mooring line.
10.description: Barely an hour under sail separates Palio Trikeri from Agia Kiriaki, so this is a lazy morning with time for a last swim before moving. Agia Kiriaki sits on the very tip of the Pelion peninsula and still works for a living: caiques hauled out for repair, nets drying on the quay, a traditional wooden-boat yard that has been going for generations. For visiting yachts the drill is simple. Moor stern-to at the pier, or pick up a line from one of the two busy fish taverns on the front, on the understanding that you will eat there, and you will not regret it. Behind the village, a 300-year-old cobbled path climbs to Trikeri proper on its hill, and the view back over the gulf from up there is worth the sweat.
thingsToDo: Moor off the fish taverns and dine on the catch
Watch caiques being built at the boatyard
Climb the old cobbled path to Trikeri village
Swim off the pier before the afternoon breeze
10.mooringTip: Space at Agia Kiriaki is tight and the free quay is tricky, so the easy option is to take a taverna's ready-laid line in front of their terrace and settle up over dinner; arrive early as the two good berths go fast.

11.shortDescription: Sail up the gulf to Volos, the lively mainland city famous for its tsipouradika, meze bars pairing small plates with tsipouro.
11.description: The gulf narrows and the mountains close in as you sail up toward Volos, roughly twenty miles at the head of the bay beneath the bulk of Mount Pelion. This is the one proper city on the route, a busy university port with a long waterfront promenade, and the marina gives you shore power, showers and a chance to deal with laundry and a big provisioning run. Volos has one great ritual worth planning your evening around: the tsipouradika. You order a small carafe of tsipouro, the local spirit, and the kitchen sends out plate after plate of meze to match it, mostly seafood, until you tell them to stop. It is cheap, sociable and very Greek. If there is time in the afternoon, the mountain villages of Makrinitsa and Portaria are a short taxi ride up into the cool of Pelion.
thingsToDo: Do a tsipouradiko crawl along the waterfront
Taxi up to Makrinitsa's balcony village
See the Bronze Age finds at the Volos museum
Stroll the seafront promenade at dusk
11.mooringTip: Volos marina has proper pontoons with power and water, the easiest berthing of the trip, but it is popular, so radio ahead or call in the morning to reserve a stern-to spot for the night.

12.shortDescription: Run back down the gulf toward Trikeri, with time to break the passage at a quiet Pelion cove for lunch and a swim.
12.description: With the city behind you, the run back down the Pagasetic is unhurried, around twenty miles with the whole gulf to yourselves. The sheltered water here makes it ideal for a long lunch stop, so pick one of the coves along the Pelion shore, drop anchor over sand in a few metres, and let the crew swim and eat before carrying on. Divari and the beaches near Milina are popular for this, calm and shallow with easy holding. By late afternoon you are back down at the Trikeri narrows, either anchoring again off Palio Trikeri or lying to a taverna line at Agios Ioannis for a second, familiar night. After the buzz of Volos it feels like coming back to somewhere you know. An early dinner ashore, then a quiet one before the crossing back to Skiathos.
thingsToDo: Anchor for a swim lunch off Milina
Explore the shallows near Divari beach
Return to Agios Ioannis for taverna dinner
Watch the sun drop behind Pelion's ridge
12.mooringTip: The Pelion coves off Milina and Divari give easy daytime anchoring over sand, but they are open to the afternoon breeze; for the night drop back to sheltered Agios Ioannis or the Trikeri anchorage.

13.shortDescription: Cross back out of the gulf to Skiathos and anchor off Koukounaries, the island's famous pine-backed strip of golden sand.
13.description: Time to leave the gulf and cross back to the Sporades proper. It is a fifteen-mile hop from the Trikeri narrows over to the southwest corner of Skiathos, and if the meltemi is running you will get a lively downwind sail to finish. The target is Koukounaries, the long curve of fine golden sand backed by an umbrella-pine forest that regularly tops lists of the best beaches in Greece. Anchor off the bay in sand in four to eight metres, well out from the swimmers, and the water is warm and shallow enough for the whole crew to spend the afternoon in it. It does get busy, so the early birds get the calmer corner. For something wilder, it is a short day-sail round to Lalaria, the cliff-lined pebble beach on the north coast reachable only from the water.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Koukounaries' Blue Flag beach
Walk the umbrella-pine forest behind the sand
Day-sail round to Lalaria's white pebbles
Sundowner at a Koukounaries beach bar
13.mooringTip: Anchor in sand off Koukounaries in four to eight metres, staying outside the swim buoys; it is exposed to the south and afternoon boat traffic, so for an overnight consider nearby Agia Eleni or a more sheltered cove.

14.shortDescription: A short final run along the coast into Skiathos Marina, with a last swim before handing the boat back at journey's end.
14.description: The last morning is a gentle one. Skiathos town lies only four or five miles back along the south coast from Koukounaries, so there is no reason to hurry off, and most crews squeeze in a final swim at Agia Eleni or Banana beach before the engine goes on. Rounding into Skiathos harbour you pass the little wooded Bourtzi peninsula that splits the old and new ports, with the town rising behind in a jumble of red roofs. Most charter bases want the boat back alongside by evening or first thing the following morning, so aim to be berthed with time to clean up, settle the paperwork and pack. If your flight is later, the town rewards a wander: the cobbled main street, the Papadiamantis house, and a farewell dinner looking out over the boats you have spent two weeks among.
thingsToDo: Last swim at Agia Eleni or Banana beach
Walk out to the wooded Bourtzi peninsula
Visit the Papadiamantis house museum
Farewell dinner on the old-port waterfront
14.mooringTip: Skiathos's old-port town quay is stern-to and gets chaotic with day boats and ferries; most bases berth in the new marina, so confirm your return slot with the charter company and aim to be alongside well before the evening rush.
